title: Accountants and auditors
old_rationale: Accountancy is a fundamentally digital occupation centered on data analysis, regulatory compliance, and reporting, all of which are highly susceptible to AI and robotic process automation. While high-level advisory roles and complex forensic audits require human judgment and relationship management, the core tasks of data organization, tax computation, and anomaly detection are increasingly being automated, significantly increasing worker productivity and restructuring the entry-level labor market.
new_rationale: Accounting and auditing are largely computer-based information work: analyzing financial records, preparing reports, checking compliance, and communicating findings. AI can automate many routine and even some analytical tasks, boosting productivity significantly, but human judgment, client trust, regulatory accountability, and licensed sign-off keep the occupation from being fully automatable.

title: Actors
old_rationale: While live theater and physical presence on set remain human-centric, the occupation is highly exposed due to AI's ability to generate digital likenesses, synthesize voices, and replace background extras or voiceover artists. The core work product—audio and video—is increasingly susceptible to generative AI, leading to significant industry restructuring and potential displacement in digital media productions.
new_rationale: Acting depends heavily on embodied human performance, physical presence, live collaboration, and audience appeal, which limits direct AI automation of the core job. However, AI can replace or reduce demand in some subfields—such as voiceover, background acting, and digital likeness/performance augmentation—so the occupation faces meaningful but not overwhelming exposure.

title: Actuaries
old_rationale: Actuarial work is fundamentally digital, involving the analysis of large datasets, statistical modeling, and financial forecasting—all areas where AI and machine learning excel. While the profession requires high-level human judgment and the ability to explain complex risks to stakeholders, the core technical tasks of data compilation and probability estimation are highly susceptible to automation and significant productivity gains through AI.
new_rationale: Actuarial work is overwhelmingly digital: analyzing data, building models, forecasting risk, pricing policies, and producing reports are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. The occupation still requires domain-specific judgment, regulatory accountability, and communication with executives and clients, which keeps exposure below the maximum, but AI is likely to significantly reshape workflows and raise productivity.

title: Administrative services and facilities managers
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of digital knowledge work and physical site management. While AI can significantly automate administrative tasks like recordkeeping, supply procurement, and energy pattern analysis, the role requires a physical presence to inspect facilities, oversee manual labor (janitors, repair workers), and manage real-world safety and security compliance.
new_rationale: This job mixes substantial computer-based planning, coordination, records management, policy review, and communication—areas where AI can boost productivity—with important onsite responsibilities like monitoring facilities, supervising maintenance staff, and handling building safety, repairs, and compliance. AI is likely to automate or streamline the administrative and analytical parts of the role, but the physical oversight, vendor coordination, and real-world judgment keep exposure below the highest tier.

title: Adult basic and secondary education and ESL teachers
old_rationale: The core work product—language instruction, literacy training, and test preparation—is fundamentally digital and information-based, making it highly susceptible to AI-driven tutoring and translation tools. While the role requires significant cultural sensitivity and interpersonal motivation, AI can now provide personalized lesson plans, real-time feedback, and adaptive learning paths that significantly increase teacher productivity or replace routine instructional tasks.
new_rationale: This job combines significant in-person teaching, motivation, and cultural sensitivity with substantial knowledge-work tasks such as lesson planning, assessment, progress monitoring, and individualized instruction. AI can increasingly handle tutoring, translation, practice exercises, and administrative prep, which could materially raise teacher productivity, but the core role still depends on human classroom management, adaptation, and relationship-building.

title: Advertising sales agents
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves digital knowledge work such as analyzing sales statistics, preparing media kits, and drafting contracts, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation. While the role requires interpersonal relationship building, the shift toward programmatic advertising and AI-driven ad placement is already reducing the need for human intermediaries in the sales process, as reflected in the projected employment decline.
new_rationale: This job mixes relationship-driven selling with a large amount of digital information work: prospecting, preparing proposals, analyzing accounts, drafting contracts, and creating sales materials are all increasingly AI-automatable. But winning clients still depends heavily on human persuasion, trust, and ongoing account management, especially in live interactions, so AI is more likely to reshape and compress the role than fully replace it.

title: Advertising, promotions, and marketing managers
old_rationale: This occupation is predominantly digital and information-based, involving market research, data analysis, and content strategy—all areas where AI is rapidly advancing. While high-level strategic decision-making and stakeholder management provide some insulation, AI can now automate significant portions of campaign planning, creative drafting, and consumer data processing, leading to major restructuring of the role.
new_rationale: This is predominantly digital knowledge work: analyzing markets, developing campaigns, choosing media, pricing strategy, and communicating with clients and staff are all areas where AI can already assist heavily and is improving fast. Human judgment, client relationships, negotiation, and team leadership still matter, but AI is likely to significantly reshape workflows and raise productivity enough to reduce demand for some managerial and supporting roles.

title: Aerospace engineering and operations technologists and technicians
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of physical labor, such as building test facilities, installing instruments, and maintaining hardware, which provides a buffer against AI automation. However, the role also includes substantial digital tasks like recording data, running computer simulations, and calibrating systems—areas where AI is rapidly improving and can significantly enhance productivity or automate data analysis.
new_rationale: This job mixes technical knowledge work with substantial hands-on physical tasks such as building test facilities, installing instruments, calibrating equipment, and supporting manufacturing and assembly. AI can meaningfully assist with simulations, data analysis, troubleshooting, and documentation, but the core work still requires on-site interaction with specialized hardware, safety procedures, and real-world testing environments.

title: Aerospace engineers
old_rationale: Aerospace engineering is a high-level knowledge occupation where core tasks like aerodynamic modeling, structural analysis, and propulsion design are increasingly performed using digital simulation and AI-driven optimization tools. While the role requires critical human judgment for safety, regulatory compliance, and physical inspection of prototypes, the heavy reliance on computer-aided design (CAD) and complex data analysis makes it highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and task automation.
new_rationale: Aerospace engineering is largely computer-based knowledge work—designing, simulating, analyzing, documenting, and evaluating systems—so AI can strongly assist and increase productivity in many core tasks. But the occupation remains constrained by safety-critical judgment, physical testing, regulatory compliance, and accountability for real-world hardware, which makes full automation much harder than for purely digital roles.

title: Agricultural and food science technicians
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of physical labor, such as collecting biological samples, maintaining farm equipment, and operating laboratory hardware, which provides a buffer against full automation. However, AI will heavily impact the data-heavy portions of the job, including compiling test results, analyzing chemical properties, and generating reports, making technicians more productive but potentially reducing the number of workers needed for data processing.
new_rationale: This job mixes physical lab, plant, farm, and field work with data analysis and reporting. AI can help interpret test results, automate documentation, and support quality-control decisions, but core tasks like collecting samples, operating equipment, maintaining sterile environments, and inspecting physical materials still require hands-on human presence.

title: Agricultural and food scientists
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, including data analysis, research synthesis, and report writing, which are highly susceptible to AI enhancement. However, the role is anchored by physical requirements such as field sampling, laboratory experiments, and on-site inspections at farms or processing plants that AI cannot currently replicate.
new_rationale: Agricultural and food scientists do substantial knowledge work that AI can increasingly assist with, including data analysis, literature review, report writing, communication, and parts of experiment design. But the occupation also depends heavily on laboratory work, field sampling, facility visits, regulatory oversight, and scientific judgment tied to physical biological systems, which limits full automation.

title: Agricultural engineers
old_rationale: Agricultural engineering is a hybrid role that combines digital design and data analysis with physical site visits and hardware testing. While AI will significantly automate core tasks like CAD modeling, environmental simulation, and precision farming data analysis, the requirement for physical oversight of construction, equipment testing in the field, and real-world troubleshooting provides a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Agricultural engineers do substantial computer-based design, analysis, modeling, and documentation work, so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in parts of the job. But the occupation also depends on field visits, equipment testing, site-specific engineering judgment, and coordination around physical agricultural systems, which limits full automation.

title: Agricultural workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, animal handling, and operating machinery in unpredictable outdoor environments, which provides a strong natural barrier to AI. While AI and robotics are increasingly used for precision agriculture and automated harvesting, the high cost of hardware and the need for manual dexterity in varied conditions mean human workers remain essential for the majority of tasks.
new_rationale: Agricultural workers’ core tasks are physical, outdoor, and hands-on: planting, harvesting, feeding animals, cleaning enclosures, and operating machinery in real environments. AI may indirectly reduce labor demand through smarter equipment, robotics, and monitoring systems, but it does not directly automate most day-to-day work from a computer.

title: Air traffic controllers
old_rationale: The core work is fundamentally digital and data-driven, involving the monitoring of radar screens and the processing of complex spatial information to ensure aircraft separation. While the high-stakes nature of safety and the need for real-time human communication with pilots provide a buffer, AI and automated systems (like NextGen) are already increasing productivity and handling more traffic per controller, leading to a very low projected growth in human employment.
new_rationale: Air traffic control involves substantial information processing on screens, and AI can assist with routing, conflict detection, weather integration, and decision support—potentially letting each controller manage more traffic. But the core job is real-time, safety-critical control of aircraft in the physical world under heavy regulation, where human judgment, accountability, and rapid coordination remain major barriers to full automation.

title: Aircraft and avionics equipment mechanics and technicians
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, manual dexterity, and real-time troubleshooting in a physical environment, which provides a strong barrier against AI automation. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic capabilities, predictive maintenance, and the interpretation of flight data, the actual repair, assembly, and FAA-regulated physical inspections must still be performed by human technicians.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is highly physical, hands-on, and safety-critical: inspecting, testing, repairing, and installing aircraft components in hangars and on airfields. AI can help with diagnostics, interpreting manuals and flight-test data, and paperwork, but it does not remove the need for certified technicians to perform and verify repairs in the physical world.

title: Airline and commercial pilots
old_rationale: The occupation is a hybrid of high-level digital information processing and critical physical presence. While AI and advanced automation can handle navigation, flight planning, and system monitoring, the role requires real-time human intervention for physical emergencies, complex sensory awareness in unpredictable weather, and the legal/ethical responsibility of passenger safety in the physical world.
new_rationale: Piloting involves significant physical presence, real-time control, safety-critical judgment, and coordination in unpredictable environments, which creates a strong barrier to full AI replacement. However, aviation is already highly automated, and AI can further reshape flight planning, monitoring, training, and some cockpit decision support, so the occupation faces meaningful but not near-term complete automation.

title: Animal care and service workers
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are physical and require real-time presence, such as feeding, grooming, cleaning, and physically training animals. AI may assist with peripheral administrative tasks like scheduling or monitoring animal health data, but it cannot replace the manual labor or the empathetic human-animal interaction required for the job.
new_rationale: This work is overwhelmingly physical and in-person: feeding, cleaning, grooming, exercising, handling, and training animals all require direct human presence in unpredictable real-world settings. AI may help with peripheral tasks like scheduling, recordkeeping, or monitoring animal behavior, but it is unlikely to automate the core hands-on care and service work.

title: Announcers and DJs
old_rationale: The occupation is split between highly exposed digital work and less exposed physical work. Broadcast and radio roles are under significant threat from AI-generated voices and automated content curation, whereas live event DJs and emcees retain a physical and interpersonal barrier that is harder to automate.
new_rationale: This occupation is split between highly exposed broadcast/podcast work and lower-exposure live event DJ/emcee work. Broadcast announcing is largely digital—researching, writing, recording, editing, and even presenting scripted content—and BLS explicitly notes stations may replace live DJs with AI, but live audience interaction, on-air personality, interviewing, and in-person event hosting still preserve meaningful human value.

title: Anthropologists and archeologists
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical fieldwork and digital knowledge work. While AI can significantly automate data analysis, pattern recognition in artifacts, and report writing, it cannot replace the physical requirements of excavation, site surveying, and in-person ethnographic observation.
new_rationale: This job mixes significant knowledge work—analyzing findings, managing records, writing reports, and advising organizations—with substantial fieldwork, interviews, excavation, and on-site assessment that require physical presence and human judgment. AI will likely boost productivity in research, data analysis, and documentation, but it is less able to replace the core observational, interpretive, and field-based parts of the occupation.

title: Arbitrators, mediators, and conciliators
old_rationale: This occupation is primarily digital and information-based, involving the analysis of legal documents, evidence, and the drafting of settlement agreements—tasks where AI is rapidly achieving high proficiency. While the role requires significant interpersonal skills and emotional intelligence to manage confrontational parties, the core analytical and administrative functions are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and partial automation.
new_rationale: This job mixes substantial knowledge work—reviewing documents, applying laws or precedents, drafting agreements, and managing case information—with a core function of live human facilitation in emotionally charged disputes. AI can significantly assist with research, summarization, document preparation, and procedural tasks, but the occupation still depends heavily on interpersonal trust, neutrality, and real-time judgment between conflicting parties.

title: Architects
old_rationale: Architects perform high-level knowledge work that is increasingly digital, utilizing CADD and BIM software which is highly susceptible to AI-driven automation in generative design and technical documentation. While the role requires physical site visits and complex client negotiations, a significant portion of the core work—drafting, specification writing, and regulatory compliance—is moving toward high AI integration.
new_rationale: Architecture has a large digital core: creating plans in CADD/BIM, producing drawings and specifications, analyzing constraints, and communicating designs are all areas where AI can substantially boost productivity. But the occupation also depends on client relationships, professional judgment, legal responsibility, and physical site visits to verify construction, which limits full automation.

title: Architectural and engineering managers
old_rationale: This occupation is predominantly knowledge-based and digital, involving project planning, budgeting, and technical oversight that can be significantly enhanced by AI tools. While the role requires high-level human judgment, leadership, and physical site visits, the core tasks of analyzing technical data, scheduling, and coordinating complex engineering workflows are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and automation.
new_rationale: This is mostly knowledge and coordination work—planning projects, budgeting, scheduling, supervising technical staff, and communicating across teams—so AI can significantly assist with analysis, documentation, forecasting, and management workflows. But the role also depends heavily on human leadership, accountability, cross-functional coordination, and oversight of real-world engineering, construction, manufacturing, or R&D activities, which limits full automation.

title: Archivists, curators, and museum workers
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of digital knowledge work and physical preservation. AI will significantly automate archival tasks like cataloging, metadata generation, and electronic record management, while also assisting curators with research and exhibit design. However, the physical requirements of handling artifacts, restoring delicate items, and managing in-person public interactions provide a substantial buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: This occupation mixes significant knowledge work—cataloging, research, documentation, database management, exhibit planning, and public communications—with substantial physical, hands-on work preserving objects, installing exhibits, and interacting with visitors. AI can strongly assist with archival organization, metadata creation, research, writing, and records management, but the physical stewardship, authentication, conservation, and in-person museum functions limit full automation.

title: Art directors
old_rationale: Art directors work in a fundamentally digital domain where AI is rapidly advancing in image generation, layout design, and video synthesis. While the role requires high-level human judgment, leadership, and client communication, the core work product can be heavily augmented or automated by AI, significantly increasing productivity and potentially reducing the number of directors needed to oversee creative projects.
new_rationale: Art direction is largely digital creative work: developing visual concepts, reviewing designs, selecting imagery, and communicating with clients and design teams, all in domains where generative AI is advancing quickly. AI can already accelerate concept generation, mood boards, layout exploration, and image production, though human judgment, taste, client management, and leadership still remain important.

title: Assemblers and fabricators
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical manipulation, manual dexterity, and real-time adjustments in a physical environment, which provides a significant buffer against AI. While AI and robotics are increasing efficiency and automating some repetitive tasks, a substantial portion of the work—especially in complex assembly, prototyping, and custom fabrication—still requires human presence and tactile skill.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical, hands-on assembly in manufacturing plants using tools, machines, and direct manipulation of parts, which limits direct automation by general-purpose AI. However, it has meaningful indirect exposure because AI-enabled robotics, machine vision, and process automation can increasingly handle repetitive assembly and quality-check tasks, reducing labor demand in some settings.

title: Athletes and sports competitors
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is physical performance, manual dexterity, and real-time human presence in the physical world, which AI cannot replicate. While AI is used as a peripheral tool for performance analytics, strategy optimization, and diet planning, it does not replace the athlete's physical labor or the entertainment value of human competition.
new_rationale: The core work of athletes is intensely physical, in-person performance that depends on bodily skill, training, and real-time competition, which AI cannot replace directly. AI may help with peripheral tasks like video analysis, strategy, scouting opponents, and training optimization, but it does not substitute for the athlete’s on-field or on-court role.

title: Athletic trainers
old_rationale: The core duties of athletic trainers—such as applying tape/braces, providing emergency first aid on the field, and performing manual rehabilitation—require physical presence and tactile skills that AI cannot replicate. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like injury analysis, record-keeping, and designing recovery protocols, the job remains fundamentally grounded in real-time human interaction and physical healthcare delivery.
new_rationale: Athletic trainers’ core work is hands-on and in-person: evaluating injuries, providing emergency care, applying supports, and guiding rehabilitation in real physical settings. AI may help with documentation, recordkeeping, treatment planning support, and compliance paperwork, but it is unlikely to replace the direct physical care, on-site judgment, and interpersonal coordination central to the job.

title: Atmospheric scientists, including meteorologists
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves analyzing digital data, running computer models, and writing code, all of which are highly susceptible to AI advancement. Machine learning is already revolutionizing weather forecasting by outperforming traditional numerical models in speed and efficiency, potentially reducing the number of human forecasters needed for routine data processing and report generation.
new_rationale: This occupation is heavily exposed because much of the core work is digital: analyzing large datasets, running models, writing code, generating forecasts, graphics, and reports. AI can significantly automate and accelerate these tasks, though human judgment, scientific interpretation, and responsibility for high-stakes warnings and public communication keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Audiologists
old_rationale: Audiology is a clinical healthcare profession that requires physical presence for examinations, wax removal, and fitting hardware like hearing aids or cochlear implants. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic accuracy, signal processing in hearing devices, and automated testing, the core of the job involves interpersonal counseling, physical patient interaction, and complex clinical judgment that cannot be fully digitized.
new_rationale: Audiologists combine diagnostic analysis with substantial in-person clinical work: examining patients, administering hearing and balance tests, fitting devices, and counseling patients and families. AI can meaningfully assist with interpreting test data, documentation, and treatment planning, but the occupation’s core hands-on care, patient interaction, and licensed clinical judgment limit full automation.

title: Automotive body and glass repairers
old_rationale: The core tasks are highly physical, involving manual dexterity, welding, and the use of pneumatic tools in unpredictable repair environments that are difficult to automate. AI's primary impact is limited to peripheral administrative tasks like cost estimation and damage assessment via computer vision, but the physical restoration of vehicles remains a human-centric trade.
new_rationale: This job is centered on physical, hands-on repair work: removing and fitting parts, welding, aligning frames, installing glass, and working directly on vehicles in shops or in the field. AI may help with peripheral tasks like damage estimating, repair documentation, or technical lookup, but it does not meaningfully automate the core manual work requiring dexterity, physical presence, and tool use.

title: Automotive service technicians and mechanics
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, manual dexterity, and real-time mechanical repair in a physical environment, which provides a strong barrier against AI automation. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic capabilities and predictive maintenance software, the actual physical replacement of parts and hands-on troubleshooting remains a human-centric task.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical: inspecting vehicles, using tools, replacing parts, and working in a repair shop on real machines. AI can assist with diagnostics, repair documentation, and troubleshooting support, but it cannot easily replace the hands-on mechanical work, physical dexterity, and in-person customer interaction that define the occupation.

title: Bakers
old_rationale: The core duties of a baker are physical and sensory, involving manual dexterity for shaping dough and real-time monitoring of physical products in high-heat environments. While AI can optimize recipes or assist with inventory management and scheduling, the fundamental work of kneading, decorating, and handling equipment remains a physical task resistant to digital automation.
new_rationale: Bakers’ core work is physical and hands-on: measuring ingredients, shaping dough, operating ovens, monitoring batches, cleaning equipment, and often serving customers in person. AI may help at the margins with inventory, scheduling, demand forecasting, or recipe standardization, but it does not substantially automate the central manual, on-site tasks of baking.

title: Barbers, hairstylists, and cosmetologists
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves manual dexterity and physical interaction in a highly unpredictable environment (human hair and skin). While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like appointment scheduling, marketing, or visualizing hairstyles for clients, the physical act of cutting, coloring, and styling hair remains a significant barrier to automation.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is highly physical and in-person: cutting, coloring, styling, shaving, and interacting directly with clients. AI may help with peripheral tasks like appointment scheduling, marketing, recordkeeping, or hairstyle visualization, but it does not meaningfully automate the hands-on service itself.

title: Bartenders
old_rationale: The core of bartending involves physical manipulation of objects, manual cleaning, and real-time social interaction in a physical environment. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like inventory management or automated ordering, the primary value of the role lies in human presence, physical service, and the social 'hospitality' aspect which remains difficult to automate.
new_rationale: Bartending is centered on physical, in-person service: mixing and serving drinks, checking IDs, monitoring intoxication, handling payments, cleaning, and managing a live social environment. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like inventory, scheduling, or POS systems, but it does not substantially automate the core hands-on and interpersonal work.

title: Bill and account collectors
old_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital, consisting of data analysis, skip tracing, and communication via phone or computer. AI and automated systems are already driving a projected 10% decline in employment by handling routine notifications and payment processing, while advanced LLMs are increasingly capable of managing the negotiation and customer service aspects of debt collection.
new_rationale: This job is largely computer-and-phone-based, with routine digital tasks such as locating debtors, reviewing account information, making scripted outreach, documenting interactions, and negotiating repayment plans. AI and automation can already handle or heavily assist much of this workflow—especially calling, recordkeeping, compliance scripting, and prioritization—though difficult negotiations and handling emotionally charged conversations still leave some role for humans.

title: Biochemists and biophysicists
old_rationale: This occupation is heavily centered on knowledge work, data analysis, and complex modeling, all of which are being revolutionized by AI (e.g., AlphaFold for protein structure prediction). While physical laboratory work and team management provide a buffer, a significant portion of the role involves literature review, grant writing, and data interpretation—tasks where AI can exponentially increase productivity or automate core analytical functions.
new_rationale: AI can meaningfully accelerate major parts of this job, including literature review, data analysis, protein modeling, report writing, and grant drafting. But the occupation still depends heavily on physical laboratory experiments, interdisciplinary teamwork, and high-level scientific judgment in novel research, which limits full automation.

title: Bioengineers and biomedical engineers
old_rationale: This occupation involves heavy digital knowledge work, including software design, statistical modeling, and technical writing, all of which are highly susceptible to AI augmentation and automation. While physical tasks like equipment maintenance and clinical collaboration provide a buffer, the core engineering and research functions are increasingly driven by AI-enhanced simulations and data analysis.
new_rationale: Bioengineers and biomedical engineers do substantial computer-based work—modeling, simulation, software, analysis, documentation, and research—where AI can materially boost productivity and automate parts of design and reporting. But the occupation also depends heavily on physical device development, experimentation, regulatory/safety validation, and collaboration with clinicians, manufacturers, and labs, which limits full automation. AI is likely to significantly reshape the job, but not replace the hands-on engineering and real-world testing core.

title: Biological technicians
old_rationale: The role is a hybrid of physical laboratory work and digital data analysis. While AI can significantly automate data interpretation, report writing, and experimental modeling, the core duties involve manual tasks like setting up equipment, preparing biological samples, and conducting physical experiments that require a human presence in a lab or field environment.
new_rationale: Biological technicians have meaningful AI exposure in the digital parts of the job—data analysis, recordkeeping, report writing, and some interpretation of results. But much of the core work still requires physical lab presence, sample handling, equipment setup, experiment execution, and field collection, which limits full automation.

title: Boilermakers
old_rationale: The core work is highly physical, involving manual assembly, welding, and repair in unpredictable and cramped environments like boilers and tanks. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like blueprint analysis or maintenance scheduling, the primary duties require human dexterity and physical presence that current AI and robotics cannot replicate in non-factory settings.
new_rationale: Boilermaking is overwhelmingly physical, on-site work involving welding, fitting, inspecting, and repairing large equipment in cramped, hazardous, and unpredictable environments. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like blueprint interpretation, diagnostics, or planning, but it does not meaningfully automate the core hands-on trade work.

title: Bookkeeping, accounting, and auditing clerks
old_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital and involves routine information processing, data entry, and mathematical verification—tasks that AI and automated software already perform with high accuracy. The BLS already projects a decline in employment due to technological change, and the shift toward 'analytical and advisory' roles is increasingly being encroached upon by advanced LLMs and specialized financial AI agents.
new_rationale: This job is almost entirely digital, centered on entering, classifying, reconciling, and checking financial data in software, spreadsheets, and databases—exactly the kind of routine information processing AI and automation can increasingly perform. BLS already notes software is reducing demand and shifting remaining workers toward more analytical and advisory tasks, indicating major restructuring even if some human oversight and client communication remain.

title: Broadcast, sound, and video technicians
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical labor (setting up hardware, rigging lights, running cables) and digital knowledge work (mixing sound, editing video, managing broadcast signals). While the physical installation tasks are resistant to AI, the digital components—such as sound leveling, video editing, and signal monitoring—are being rapidly automated by AI tools, leading to higher productivity and a projected decline in specialized roles like sound and broadcast technicians.
new_rationale: This job mixes substantial hands-on physical work—setting up, operating, repairing, and dismantling equipment on site—with digital tasks such as recording, editing, mixing, monitoring, and format conversion. AI can increasingly automate parts of the digital production and control workflow and raise each technician’s productivity, but live-event support, installation, troubleshooting, and physical equipment handling keep exposure below fully digital occupations.

title: Budget analysts
old_rationale: Budget analysts perform work that is almost entirely digital, involving data analysis, financial modeling, and report writing—all areas where AI is highly capable. While the role requires human negotiation and communication with stakeholders, the core tasks of consolidating budgets, identifying discrepancies, and performing cost-benefit analyses are highly susceptible to automation and significant productivity gains through AI.
new_rationale: Budget analysts do predominantly digital knowledge work: reviewing proposals, analyzing financial data, preparing reports, forecasting needs, and writing recommendations. AI can automate or heavily assist much of this analysis, summarization, compliance checking, and report drafting, though human judgment, organizational context, and explaining recommendations to managers or legislators keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Bus drivers
old_rationale: While autonomous driving technology is a direct long-term threat to the core task of vehicle operation, the role involves significant physical and interpersonal responsibilities that AI cannot yet replicate. School bus drivers must manage student behavior and safety, while transit and charter drivers assist passengers with disabilities and handle physical luggage, creating a substantial barrier to full automation in the near term.
new_rationale: Bus driving is centered on physical, real-world vehicle operation, passenger safety, and in-person assistance, which limits near-term AI automation compared with fully digital jobs. However, AI and autonomy could meaningfully reshape the occupation over time—especially on fixed routes through driver-assistance systems, scheduling, dispatch, fare handling, and eventually some forms of autonomous transit—so exposure is moderate rather than low.

title: Butchers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves manual dexterity, physical strength, and real-time interaction with physical products in a cold, unpredictable environment. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like inventory tracking and sales forecasting, the primary duties of cutting, deboning, and handling meat are resistant to digital automation and require specialized robotics rather than AI software.
new_rationale: Butchers’ core work is highly physical and hands-on: cutting, deboning, packaging meat, handling tools, maintaining sanitation, and serving customers in person. AI may help only at the margins with inventory tracking, ordering, and sales analysis, but it does little to automate the central manual and on-site tasks.

title: Calibration technologists and technicians
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant physical component, requiring manual dexterity to adjust hardware and presence in industrial or laboratory environments. While AI can automate data recording, scheduling, and the analysis of calibration deviations, the core tasks of physical inspection, maintenance, and hands-on equipment manipulation provide a natural barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is hands-on: inspecting, adjusting, testing, and maintaining physical measurement devices in labs, plants, and other on-site settings. AI may help with documentation, scheduling, diagnostics, and analyzing test data, but it does not remove the need for physical manipulation, precision adjustment, and real-world verification against standards.

title: Cardiovascular technologists and technicians
old_rationale: The role requires significant physical presence for tasks like positioning patients, attaching electrodes, and assisting in invasive surgical procedures. While AI will heavily automate the analysis of EKG and pulmonary data, the core requirements of patient care, physical stamina, and real-time technical assistance in a clinical environment provide a strong barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is hands-on and patient-facing: preparing and positioning patients, operating diagnostic equipment, assisting in procedures, and monitoring patients in real time in clinical settings. AI can help with interpreting test data, flagging abnormalities, and documentation, but it is unlikely to replace the physical presence, technical operation, and direct teamwork with physicians that define the occupation.

title: Career and technical education teachers
old_rationale: While AI can significantly automate lesson planning, grading, and the delivery of theoretical knowledge, the core of this occupation involves supervising hands-on activities in physical environments like workshops and labs. Teaching students to safely use physical tools for trades like welding, culinary arts, or auto repair requires real-time human presence and physical intervention that AI cannot currently replicate.
new_rationale: AI can meaningfully assist with lesson planning, creating instructional materials, grading, and some tutoring or content delivery. But career and technical education teachers still rely heavily on in-person instruction, hands-on demonstrations, classroom management, and supervision of tools, equipment, and safety procedures, which limits full automation.

title: Carpenters
old_rationale: Carpentry is a highly physical occupation requiring manual dexterity, strength, and real-time problem-solving in unpredictable physical environments. While AI may assist with peripheral tasks like blueprint analysis, material estimation, or business management, the core work of cutting, fitting, and installing materials remains resistant to automation.
new_rationale: Carpentry is overwhelmingly physical, hands-on work performed on construction sites using tools, materials, and real-time adaptation to specific conditions. AI may help with peripheral tasks like estimating, blueprint interpretation, scheduling, or calculations, but it does not substantially automate the core manual installation, repair, and construction work.

title: Cartographers and photogrammetrists
old_rationale: The core tasks of collecting, analyzing, and visualizing geospatial data are fundamentally digital and highly susceptible to AI-driven automation. Computer vision and machine learning are already significantly faster than humans at interpreting satellite imagery, LiDAR, and survey data, while generative AI can increasingly automate the design and production of both static and interactive maps.
new_rationale: This occupation is largely digital knowledge work: analyzing geospatial data, integrating GIS/CAD inputs, and producing maps and visualizations on a computer. AI is rapidly improving at image interpretation, feature extraction, data fusion, and automated map generation, though human judgment, quality control, and occasional field verification still limit full automation.

title: Cashiers
old_rationale: While the job has physical components like bagging and stocking, the core function of processing transactions is highly susceptible to automation through computer vision and AI-driven self-checkout systems. The BLS already projects a significant decline in employment due to these technological shifts, as AI can increasingly handle age verification, fraud detection, and customer inquiries.
new_rationale: Cashiers have significant AI exposure because much of the core job is routine transaction processing that can be automated through self-checkout, computer vision, digital payments, and retail automation systems. The role still requires physical presence and customer interaction, which prevents maximum exposure, but AI and adjacent automation are likely to keep reducing headcount and reshaping the job substantially.

title: Chefs and head cooks
old_rationale: The core of the job is highly physical and sensory, requiring manual dexterity, real-time human leadership in a fast-paced kitchen, and the use of taste and smell. While AI can significantly assist with peripheral digital tasks like menu planning, inventory management, and recipe generation, it cannot replace the physical oversight and hands-on food preparation essential to the role.
new_rationale: Chefs and head cooks do highly physical, on-site work centered on cooking, tasting, supervising kitchens, and managing real-time food service operations, which limits direct automation by AI. AI can assist with peripheral tasks like menu drafting, inventory, scheduling, costing, and marketing, but it does not replace the core hands-on culinary and leadership work.

title: Chemical engineers
old_rationale: Chemical engineering involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, including process simulation, CAD design, and data analysis, which are highly susceptible to AI-driven optimization. However, the role is anchored by the need for physical presence in laboratories and industrial plants to monitor equipment, ensure safety compliance, and troubleshoot hardware in the real world.
new_rationale: Chemical engineers do substantial knowledge work on computers—process design, analysis, simulation, research, compliance, and documentation—so AI can meaningfully assist and raise productivity. But the occupation also depends on plant operations, lab testing, safety oversight, regulatory judgment, and troubleshooting in physical manufacturing environments, which limits full automation.

title: Chemical technicians
old_rationale: The role is a hybrid of physical laboratory work and digital data analysis. While AI can significantly automate the compilation of results, report generation, and predictive modeling of chemical properties, the core duties of setting up equipment, preparing physical solutions, and maintaining laboratory instruments require a human presence in a physical environment.
new_rationale: Chemical technicians do substantial hands-on physical work in labs and manufacturing settings: setting up instruments, preparing solutions, monitoring processes, maintaining equipment, and running experiments all require onsite presence. AI can meaningfully assist with data analysis, interpreting results, documentation, reporting, and troubleshooting support, but it does not eliminate the core lab-based and process-monitoring duties.

title: Chemists and materials scientists
old_rationale: This occupation is a high-level blend of digital knowledge work and physical laboratory experimentation. AI is already revolutionizing the digital aspects—such as molecular modeling, predictive simulation, and technical reporting—allowing scientists to screen millions of compounds in silico before ever entering a lab. While the physical requirement of conducting experiments and handling chemicals provides a buffer, the massive productivity gains in research and analysis mean AI will fundamentally restructure how these scientists spend their time.
new_rationale: AI is likely to significantly reshape the analytical and digital parts of this job—literature review, modeling, simulation, experimental design support, data analysis, and technical writing. But much of the occupation still depends on physical laboratory work, hands-on experimentation, safety procedures, and real-world validation, which limits full automation.

title: Childcare workers
old_rationale: The core responsibilities of this role—physical care, safety monitoring, hygiene, and emotional support—require a physical presence and human empathy that AI cannot replicate. While AI may assist with peripheral tasks like lesson planning, record-keeping, or parent communication, the fundamental nature of the work is hands-on and occurs in the physical world.
new_rationale: Childcare work is centered on physical presence, hands-on care, safety monitoring, and real-time interaction with children in unpredictable environments, which creates a strong barrier to AI automation. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like recordkeeping, scheduling, activity planning, or parent communication, but it does not meaningfully replace the core work.

title: Chiropractors
old_rationale: The core of chiropractic work is highly physical, requiring manual dexterity for spinal adjustments and real-time physical assessment of a patient's musculoskeletal system. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like medical imaging analysis, diagnostic suggestions, and administrative business management, it cannot replace the essential hands-on therapeutic interventions that define the profession.
new_rationale: Chiropractors’ core work is hands-on and in-person: examining patients, performing spinal and joint adjustments, and building trust with people in pain. AI may assist with documentation, scheduling, imaging interpretation, and practice management, but it is unlikely to replace the manual treatment and physical patient interaction that define the occupation.

title: Civil engineering technologists and technicians
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of digital knowledge work and physical field work. While AI and advanced CAD software are significantly automating drafting, cost estimation, and data analysis tasks, the role still requires physical site visits, material testing, and real-world inspections that AI cannot currently perform.
new_rationale: This job mixes digital design, drafting, cost estimating, data analysis, and reporting—tasks where AI can significantly boost productivity—with field surveys, site inspections, materials testing, and construction observation that still require physical presence. AI is likely to automate or accelerate the office-based support work, but the occupation is not fully digital because core responsibilities remain tied to real-world infrastructure projects.

title: Civil engineers
old_rationale: Civil engineering involves significant digital knowledge work, including CAD design, data analysis, and cost estimation, which are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains. However, the role is anchored by the necessity for physical site inspections, real-world surveying, and the legal accountability of a Professional Engineer (PE) license, which requires human judgment and liability that AI cannot currently replace.
new_rationale: Civil engineers do substantial computer-based work—analyzing data, producing designs in CAD, estimating costs, preparing permits, and reviewing compliance—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in core parts of the job. But the occupation also depends heavily on site conditions, construction oversight, coordination with stakeholders, and licensed human judgment tied to safety and legal responsibility, which limits full automation.

title: Claims adjusters, appraisers, examiners, and investigators
old_rationale: This occupation is heavily focused on information processing, document review, and data-driven decision-making, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation. While physical inspections and interpersonal negotiations provide some protection, the BLS already projects a decline in employment due to AI's ability to analyze photos of damage and automate claim calculations.
new_rationale: This occupation combines substantial information-processing work—reviewing documents, evaluating coverage, estimating losses, detecting fraud, and authorizing payments—with some field inspection, interviewing, and negotiation. AI is already well suited to photo-based damage assessment, claims triage, document review, and anomaly detection, and BLS explicitly expects automation and AI to raise productivity and reduce employment, though physical inspections and contested cases still require human judgment.

title: Clinical laboratory technologists and technicians
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical laboratory work and digital data analysis. While AI and computer vision are rapidly automating the interpretation of slides and test results (high exposure), the physical requirements of preparing specimens, maintaining complex hardware, and interacting with patients provide a significant barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: This job mixes significant hands-on physical lab work—handling specimens, operating and maintaining equipment, following safety protocols, and sometimes interacting with patients—with information-processing tasks like analyzing results and documenting them. AI and automation can increasingly assist with image analysis, pattern detection, quality control, and routine testing workflows, but the need for physical specimen handling, regulated procedures, and on-site laboratory presence limits full automation.

title: Coaches and scouts
old_rationale: While AI is significantly impacting the analytical side of the job—such as processing player data, optimizing game strategy, and analyzing opponent film—the core of the occupation remains deeply physical and interpersonal. Coaches must provide real-time motivation, physical instruction, and human leadership on the field, which cannot be replicated by digital tools.
new_rationale: AI can assist coaches and scouts with video analysis, performance data, opponent scouting, recruiting research, and training-plan support. But the core of the job still depends heavily on in-person instruction, live game decisions, athlete motivation, relationship-building, and physical presence at practices and competitions, which limits overall exposure.

title: Community health workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is built on trust, cultural sensitivity, and physical presence within specific communities, which AI cannot replicate. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like data collection, report writing, and resource matching, the primary duties involve home visits, hands-on advocacy, and real-time interpersonal coaching for vulnerable populations.
new_rationale: Community health workers spend much of their time in the field doing outreach, home visits, coaching, advocacy, and culturally sensitive interpersonal support—core tasks that require human trust and physical presence. AI can help with documentation, translation, resource matching, and case-management logistics, but it is unlikely to replace the relationship-based, community-embedded nature of the work.

title: Compensation and benefits managers
old_rationale: This occupation is primarily digital and data-driven, involving the analysis of wage trends, benefits structures, and regulatory compliance—tasks where AI excels. While the role requires high-level human judgment for vendor negotiations and strategic decision-making, AI can automate significant portions of the data analysis, reporting, and routine employee communication, leading to higher productivity and potential workforce consolidation.
new_rationale: This is predominantly computer-based knowledge work: analyzing compensation data, monitoring regulations, preparing budgets, evaluating vendors, and communicating recommendations are all tasks AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. Exposure is tempered by the job’s managerial, strategic, and compliance-heavy nature, which still requires human judgment, cross-functional coordination, and accountability for sensitive pay and benefits decisions.

title: Compensation, benefits, and job analysis specialists
old_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally digital and data-driven, involving core tasks like market research, cost analysis, and report generation that align perfectly with AI's strengths. AI can automate the drafting of job descriptions, perform complex compensation benchmarking, and monitor regulatory changes, significantly increasing individual productivity and reducing the need for manual data processing.
new_rationale: This occupation is predominantly computer-based knowledge work: researching policies, analyzing compensation data, evaluating job descriptions, preparing reports, and monitoring regulations. AI can substantially automate and accelerate these digital tasks, though human judgment, legal accountability, and stakeholder communication still matter enough to keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Compliance officers
old_rationale: Compliance work is primarily digital and involves analyzing vast amounts of regulatory text, auditing data, and generating reports—all areas where LLMs and automated systems excel. While high-level judgment and interpersonal investigations provide some insulation, the core tasks of monitoring, risk assessment, and documentation are highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and productivity gains.
new_rationale: Compliance officers do predominantly computer-based knowledge work: reading regulations, analyzing risks, auditing records, writing policies and reports, and answering compliance questions. AI is well suited to summarizing rules, monitoring documents, drafting guidance, and flagging issues, though human judgment, accountability, investigations, and stakeholder interaction keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Computer and information research scientists
old_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally digital, involving high-level coding, algorithm design, and data analysis—all areas where AI is rapidly advancing. While these scientists are the ones building AI, the tools they create are increasingly capable of automating their own core tasks, such as writing code, simplifying algorithms, and analyzing experimental results, leading to extreme productivity gains and role restructuring.
new_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: researching, coding, experimenting, analyzing results, and writing papers can largely be done on a computer, so AI is positioned to affect nearly every core task. Although frontier research, novel problem formulation, and interdisciplinary judgment still require substantial human expertise, AI is likely to sharply increase productivity and restructure how this work is done.

title: Computer and information systems managers
old_rationale: This occupation is predominantly digital and knowledge-based, involving strategic planning, analysis, and technical oversight that AI can significantly augment. While high-level leadership, vendor negotiation, and personnel management provide a buffer of human judgment, the core tasks of assessing IT needs, monitoring security, and directing technical workflows are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and automation.
new_rationale: This is highly digital knowledge work: analyzing IT needs, evaluating projects, coordinating technical teams, and communicating plans are all areas where AI can significantly augment productivity. But the role also depends heavily on leadership, organizational judgment, vendor negotiation, and accountability for business-critical decisions, which makes full automation less likely than for purely individual contributor digital jobs.

title: Computer hardware engineers
old_rationale: The core work of designing schematics, analyzing circuits, and testing components is increasingly performed using digital Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools that are highly susceptible to AI-driven optimization and automation. While there is a physical component to testing and manufacturing oversight, the vast majority of the engineering lifecycle is digital and benefits significantly from AI's ability to optimize complex systems and generate code/hardware descriptions.
new_rationale: Computer hardware engineering involves substantial digital knowledge work—designing schematics, analyzing test results, simulating circuits, and coordinating with software—so AI can significantly accelerate core parts of the job. But the occupation is still constrained by physical-world requirements like hardware testing, manufacturing oversight, and real-world performance validation, which limit full automation.

title: Computer network architects
old_rationale: The core work of designing, documenting, and configuring networks is fundamentally digital and data-driven, making it highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and optimization tools. While physical hardware deployment and complex stakeholder management provide a slight buffer, AI is rapidly advancing in automated network topology design, security configuration, and predictive troubleshooting, which will significantly increase individual productivity and restructure the role.
new_rationale: This is predominantly digital knowledge work: designing network layouts, analyzing performance, documenting systems, recommending technologies, and troubleshooting are all tasks AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. Exposure is somewhat tempered by the need for high-stakes judgment, security considerations, coordination with teams and vendors, and occasional hands-on deployment with hardware.

title: Computer programmers
old_rationale: The core tasks of writing, testing, and debugging code are entirely digital and align perfectly with the strengths of Large Language Models, which are already highly proficient in these domains. The BLS specifically projects a decline in this occupation due to automation and AI, as these tools allow fewer workers to produce the same volume of code and shift higher-level logic to software developers.
new_rationale: Computer programming is almost entirely digital work done on a computer: writing, modifying, testing, and debugging code are all tasks where AI is advancing rapidly. AI can already automate substantial portions of routine coding and error-fixing, and the BLS description itself notes automation and AI reducing demand, though human oversight, system understanding, and collaboration keep exposure just below the absolute maximum.

title: Computer support specialists
old_rationale: The core tasks of diagnosing technical issues, documenting problems, and guiding users through digital workflows are highly susceptible to AI automation via advanced chatbots and LLM-driven troubleshooting. While some physical hardware setup and complex onsite networking remain, the vast majority of the work is digital information processing that AI can increasingly handle, as evidenced by the BLS's projected decline in employment due to automation.
new_rationale: Computer support specialists do predominantly digital work—diagnosing issues, guiding users, documenting problems, and troubleshooting systems over phone, chat, email, or remote tools—so AI can automate or heavily assist much of the core workflow. Some exposure is limited by the need for hands-on device setup, onsite support, and complex real-time customer interaction, but the occupation is already being reshaped by chatbots and automated troubleshooting.

title: Computer systems analysts
old_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally digital, involving data modeling, system design, and technical analysis that can be performed entirely on a computer. AI is highly capable of automating core tasks such as writing technical documentation, generating system diagrams, analyzing cost-benefit data, and assisting with the programming and debugging aspects of the role.
new_rationale: This is highly digital knowledge work: analyzing systems, researching technologies, modeling processes, writing documentation, and sometimes coding are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. Exposure is tempered by the need for business context, stakeholder consultation, cross-team coordination, and judgment about organizational tradeoffs, which still leave an important human role.

title: Concierges
old_rationale: The role is a hybrid of digital information processing and physical hospitality. While AI can automate core tasks like making reservations, providing local recommendations, and answering emails, the job still requires a physical presence for greeting guests, handling packages, and providing high-touch interpersonal service that is central to the luxury and residential experience.
new_rationale: Concierge work has meaningful AI exposure because many core tasks—answering routine questions, making reservations, handling messages, and coordinating services—can be assisted or partly automated by apps and AI agents. But the job still depends heavily on physical presence, real-time customer service, trust, and handling in-person requests in hotels, residences, and offices, which limits full automation.

title: Conservation scientists and foresters
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of physical field work, such as fire suppression, planting trees, and navigating difficult terrain, which provides a natural barrier to AI automation. However, a substantial portion of the role involves data analysis, GIS mapping, and regulatory compliance—tasks where AI can significantly enhance productivity and decision-making. While AI will reshape the information-processing aspects of the job, the requirement for real-world presence and manual intervention keeps exposure moderate.
new_rationale: This job mixes meaningful knowledge work—analyzing environmental data, GIS and remote sensing, planning, reporting, and contracts—with substantial fieldwork, physical presence, and on-the-ground judgment in forests, rangelands, and fire conditions. AI will likely improve productivity on mapping, monitoring, and analysis tasks, but it cannot replace much of the core in-person inspection, land management, emergency response, and stakeholder coordination.

title: Construction and building inspectors
old_rationale: The occupation is a hybrid of physical site visits and digital knowledge work. AI and computer vision can significantly automate the 'plans examiner' and report-writing functions, while remote inspection technology is already projected to reduce labor demand. However, the core requirement for physical presence to navigate tight spaces, use manual testing equipment, and verify real-world conditions provides a substantial barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical and site-based: inspecting structures, using testing equipment, climbing through spaces, and verifying real-world conditions against code. AI can help with plan review, report writing, code lookup, scheduling, and some remote inspection workflows, but it cannot replace most onsite inspection duties, so exposure is moderate rather than high.

title: Construction equipment operators
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical operation of heavy machinery in unpredictable, outdoor environments that require real-time human coordination and manual maintenance. While AI and automation are increasingly integrated into equipment via GPS and computerized controls, the physical nature of the work and the need for on-site safety judgment provide a significant barrier to full automation in the near term.
new_rationale: This job is centered on operating heavy machinery in physical, dynamic construction environments, with real-time spatial judgment, safety awareness, and on-site coordination that AI alone cannot easily replace. AI may increasingly assist through GPS guidance, automation features, diagnostics, and planning, but the core work still requires human presence and control of equipment in the field.

title: Construction laborers and helpers
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are almost entirely physical, involving manual labor, tool operation, and site preparation in unpredictable outdoor environments. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like scheduling or specialized robotic equipment control, the vast majority of the work requires human presence, physical strength, and real-time adaptation to physical obstacles.
new_rationale: This job is overwhelmingly physical and site-based: moving materials, cleaning sites, digging, setting up scaffolding, and assisting tradesworkers in real-world environments. AI may modestly affect planning, equipment, or prefabrication around the edges, but it does not directly automate most core daily tasks without broader advances in robotics.

title: Construction managers
old_rationale: Construction management is a hybrid role that combines digital knowledge work—such as cost estimation, scheduling, and contract analysis—with essential physical presence. While AI can significantly automate budgeting and logistical planning, the core of the job requires real-world site inspections, physical problem-solving, and high-stakes interpersonal negotiation with tradesworkers and inspectors that cannot be replicated digitally.
new_rationale: AI can meaningfully assist with estimating, budgeting, scheduling, contract review, progress reporting, and other information-heavy parts of the job. But construction managers still spend much of their time onsite coordinating people, handling delays and emergencies, and making real-world judgment calls in changing physical environments, which limits full automation.

title: Cooks
old_rationale: The core of the job involves physical dexterity, sensory judgment (taste and smell), and real-time adaptation in a high-heat, unpredictable kitchen environment. While AI-driven automation is already impacting fast food and routine institutional cooking, high-end restaurant and private household cooking remain protected by the need for complex manual skills and human creativity.
new_rationale: Cooking is fundamentally physical, hands-on work that requires on-site food preparation, kitchen coordination, and sensory judgment, which creates a strong barrier to direct AI automation. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like menu planning, inventory, scheduling, and some fast-food process automation, but it does not substantially replace the core work of most cooks.

title: Correctional officers and bailiffs
old_rationale: The core duties are physical and interpersonal, requiring real-time human presence to maintain security, conduct searches, and physically restrain individuals. AI exposure is limited to peripheral administrative tasks like report writing and log entry, or enhancing surveillance systems, but it cannot replace the physical oversight and intervention required in correctional and courtroom environments.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical, in-person security and supervision in prisons and courtrooms: maintaining order, escorting people, conducting searches, and responding to unpredictable confrontations. AI may assist with paperwork, surveillance review, risk flagging, and scheduling, but it does not replace the need for constant human presence, authority, and physical intervention.

title: Cost estimators
old_rationale: Cost estimation is a data-intensive, digital-first occupation centered on analyzing blueprints, technical documents, and historical databases to predict costs. AI and advanced software are already driving a projected decline in employment by automating routine calculations and simulations, though human oversight remains necessary for complex site-specific variables and high-stakes negotiations.
new_rationale: Cost estimating is largely digital knowledge work: analyzing data, reading technical documents, preparing bids, and writing reports are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. Some human judgment and occasional site visits remain important, but improving estimation software and the occupation’s already declining employment outlook indicate substantial AI-driven productivity gains and restructuring.

title: Court reporters and simultaneous captioners
old_rationale: The core product of this occupation is a verbatim digital transcript, a task where AI speech-to-text and natural language processing are already highly proficient. While legal requirements for human oversight and the need to clarify inaudible speech provide a temporary buffer, the rapid advancement of AI in transcription and real-time captioning creates extreme pressure for automation and massive productivity gains that reduce the need for human workers.
new_rationale: This occupation’s core output is fully digital text generated from speech, and AI speech recognition, real-time transcription, and captioning tools are improving rapidly and already automate large portions of the work. Human workers still matter for legal-grade accuracy, speaker identification, clarification, and quality control in high-stakes settings, but the occupation is likely to be heavily reshaped by AI through automation and reduced labor needed per transcript.

title: Craft and fine artists
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical craftsmanship and digital creation. While craft artists (potters, glassblowers) and sculptors have low exposure due to the physical nature of their work, digital artists and illustrators face very high exposure as AI can now generate high-quality imagery and complex designs from text prompts. The overall score reflects this split, as AI significantly impacts the commercial and digital segments of the field while leaving traditional physical mediums largely intact.
new_rationale: AI can strongly affect the digital parts of this occupation—especially illustration, concept generation, portfolio creation, marketing copy, and online sales—while also increasing competition through cheap AI-generated images. But much of the occupation remains rooted in physical making, distinctive personal style, hands-on materials, and the human value buyers place on originality and craft, which limits full automation.

title: Credit counselors
old_rationale: The core tasks of credit counseling—analyzing financial data, creating budgets, and explaining debt management options—are fundamentally digital and information-based, making them highly susceptible to AI automation. While the role requires interpersonal skills and emotional support for clients in financial distress, AI can already perform the complex calculations and personalized recommendation generation that constitute the bulk of the technical work.
new_rationale: Credit counselors do substantial digital information work—analyzing finances, calculating budgets, generating debt plans, and preparing written recommendations—all areas where AI can significantly assist or partially automate. But the job also depends heavily on trust, empathy, client communication, and negotiation with creditors, so AI is more likely to augment and reduce headcount somewhat than fully replace the human role.

title: Customer service representatives
old_rationale: The core duties of this role—answering questions, processing orders, and resolving complaints—are fundamentally digital and information-based, making them highly susceptible to AI-driven automation. Large Language Models and AI agents are already capable of handling complex natural language interactions, and the BLS specifically projects a decline in employment due to automation in this sector.
new_rationale: This job is highly exposed because much of the core work—answering questions, handling routine requests, processing account changes, documenting interactions, and communicating by phone/chat/email—is digital and increasingly automatable by AI. Some roles still require human judgment, empathy, or in-person help for complex and upset customers, but the occupation is already being reduced by self-service systems and AI will likely further shrink headcount by handling a growing share of standard interactions.

title: Dancers and choreographers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is physical performance, athleticism, and real-time human interaction in physical spaces, which AI cannot replicate. While AI may assist choreographers with music selection or administrative tasks, the fundamental value of the work lies in the human body's movement and live artistic expression.
new_rationale: The core of this occupation is embodied physical performance, rehearsal, and in-person artistic direction, which creates a strong barrier to AI automation. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like music selection, promotion, budgeting, or generating choreographic ideas, but it is unlikely to replace live dancers’ physical skill or choreographers’ hands-on instruction and staging in the near term.

title: Data scientists
old_rationale: Data science is a fully digital occupation centered on coding, statistical modeling, and data analysis—all areas where AI is rapidly achieving parity or superiority. While human judgment is still needed for business context and ethical oversight, AI can now automate significant portions of the data pipeline, including cleaning raw data, generating complex code, and even suggesting optimal model architectures.
new_rationale: This job is almost entirely digital: collecting, cleaning, analyzing data, building models, writing code, creating visualizations, and communicating findings are all tasks where AI is advancing rapidly. Human judgment, stakeholder communication, and domain context still matter, but AI is likely to dramatically increase each data scientist’s productivity and reshape the role.

title: Database administrators and architects
old_rationale: This occupation is entirely digital, involving coding (SQL), system architecture, and data management—all areas where AI is exceptionally proficient. AI can already automate routine DBA tasks like performance tuning, query optimization, and security monitoring, while LLMs are highly capable of generating and debugging the complex schemas and scripts used by database architects.
new_rationale: This is highly digital, computer-based work: designing schemas, writing/querying SQL, monitoring performance, troubleshooting, documenting, and managing permissions are all tasks where AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. But the role still carries significant responsibility for security, reliability, production risk, and organization-specific architecture decisions, so AI is more likely to reshape and boost productivity than fully replace the occupation.

title: Delivery truck drivers and driver/sales workers
old_rationale: While AI and autonomous vehicle technology are advancing, the core of this job involves significant physical labor, such as loading/unloading cargo and navigating unpredictable 'last-mile' environments. AI may optimize routing and assist with sales communication, but the physical requirement to move goods from a vehicle to a customer's door remains a major barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: This job is centered on physical driving, loading/unloading, and in-person delivery in unpredictable real-world environments, which limits near-term AI automation. But AI can already optimize routing, dispatching, customer communication, and paperwork, and over time advances in autonomous vehicles and delivery systems could significantly reshape staffing and productivity.

title: Dental and ophthalmic laboratory technicians and medical appliance technicians
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical craftsmanship and digital design, with AI and automation already driving a projected decline in dental technician roles. While the physical assembly, polishing, and repair of medical devices require manual dexterity, the 'knowledge' phase of the job—interpreting prescriptions and designing appliances—is rapidly shifting toward AI-integrated CAD/CAM software and 3D printing.
