new_rationale: This work is centered on physical fabrication, repair, finishing, and quality inspection of medical devices, dentures, and lenses, which limits direct AI automation because it requires hands-on dexterity, specialized materials handling, and use of tools and machinery. AI and related automation can still materially reshape the occupation through CAD software, 3D printing, and automated lens or dental part production, increasing productivity and reducing labor needs in some specialties.

title: Dental assistants
old_rationale: The core of the job involves physical, hands-on tasks such as sterilizing instruments, assisting in surgeries, and providing direct patient care that requires human presence and manual dexterity. While AI can significantly automate the administrative and digital aspects of the role—such as scheduling, billing, and preliminary X-ray analysis—the majority of the workday is spent in the physical dental operatory where AI cannot currently replace human labor.
new_rationale: Dental assistants’ core work is hands-on and in-person: preparing treatment rooms, sterilizing instruments, assisting during procedures, taking x rays, and supporting patients in real time. AI may reduce time spent on scheduling, billing, records, and some imaging/documentation tasks, but it does not replace the physical chairside assistance and patient interaction that define the job.

title: Dental hygienists
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves high-precision manual dexterity and physical procedures performed on patients in a clinical setting, which AI cannot replicate. While AI may assist with peripheral tasks like analyzing X-rays or automating patient documentation, the primary work of cleaning teeth and conducting physical exams remains insulated from automation.
new_rationale: Dental hygienists’ core work is hands-on, physical, and performed in person: cleaning teeth, applying treatments, taking x-rays, and working precisely inside patients’ mouths. AI may help with documentation, imaging interpretation, and care planning, but it does not replace the occupation’s central manual and patient-facing tasks.

title: Dentists
old_rationale: The core of dentistry involves highly precise physical procedures, manual dexterity, and real-time patient interaction in a clinical setting, which are resistant to AI automation. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic accuracy through X-ray analysis and streamline administrative practice management, the physical requirement of performing surgery and restorative work creates a strong barrier to high exposure.
new_rationale: Dentistry has meaningful AI exposure in diagnostic imaging, treatment planning, documentation, scheduling, and practice management, where AI can improve productivity. But the core job is hands-on physical care in a patient’s mouth requiring dexterity, licensure, and in-person patient interaction, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Desktop publishers
old_rationale: The work product is entirely digital and involves routine information processing, layout design, and basic editing—all areas where AI is highly proficient. Generative AI and automated layout tools can now handle text integration, image adjustment, and formatting with minimal human intervention, leading to a projected decline in the occupation as these tasks are automated or absorbed by other roles using AI tools.
new_rationale: Desktop publishing is almost entirely digital work done on a computer: formatting text, integrating graphics, revising layouts, and preparing files for print or online publication. AI can increasingly automate or accelerate core tasks such as layout generation, proofreading, image editing, and template-based design, likely reducing headcount even where human aesthetic judgment and collaboration still matter.

title: Diagnostic medical sonographers
old_rationale: The occupation is a hybrid of physical and digital work; while AI is rapidly advancing in image analysis and diagnostic assistance, the role requires significant physical presence to position patients and manipulate transducers with high hand-eye coordination. Furthermore, the interpersonal aspect of patient care and the real-time physical adjustments needed during a scan provide a substantial barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: This job’s core work requires in-person patient care, physical positioning, hands-on operation of ultrasound equipment, and real-time adjustment of the scan based on what is seen, which creates a strong barrier to full AI automation. AI can meaningfully assist with image quality checks, abnormality detection, measurements, documentation, and summaries, but it is more likely to augment sonographers than replace most of the role.

title: Diesel service technicians and mechanics
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is highly physical, requiring manual dexterity, strength, and real-time problem-solving in a hands-on environment. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic software and technical manuals used by technicians, it cannot perform the physical repairs, parts replacement, or tool manipulation that define the job.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical, hands-on inspection, repair, and maintenance of diesel vehicles in shops or at worksites, which creates a strong barrier to AI automation. AI can help with diagnostics, interpreting manuals, and administrative tasks, but it does not replace the manual dexterity, troubleshooting in the physical world, and in-person testing that define the occupation.

title: Dietitians and nutritionists
old_rationale: The core tasks of analyzing nutritional data, interpreting research, and creating personalized meal plans are highly digital and data-driven, making them susceptible to AI automation and augmentation. However, the role requires significant interpersonal counseling, empathy, and clinical judgment in medical settings, which provides a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Dietitians and nutritionists do substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with: analyzing dietary information, drafting meal plans, creating educational materials, summarizing research, and documenting client progress. But the occupation also depends on licensed clinical judgment, individualized counseling, empathy, and coordination with patients and care teams, which limits full automation of the core role.

title: Drafters
old_rationale: Drafters perform work that is almost entirely digital, converting conceptual sketches into technical CAD and BIM models. AI is rapidly advancing in generative design and automated technical drawing, allowing for the immediate translation of engineering specifications into precise models, which significantly reduces the human labor required for these tasks.
new_rationale: Drafters' core work is highly digital: translating sketches and specifications into CAD/BIM technical drawings on a computer. That makes the occupation very exposed to AI-driven automation and productivity gains, especially because generative design tools can create, revise, and detail plans, and BLS already notes that software lets engineers and architects absorb tasks once done by drafters.

title: Drywall installers, ceiling tile installers, and tapers
old_rationale: The core tasks of this occupation are entirely physical, involving manual labor, dexterity, and movement in unpredictable construction environments. While AI might assist with peripheral administrative tasks or material estimation, it cannot perform the physical installation, cutting, or finishing of drywall and ceiling tiles.
new_rationale: This work is overwhelmingly physical and on-site: measuring, cutting, lifting, fastening, taping, and finishing drywall in real buildings. AI may modestly help with estimating materials, scheduling, or planning, but it does not meaningfully automate the core hands-on tasks, so overall exposure is low.

title: Economists
old_rationale: Economists perform work that is almost entirely digital, involving data analysis, mathematical modeling, and technical writing. AI is exceptionally proficient at the core tasks of this role, including processing large datasets, identifying statistical patterns, and generating reports, which significantly increases productivity and reduces the number of human workers needed for routine analysis.
new_rationale: Economists do predominantly digital knowledge work: collecting and analyzing data, building models, forecasting trends, and writing reports and presentations. AI is rapidly improving at statistical analysis, coding, summarization, and drafting, so it can automate or accelerate large parts of the job, though higher-level judgment, policy interpretation, and credibility with decision-makers still keep humans central.

title: Editors
old_rationale: Editing is a fully digital occupation centered on language processing, a core strength of Large Language Models. AI can already perform high-level copy editing, fact-checking, and rewriting, significantly increasing individual productivity and reducing the total number of human editors required for routine content management.
new_rationale: Editing is overwhelmingly digital work: reading, rewriting, proofreading, fact-checking, headline generation, and coordinating written content are all tasks AI can already assist with and increasingly automate. Human judgment, taste, and editorial decision-making still matter, especially for ethics and publication choices, but the occupation is likely to be heavily reshaped as AI raises productivity and reduces demand for routine editing work.

title: Electrical and electronic engineering technologists and technicians
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of physical labor, such as assembling prototypes, soldering circuitry, and repairing hardware, which provides a buffer against full automation. However, a substantial portion of the role involves digital tasks like drafting schematics, analyzing test data, and writing technical reports—all areas where AI is becoming highly proficient. AI will likely augment their troubleshooting capabilities and automate the documentation aspects of the job, increasing individual productivity but potentially reducing overall demand.
new_rationale: This job mixes hands-on physical work—assembling prototypes, calibrating instruments, testing equipment, repairing components, and visiting sites—with technical analysis and documentation. AI can significantly assist with diagnostics, test-data analysis, drafting diagrams/specifications, and reporting, but much of the core work still requires physical presence, lab equipment, and manual troubleshooting.

title: Electrical and electronics engineers
old_rationale: The core work of designing circuits, modeling systems, and writing documentation is digital and highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and optimization. While physical testing, site visits, and manufacturing supervision provide a buffer, the rapid advancement of AI in CAD, simulation, and coding significantly increases the productivity and exposure of the knowledge-work components.
new_rationale: This is largely knowledge work done with CAD, simulation, analysis, programming, and documentation, so AI can significantly assist or automate parts of design iteration, troubleshooting, and technical writing. But the job still depends heavily on domain judgment, safety-critical validation, hardware testing, and coordination with manufacturing and physical systems, which limits full automation.

title: Electrical and electronics installers and repairers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical manipulation of hardware, manual troubleshooting in unpredictable environments, and the use of hand tools, which are highly resistant to AI automation. While AI can significantly enhance diagnostic software and predictive maintenance schedules, the physical acts of disassembling, repairing, and reassembling complex electrical components require human dexterity and presence.
new_rationale: This job is centered on physical, on-site installation, testing, disassembly, and repair of electrical and electronic equipment, which limits direct AI automation. AI can help with diagnostics, documentation, and troubleshooting support, but the core work still requires hands-on skill, use of tools, and presence in factories, substations, vehicles, or other job sites.

title: Electrical power-line installers and repairers
old_rationale: The core work is highly physical, involving climbing towers, operating heavy machinery, and manual repair in unpredictable outdoor environments. While AI can assist with remote monitoring and diagnostic data analysis, it cannot perform the high-risk physical labor required to install or repair high-voltage infrastructure.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical, outdoor, and safety-critical: climbing poles, operating equipment, handling high-voltage lines, and restoring service in unpredictable field conditions. AI may help with peripheral tasks like outage analysis, diagnostics, and planning, but it does not substantially automate the hands-on repair and installation work.

title: Electricians
old_rationale: The core work of an electrician is highly physical, involving manual installation, repair, and navigation of complex, unpredictable physical environments like construction sites and crawl spaces. While AI may assist with peripheral tasks like blueprint analysis, diagnostic calculations, or scheduling, it cannot perform the tactile labor or real-time physical problem-solving required for the job.
new_rationale: Electricians’ core work is hands-on and site-specific: installing, troubleshooting, and repairing physical electrical systems in homes, businesses, and construction environments. AI may help with peripheral tasks like reading plans, diagnostics support, code lookup, or scheduling, but it does not replace the manual skill, safety judgment, and physical presence the job requires.

title: Electro-mechanical and mechatronics technologists and technicians
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of physical labor, including soldering, operating metalworking machinery, and repairing hydraulic systems, which provides a strong barrier against full AI automation. However, AI will increasingly assist with the knowledge-based aspects of the job, such as analyzing test results, interpreting complex schematics, and diagnosing faults in the very robotic systems these technicians maintain.
new_rationale: This job mixes technical knowledge work with substantial hands-on physical work: assembling, testing, calibrating, repairing, and maintaining electromechanical and robotic equipment on-site. AI can assist with diagnostics, documentation, test analysis, and troubleshooting, but the core tasks still require physical presence, manual skill, and interaction with real machinery.

title: Elementary, middle, and high school principals
old_rationale: Principals perform significant knowledge work—such as curriculum development, budget management, and data analysis of student performance—that is highly susceptible to AI augmentation and automation. However, the core of the role requires physical presence for school safety, real-time crisis management, and high-stakes interpersonal interactions with students, parents, and staff that AI cannot currently replicate.
new_rationale: School principals do substantial knowledge work—analyzing achievement data, writing reports, managing schedules and budgets, and coordinating staff—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity on many administrative tasks. But the core of the job still depends heavily on in-person leadership, student discipline, teacher observation, crisis handling, and trust-based relationships with parents, staff, and the community, which limits full automation.

title: Elevator and escalator installers and repairers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves highly physical, manual labor in unpredictable environments, such as elevator shafts and machine rooms, which provides a strong barrier against AI automation. While AI can significantly enhance diagnostic troubleshooting and predictive maintenance through computerized control systems, the actual installation, repair, and physical manipulation of heavy mechanical components still require a human presence. AI acts as a sophisticated tool for the technician rather than a replacement for the physical work.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical, on-site installation, maintenance, and repair of complex equipment in shafts, machine rooms, and other real-world environments. AI may help with diagnostics, reading documentation, and service records, but it is unlikely to replace the hands-on mechanical, electrical, and safety-critical work that defines the occupation.

title: Emergency management directors
old_rationale: The role involves significant digital knowledge work, such as analyzing hazards, drafting complex response plans, and managing federal funding applications, all of which are highly susceptible to AI augmentation. However, the core of the job requires high-stakes real-time decision-making, physical presence at command centers during disasters, and intense interpersonal coordination with government officials and the public, providing a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: This job includes substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with: drafting emergency plans, analyzing hazards and damage assessments, reviewing regulations, preparing grant applications, and coordinating information. But core responsibilities also depend heavily on real-world leadership, interagency coordination, public communication, and high-stakes decision-making during live emergencies, which require human presence and judgment.

title: EMTs and paramedics
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical intervention, manual dexterity, and real-time human presence in unpredictable environments, which are highly resistant to AI. While AI will significantly assist with peripheral tasks like EKG interpretation, diagnostic decision support, and automated documentation, the physical requirements of patient transport and hands-on emergency care remain essential human functions.
new_rationale: EMTs and paramedics do highly physical, real-time emergency work that requires on-scene presence, patient handling, transport, and rapid interpersonal judgment in unpredictable environments. AI may help with documentation, dispatch, decision support, and interpreting data like EKGs, but it is unlikely to replace the core hands-on field care and transport functions.

title: Entertainment and recreation managers
old_rationale: This role involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, such as budgeting, scheduling, and strategic planning, which is highly susceptible to AI optimization. However, the core of the job requires physical presence to inspect facilities, manage real-time emergencies, and provide high-touch interpersonal leadership and customer service in physical environments like parks and fitness centers.
new_rationale: This job mixes office-based planning, budgeting, scheduling, and staff coordination—areas where AI can provide meaningful assistance—with substantial on-site operational oversight, customer service, and responsibility for facility safety. AI will likely boost productivity in the administrative and analytical parts of the role, but the core job still depends heavily on in-person management, judgment, and real-world coordination.

title: Environmental engineering technologists and technicians
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical fieldwork and digital knowledge work. While AI can significantly automate data analysis, report reviewing, and regulatory compliance checks, it cannot physically collect soil and water samples, maintain field equipment, or perform on-site hazardous material disposal. The BLS already notes that automated testing is increasing productivity, which limits job growth even as the core physical tasks remain human-centric.
new_rationale: This job combines meaningful digital and analytical tasks—recordkeeping, document review, sample analysis, scheduling, and compliance work—with substantial hands-on field and lab work that requires physical presence. AI and automation can boost productivity in testing, reporting, and monitoring, but they are less able to replace on-site sampling, equipment handling, inspections, and hazardous-material coordination.

title: Environmental engineers
old_rationale: Environmental engineering involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, including data analysis, system design, and report writing, which are highly susceptible to AI augmentation. However, the role is insulated by the necessity for physical site inspections, regulatory compliance monitoring in the field, and the legal accountability associated with Professional Engineer (PE) licensure.
new_rationale: Environmental engineers do substantial computer-based knowledge work—analyzing data, preparing reports, reviewing permits, and designing pollution-control or water-treatment systems—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in core parts of the job. But the occupation also depends on site inspections, project monitoring, regulatory accountability, and engineering judgment tied to physical infrastructure, which limits full automation.

title: Environmental science and protection technicians
old_rationale: The core of this role involves physical fieldwork, such as collecting soil and water samples and inspecting facilities, which AI cannot perform. However, AI will significantly impact the digital aspects of the job, including data analysis, the preparation of reports and charts, and the modeling of environmental impacts.
new_rationale: This job mixes field inspections, physical sample collection, equipment setup, and lab handling with some digital reporting and analysis. AI can assist with documentation, data interpretation, and compliance support, but the core work still depends heavily on on-site presence, precise physical procedures, and human judgment in real-world environments.

title: Environmental scientists and specialists
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, including data analysis, report writing, and regulatory compliance, which are highly susceptible to AI augmentation. However, the role is anchored by a physical component involving fieldwork, site inspections, and laboratory sample analysis that AI cannot currently replicate. AI will likely serve as a powerful tool for modeling environmental impacts and drafting technical documents, increasing individual productivity while leaving the physical data collection and stakeholder relationship management to humans.
new_rationale: This job has substantial knowledge-work components—analyzing environmental data, preparing reports, assessing risks, and advising clients or officials—that AI can increasingly assist with. But core work still often depends on field sampling, laboratory activity, regulatory interpretation, and context-specific scientific judgment, so AI is more likely to augment than fully replace these workers.

title: Epidemiologists
old_rationale: Epidemiology is a data-intensive knowledge profession where core tasks like statistical analysis, pattern recognition in large datasets, and literature reviews are highly susceptible to AI augmentation. While the role requires human judgment for policy-making, community outreach, and field investigations, the shift toward digital health records and automated surveillance systems significantly increases the productivity and exposure of the occupation.
new_rationale: Epidiology is heavily exposed because much of the core work is digital knowledge work: analyzing datasets, designing studies, writing reports, communicating findings, and drafting grant proposals. But exposure is not at the very top because the job also depends on domain judgment, public health decision-making, fieldwork, handling samples, and coordination during real-world health investigations and emergencies.

title: Exercise physiologists
old_rationale: The role involves a significant amount of physical presence, including conducting stress tests, measuring vital signs with medical equipment, and providing hands-on rehabilitative care. While AI can highly automate the digital aspects of the job—such as analyzing medical history, processing biometric data, and generating personalized exercise regimens—the core requirement for real-time human interaction, physical assessment, and clinical supervision in a medical setting limits its total exposure.
new_rationale: Exercise physiologists do use information-heavy tasks that AI can support, such as analyzing medical histories, interpreting test data, documenting progress, and suggesting personalized exercise plans. But the core job still depends heavily on in-person assessment, monitoring patients during exercise, hands-on implementation, and human judgment and rapport in healthcare settings, which limits overall exposure.

title: Farmers, ranchers, and other agricultural managers
old_rationale: The role involves a significant amount of physical labor, equipment maintenance, and real-time management of biological systems that cannot be digitized. However, AI will heavily impact the management side of the job, including crop yield prediction, financial planning, and automated resource allocation, making individual managers significantly more productive and contributing to further farm consolidation.
new_rationale: This occupation is grounded in physical, on-site work: supervising planting, harvesting, livestock care, equipment, land, and facilities all require real-world presence and hands-on judgment. AI can meaningfully assist with planning, forecasting, recordkeeping, pricing, and operational decisions, but it does not replace the core physical and environmental management of running a farm or ranch.

title: Fashion designers
old_rationale: Fashion design is increasingly digital, utilizing CAD and graphics software for sketching, trend analysis, and virtual modeling—all areas where generative AI is rapidly advancing. While the physical aspects of selecting fabrics, creating prototypes, and overseeing production provide a buffer, the core creative and analytical tasks are highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and productivity gains.
new_rationale: Fashion design has a large digital and conceptual component—sketching, CAD work, trend research, ideation, presentation, and marketing—that AI can increasingly generate or accelerate. But the occupation is not fully automatable because material selection, fit on real bodies, prototype iteration, production oversight, and brand-specific creative judgment still require substantial human involvement.

title: Film and video editors and camera operators
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical camera operation and digital video editing. While camera operators have a physical barrier to AI due to the need for on-site equipment management and real-world positioning, editors work entirely in digital environments where AI is rapidly automating tasks like color grading, sound syncing, and even rough-cut generation. The rise of generative video and AI-driven post-production tools significantly increases the productivity of each worker, potentially reducing the total number of editors needed for complex projects.
new_rationale: AI exposure is very high overall because film and video editing is almost entirely digital, and AI can already assist with organizing footage, rough cuts, color/audio cleanup, effects, and content generation. Exposure is somewhat tempered by camera-operator work, which still requires physical presence, equipment handling, and creative on-set judgment, but robotic cameras and workflow automation will still reduce labor needs and reshape the occupation.

title: Financial analysts
old_rationale: Financial analysts perform work that is almost entirely digital, involving the processing of large datasets, trend analysis, and the generation of reports—all areas where AI excels. While high-level strategy and client relationships provide some insulation, the core tasks of data evaluation, risk assessment, and financial modeling are being rapidly automated or significantly enhanced by AI, leading to major restructuring of the occupation.
new_rationale: Financial analysts do predominantly digital work: analyzing financial data, building forecasts, valuing companies, monitoring trends, and writing reports, all of which are areas where AI is advancing quickly. Human judgment, client trust, and meetings with company officials still matter, but AI is likely to automate substantial portions of research and modeling and significantly increase each analyst’s productivity.

title: Financial clerks
old_rationale: Financial clerks perform routine, digital-first tasks such as data entry, record updating, and basic financial calculations that are highly susceptible to AI automation. While some roles involve customer interaction, the core functions—processing claims, verifying credit, and managing payroll—are increasingly handled by AI-driven software, leading to a projected decline in employment.
new_rationale: Financial clerks do largely routine, rules-based information processing: updating records, calculating charges, verifying applications, handling transactions, and communicating standard information. Those tasks are highly digitizable and already being reduced by automation, online self-service, and productivity software, though some roles still require in-person customer service or physical cash handling.

title: Financial examiners
old_rationale: Financial examiners perform work that is almost entirely digital, involving the analysis of balance sheets, loan documentation, and regulatory text. AI is exceptionally well-suited for automating the core tasks of this role, such as identifying anomalies in financial data, summarizing meeting minutes, and checking compliance against complex legal frameworks, though human judgment remains necessary for final risk assessments and high-level management evaluations.
new_rationale: Financial examiners do predominantly digital knowledge work: reviewing financial statements and loan files, analyzing regulatory impacts, assessing risk, and writing compliance reports. AI can increasingly automate document review, anomaly detection, risk analysis, and report drafting, though human judgment, accountability, and some onsite examination of institutions keep exposure below the maximum.

title: Financial managers
old_rationale: Financial management is a predominantly digital knowledge-work occupation focused on data analysis, reporting, and forecasting, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation and enhancement. While the role requires significant human judgment, strategic advisory, and leadership, the core technical tasks of monitoring financial details and preparing statements are being rapidly transformed by AI tools.
new_rationale: This is predominantly digital knowledge work: analyzing financial data, preparing reports and forecasts, monitoring compliance, and advising executives are all tasks AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. Exposure is tempered by the need for high-stakes judgment, regulatory accountability, industry-specific expertise, and management of people and organizational decisions.

title: Fire inspectors
old_rationale: The role requires a significant physical presence for onsite inspections, evidence collection in debris, and forest patrolling, which provides a natural barrier to AI. However, AI can substantially assist with digital tasks such as reviewing building blueprints, analyzing fire scene data, and automating the documentation of code violations and legal reports.
new_rationale: Fire inspectors’ core work depends heavily on physical site visits, examining buildings or fire scenes, handling evidence, and interacting with property owners, witnesses, and courts. AI can help with report writing, code lookup, blueprint review, documentation, and some analytical tasks, but it cannot replace the on-the-ground inspection, evidence collection, and enforcement functions that define the job.

title: Firefighters
old_rationale: The core duties of firefighting are intensely physical and require real-time presence in unpredictable, hazardous environments where AI cannot currently operate. While AI may enhance peripheral tasks like incident reporting, equipment maintenance scheduling, and predictive wildfire modeling, the essential work of rescuing victims and extinguishing fires remains a human-centric manual task.
new_rationale: Firefighting is overwhelmingly physical, on-site emergency response work in dangerous, unpredictable environments that require human strength, mobility, and real-time judgment. AI may help at the margins with dispatch support, reporting, training, and risk prediction, but it does not meaningfully automate the core tasks of rescuing people, operating equipment, and responding to fires and medical emergencies.

title: Fishing and hunting workers
old_rationale: The core duties are highly physical and take place in unpredictable outdoor environments, requiring manual labor, mechanical repair, and real-time physical coordination. AI's impact is limited to peripheral enhancements in fish-finding sonar, GPS navigation, and regulatory data processing, but it cannot replace the physical act of hauling nets, trapping animals, or maintaining vessels.
new_rationale: This work is overwhelmingly physical, outdoors, and done in hazardous real-world environments where workers must operate vessels, handle gear, and catch, sort, and store animals by hand. AI may modestly assist with navigation, fish-finding, weather analysis, or regulatory paperwork, but it does not substantially automate the core hands-on work.

title: Fitness trainers and instructors
old_rationale: The core of this occupation requires physical presence to demonstrate exercises, correct form in real-time to prevent injury, and provide high-touch motivational support. While AI can automate peripheral tasks like workout programming, nutrition planning, and social media marketing, it cannot replace the physical safety monitoring and interpersonal accountability that define the role.
new_rationale: The core of this job is physical, in-person, and interpersonal: demonstrating exercises, watching form, motivating clients, ensuring safety, and providing hands-on coaching. AI can assist with workout planning, progress tracking, marketing, and online content, but it does not replace the real-time human presence central to most training and instruction.

title: Flight attendants
old_rationale: The core responsibilities of flight attendants are physical and interpersonal, involving safety enforcement, emergency medical response, and manual service tasks that AI cannot perform. While AI may assist with peripheral digital tasks like report writing or passenger manifest management, the role is largely defined by a physical presence required by law for passenger safety.
new_rationale: Flight attendants’ core work is physical and in-person: ensuring passenger safety, responding to emergencies, enforcing regulations, and providing onboard service in a live aircraft environment. AI may automate peripheral tasks such as reporting, scheduling, or some customer communication, but it cannot replace the legal, physical, and interpersonal presence required onboard.

title: Flooring installers and tile and stone setters
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are entirely physical, involving manual dexterity, heavy lifting, and real-time adaptation to irregular physical surfaces in unpredictable environments. While AI might assist with minor administrative tasks or design visualization, it cannot perform the tactile labor of cutting, laying, and finishing materials on-site.
new_rationale: This job is overwhelmingly physical and site-specific: removing old materials, measuring, cutting, fitting, fastening, grouting, sanding, and finishing in real-world environments. AI may help a bit with estimating materials, scheduling, or reading design plans, but it does not meaningfully automate the core hands-on installation work.

title: Floral designers
old_rationale: The core of the job is a physical craft involving the handling of perishable biological materials and manual assembly, which AI cannot perform. However, AI can significantly assist with peripheral digital tasks such as generating design concepts for customers, managing inventory, and automating customer service for online orders.
new_rationale: Floral design’s core work is physical and hands-on: selecting fresh materials, arranging flowers, preparing orders, and setting up displays or event installations in the real world. AI may help with peripheral tasks like marketing, order intake, inventory, and suggesting design ideas, but it does not substantially automate the central manual, aesthetic, and customer-facing parts of the job.

title: Food and beverage serving and related workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, such as clearing tables, carrying heavy trays, and cleaning, which are difficult to automate with current robotics. However, AI is increasingly handling digital peripheral tasks like order-taking via kiosks or voice-AI drive-thrus and managing reservations, which reduces the need for human staff in front-of-house roles.
new_rationale: This work is dominated by in-person physical tasks: taking and delivering orders, preparing simple items, cleaning tables, stocking stations, and interacting with customers on site. AI may reduce some demand at the margins through self-order kiosks, reservation systems, and automated payment/order handling, but it does not directly replace most core duties without broader robotics adoption.

title: Food preparation workers
old_rationale: The core duties are highly physical and require manual dexterity in unpredictable kitchen environments, which are difficult for AI to automate directly. While AI might optimize inventory management or scheduling, the primary tasks of washing, cutting, and preparing food remain resistant to digital replacement, though some labor may be displaced by industrial food processing automation.
new_rationale: This job is centered on physical, in-person kitchen tasks like chopping, cleaning, stocking, and handling food safely, which are not easily automated by general AI software. AI may have small indirect effects through ordering, scheduling, or greater use of preprocessed ingredients and kitchen automation, but it does not strongly affect the core daily work.

title: Food processing equipment workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, such as loading ingredients, cleaning machinery, and manual quality checks, which are resistant to AI. While AI can optimize recipes, monitor sensor data, and automate some equipment controls, the requirement for human presence in a physical manufacturing environment to handle unpredictable mechanical issues and sanitation limits overall exposure.
new_rationale: This job is centered on physically operating, loading, monitoring, and cleaning food-processing machinery in industrial facilities, so the core work requires on-site presence and hands-on interaction with equipment and materials. AI may improve process control, quality monitoring, and reduce staffing at the margins through smarter automation, but it does not directly replace most daily tasks the way it can in fully digital occupations.

title: Food service managers
old_rationale: While AI can significantly automate administrative tasks like staff scheduling, inventory ordering, and payroll management, the core of the job requires physical presence and real-time human interaction. Managers must physically inspect facilities for safety, handle unpredictable interpersonal conflicts with staff and customers, and perform manual labor during peak hours, which provides a strong buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Food service managers have meaningful exposure because AI can help with scheduling, inventory ordering, budgeting, payroll, forecasting, promotions, and some customer-service tasks. But the core job still depends heavily on on-site physical oversight of staff, food safety, kitchen operations, and real-time handling of customers and problems in a restaurant environment, which limits overall exposure.

title: Forensic science technicians
old_rationale: The role is a hybrid of physical evidence collection and digital analysis. While AI can significantly automate pattern recognition (fingerprints, ballistics) and statistical reporting, the job requires physical presence at crime scenes to collect samples and the human credibility necessary to testify in court as an expert witness.
new_rationale: This job has meaningful AI exposure in its analytical and documentation tasks: AI can assist with image comparison, pattern matching, statistical interpretation, database searches, and drafting reports. But a large share of the occupation still requires physical crime-scene work, evidence handling, lab procedures, chain-of-custody responsibility, and court testimony, which limits full automation.

title: Forest and conservation workers
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are highly physical and performed in unpredictable outdoor environments, such as planting seedlings, clearing brush, and suppressing forest fires. While AI and remote sensing can assist with peripheral tasks like tree counting and health monitoring, the primary labor-intensive functions cannot be automated by digital AI systems.
new_rationale: This job is dominated by outdoor physical labor in remote, unpredictable environments: planting, clearing brush, operating equipment, spraying vegetation, and helping suppress fires. AI may reduce some employment through remote sensing and automation of selected forestry tasks, but it has limited impact on the core hands-on work that requires human presence and physical stamina.

title: Fundraisers
old_rationale: Fundraising involves significant digital work including donor research, data analysis, and the creation of promotional messaging, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation and enhancement. While major gift solicitation and event management require high-level interpersonal skills and physical presence, the majority of the administrative, analytical, and communication-based tasks are moving toward AI-driven workflows.
new_rationale: Fundraising combines highly AI-exposed digital tasks—researching donors, drafting messages, analyzing campaign results, managing records, and running online outreach—with relationship-driven work that still benefits from human trust and persuasion. AI is likely to make fundraisers significantly more productive and reduce labor needed for routine prospecting and communications, but in-person donor cultivation and event-based fundraising keep exposure below the highest tier.

title: Funeral service workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical tasks like preparing remains and transporting the deceased, alongside high-stakes interpersonal work providing grief counseling and comfort. While AI can streamline administrative duties like filing death certificates, scheduling, and writing obituaries, it cannot replace the physical presence and emotional intelligence required for funeral services.
new_rationale: AI can help with administrative and digital tasks such as filing documents, writing obituaries, scheduling services, handling marketing, and assisting with benefits or insurance paperwork. But the core of the occupation depends heavily on in-person grief counseling, coordinating physical logistics, and preparing and handling remains, which creates a substantial barrier to full automation.

title: Gambling services workers
old_rationale: The occupation is a hybrid of physical hospitality and digital-adjacent information processing. While AI and automation are significantly impacting sports book writers and slot operations through online platforms and automated betting, the core of the job for dealers and managers involves physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-time human interaction to ensure a 'pleasant experience' and monitor for physical rule violations.
new_rationale: Most gambling services work is in-person and physical: dealing games, taking bets, monitoring customers, paying winnings, and supervising casino floors all require real-time human presence and customer interaction. AI may reduce headcount indirectly through online gambling, automated betting, surveillance, and administrative tools, but it does not readily replace the core on-floor service work in physical casinos.

title: General maintenance and repair workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, manual dexterity, and real-time problem-solving in unpredictable physical environments, which are currently beyond the reach of AI and robotics. AI may assist with peripheral tasks like diagnosing equipment issues via computer vision or managing supply orders, but the primary work of repairing buildings and machinery remains a hands-on human requirement.
new_rationale: This job is centered on hands-on physical repair, inspection, and maintenance in buildings and around machinery, often requiring movement through real-world environments, tool use, and on-site troubleshooting. AI may help with peripheral tasks like diagnostics, recordkeeping, estimating, and ordering supplies, but it does not replace the core manual and in-person work.

title: General office clerks
old_rationale: The core duties of this role—data entry, document formatting, scheduling, and information processing—are almost entirely digital and routine, making them highly susceptible to AI automation. While some physical tasks like handling mail or greeting visitors remain, the vast majority of the workload can be absorbed by AI agents and automated software, as reflected in the BLS's projected decline for the occupation.
new_rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because many core tasks—data entry, document preparation, filing, scheduling, answering routine inquiries, and processing office paperwork—are routine information-processing activities that AI and related software can automate or greatly accelerate. Some in-person duties remain, such as receiving visitors and handling physical mail, but the role is still likely to be heavily restructured and reduced as AI boosts productivity and shifts clerical work to other employees.

title: Genetic counselors
old_rationale: Genetic counselors perform high-level knowledge work, including analyzing complex DNA data and medical histories, which is highly susceptible to AI optimization. However, the core of the role involves providing psychological support and navigating sensitive ethical decisions with patients, requiring a level of empathy and human connection that limits full automation.
new_rationale: Genetic counselors do substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with: collecting histories, reviewing records, interpreting test results, researching conditions, and drafting documentation. But the occupation also depends heavily on high-stakes clinical judgment, patient-specific communication, coordination with providers, and emotional support for distressed clients, which limits full automation.

title: Geographers
old_rationale: Geographers primarily perform digital knowledge work, including data analysis, GIS modeling, and report writing, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation and enhancement. While some fieldwork exists, the core of the profession involves processing satellite imagery and census data—tasks where AI and machine learning are rapidly becoming more efficient than humans.
new_rationale: Geographers do a large share of their core work on computers: analyzing spatial data, using GIS, creating maps, linking datasets, and writing reports—all areas where AI is advancing quickly. Fieldwork, interviews, and expert judgment about geographic methods and interpretation reduce full automation, but AI is likely to substantially reshape and increase productivity in the occupation.

title: Geological and hydrologic technicians
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical fieldwork and digital data analysis. While AI and automation (like drones and remote sensors) are increasingly handling data collection and mapping, the role still requires significant physical presence for equipment maintenance, manual sample collection, and laboratory testing that cannot be fully digitized.
new_rationale: This occupation mixes fieldwork, lab work, and office-based analysis. AI can increasingly help with data analysis, mapping, report writing, and interpreting measurements, but substantial core tasks still require physical sample collection, equipment installation and maintenance, and on-site monitoring in remote environments.

title: Geoscientists
old_rationale: Geoscientists have a balanced mix of physical and digital work. While AI is highly effective at analyzing seismic data, GIS mapping, and predictive modeling, the core of the job requires physical fieldwork, sample collection in remote environments, and laboratory testing that AI cannot perform. Consequently, AI will significantly enhance their analytical productivity but cannot replace the essential physical presence and real-world data gathering required for the profession.
new_rationale: Geoscientists have meaningful AI exposure because parts of the job involve analyzing data, interpreting imagery, using GIS/modeling software, and writing reports—all areas where AI can boost productivity. But the occupation also depends heavily on fieldwork, sample collection, laboratory testing, and physical presence in varied environments, which limits how much AI can replace the core role.

title: Glaziers
old_rationale: The core work of a glazier is highly physical, requiring manual dexterity, strength, and real-time problem-solving in unpredictable construction environments. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like blueprint analysis or precision measurements, the actual installation, cutting, and securing of glass in the physical world remain well beyond current AI and robotic capabilities.
new_rationale: Glaziers’ core work is highly physical and site-specific: measuring, cutting, lifting, positioning, and securing glass in buildings, often at heights and in changing jobsite conditions. AI may help with peripheral tasks like blueprint interpretation, scheduling, or estimating, but it does little to automate the hands-on installation work itself.

title: Graphic designers
old_rationale: Graphic design is a fundamentally digital occupation where core tasks—image generation, layout creation, and photo editing—are being rapidly transformed by generative AI. While high-level creative direction and client management remain human-centric, the automation of production tasks significantly increases individual productivity, likely reducing the total number of designers needed for routine commercial work.
new_rationale: Graphic design is overwhelmingly digital work: creating layouts, images, and visual concepts on software, often entirely from a computer. AI is rapidly improving at image generation, layout iteration, branding concepts, and production tasks, so it can both automate portions of the work and greatly increase each designer’s output, though client interaction, taste, and higher-level creative direction still keep exposure just below the maximum.

title: Grounds maintenance workers
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are almost entirely physical and performed in unpredictable outdoor environments, which provides a strong natural barrier against AI. While autonomous mowers or drones may assist with specific tasks, the majority of the work—such as planting, tree climbing, and complex manual maintenance—requires human dexterity and physical presence that AI cannot replicate.
new_rationale: This work is overwhelmingly physical, outdoor, and hands-on: mowing, planting, trimming, applying treatments, and operating equipment in varied real-world environments. AI may help marginally with scheduling, route planning, plant diagnostics, or autonomous equipment over time, but it does not strongly affect the core labor of maintaining landscapes.

title: Hand laborers and material movers
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are fundamentally physical, involving the manual movement, cleaning, and packing of heavy materials in unpredictable real-world environments. While AI can optimize logistics, routing, and inventory tracking, the physical labor itself is resistant to AI and requires advanced robotics rather than software-based intelligence to automate.
new_rationale: This work is centered on physical, hands-on tasks like lifting, moving, packing, loading, cleaning, and collecting materials in real-world environments, which limits direct AI automation. AI may improve routing, inventory tracking, warehouse coordination, and some automated handling systems, but the core job still depends heavily on human physical labor and on-site presence.

title: Hazardous materials removal workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor in unpredictable environments, such as scrubbing surfaces, constructing containment areas, and operating heavy machinery. While AI may assist with peripheral tasks like record-keeping, regulatory compliance checks, or analyzing sensor data for radiation levels, the primary work requires a physical human presence to navigate complex, hazardous sites.
new_rationale: This job is dominated by physical, on-site work in hazardous and unpredictable environments: testing materials, building containment, operating equipment, and removing waste while following strict safety procedures. AI may help a bit with documentation, compliance support, or planning, but it does not meaningfully automate the core hands-on cleanup and decontamination tasks.

title: Health and safety engineers
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant mix of digital knowledge work and physical presence. While AI can assist in reviewing equipment plans, analyzing accident data, and interpreting complex safety regulations, the core duties require physical inspections of facilities, interviewing employees for investigations, and directing the hands-on installation of safety devices.
new_rationale: Health and safety engineers do substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with, including reviewing regulations, analyzing plans and incident data, drafting reports, and creating training materials. But the job also depends heavily on site inspections, investigating real-world hazards, interviewing workers, and exercising accountable engineering judgment in physical environments, which limits full automation.

title: Health education specialists
old_rationale: A significant portion of the role involves digital knowledge work such as analyzing community health data, writing grant proposals, and creating educational materials, all of which are highly susceptible to AI augmentation. However, the core requirements of community advocacy, empathetic interpersonal interaction, and physical presence for local events and training provide a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: AI can meaningfully assist with core parts of this job such as creating educational materials, analyzing community data, drafting grant proposals, and evaluating programs. But much of the occupation still depends on in-person teaching, community outreach, relationship-building, empathy, and adapting to local human needs, which limits full automation.

title: Health information technologists and medical registrars
old_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital, involving the analysis, organization, and validation of clinical data within computerized systems. AI is exceptionally well-suited for core tasks like data abstraction, classification coding, and generating reports from large datasets, which significantly increases the risk of automation and restructuring.
new_rationale: This job is largely digital and computer-based: organizing clinical databases, validating records, generating reports, analyzing outcomes, and supporting health information systems are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. Human oversight remains important because of healthcare regulations, patient privacy, data integrity, and coordination with clinical staff, but AI is likely to significantly raise productivity and reshape the role.

title: Heating, air conditioning, and refrigeration mechanics and installers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, manual dexterity, and real-time problem solving in unpredictable physical environments, which are highly resistant to AI. While AI may assist with peripheral tasks like diagnostic software, scheduling, or energy load calculations, it cannot perform the physical installation, repair, or handling of hazardous materials required for the job.
new_rationale: HVAC work is centered on physical installation, inspection, repair, and maintenance at customer sites, often in cramped or hazardous environments, which creates a strong barrier to AI automation. AI may help with diagnostics, scheduling, recordkeeping, and interpreting data from computerized systems, but it does not replace the hands-on mechanical work, travel, and in-person customer interaction that define the job.

title: Heavy and tractor-trailer truck drivers
old_rationale: While the core task of driving is physical and occurs in a complex real-world environment, it is a primary target for autonomous vehicle AI, which could eventually automate long-haul highway segments. However, the job still requires significant physical presence for non-driving tasks like securing cargo, conducting inspections, and navigating 'last-mile' urban environments that remain challenging for current AI.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical and happens in the real world, requiring on-road vehicle control, cargo inspection, securing loads, and handling unpredictable traffic and weather. That limits near-term AI automation compared with fully digital jobs, but autonomous-driving systems, route optimization, and automated logging/dispatch could still significantly reshape long-haul trucking and reduce labor demand over time.

title: Heavy vehicle and mobile equipment service technicians
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is highly physical, involving manual dexterity, strength, and real-world troubleshooting in unpredictable environments like farms and construction sites. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic software and predictive maintenance logs, it cannot perform the physical disassembly, repair, and reassembly of heavy mechanical components.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical, hands-on inspection, disassembly, repair, and testing of large machinery in shops and field worksites, which creates a strong barrier to AI automation. AI will help with diagnostics, manuals, service records, and troubleshooting guidance, but it is unlikely to replace the manual skill, tool use, and on-site problem solving that define the occupation.

title: High school teachers
old_rationale: High school teachers are heavily exposed because core digital tasks like lesson planning, content creation, and grading are highly susceptible to AI automation and augmentation. While the physical requirement of classroom management and interpersonal mentorship provides a buffer, AI's ability to provide personalized tutoring and automated feedback significantly reshapes the instructional delivery and administrative workload of the profession.
new_rationale: High school teaching has meaningful AI exposure because lesson planning, content creation, grading support, parent communication, and individualized instructional materials are information-heavy tasks AI can assist with. But the core job still depends heavily on in-person classroom management, live instruction, student motivation, supervision, and relationship-building, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Historians
old_rationale: Historians perform high-level knowledge work—researching, analyzing, and writing—that is increasingly digital and highly susceptible to AI-driven efficiency gains in document processing and synthesis. While the job requires human judgment and physical interaction with artifacts or archives, the core output of reports and articles faces significant restructuring as AI tools become capable of parsing vast historical datasets and drafting complex narratives.
new_rationale: Historian work is heavily centered on digital and text-based tasks—researching sources, analyzing information, synthesizing findings, translating material, and writing reports or articles—all areas where AI is improving rapidly. Physical archive work, artifact handling, and nuanced interpretation of authenticity and historical significance still require human judgment, but AI is likely to substantially reshape workflows and reduce time needed for much of the core research and writing.

title: Home health and personal care aides
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are fundamentally physical and interpersonal, involving manual tasks like bathing, dressing, and lifting patients in unpredictable home environments. While AI may assist with peripheral administrative tasks like scheduling or monitoring vital signs, it cannot replace the essential human presence and physical labor required for personal care.
new_rationale: This job’s core tasks are highly physical and in-person: helping clients bathe, dress, move, eat, and manage daily life in home settings. AI may assist with peripheral documentation, scheduling, or monitoring, but it cannot meaningfully replace the hands-on care, emotional support, and real-time human presence central to the occupation.

title: Human resources managers
old_rationale: Human resources management is a digital-heavy knowledge occupation where AI can significantly automate recruitment screening, payroll processing, and regulatory compliance reporting. While the role requires high-level interpersonal skills for dispute mediation and strategic leadership, the vast majority of the administrative and analytical tasks are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and restructuring.
new_rationale: Human resources managers do predominantly computer-based knowledge work—screening candidates, drafting policies, analyzing workforce needs, compliance, and reporting—so AI can significantly automate and accelerate large parts of the job. But the role also depends heavily on interpersonal judgment, sensitive conflict resolution, leadership, and organizational trust, which limits full automation even as AI raises productivity and may reduce staffing needs in some HR functions.

title: Human resources specialists
old_rationale: The core tasks of recruiting, screening, and administrative record-keeping are highly digital and data-driven, making them prime targets for AI automation and augmentation. While the role requires significant interpersonal interaction and human judgment for interviewing and employee relations, AI can now handle complex scheduling, initial candidate vetting, and policy inquiries, significantly increasing individual worker productivity and reducing the need for entry-level administrative staff.
new_rationale: Human resources specialists do largely digital knowledge work—screening applicants, processing records, handling paperwork, answering policy questions, and coordinating hiring—so AI can automate or accelerate a significant share of the job. But hiring decisions, interviews, employee relations, compliance judgment, and trust-based communication still require substantial human involvement, keeping exposure high but not near-total.

title: Hydrologists
old_rationale: Hydrologists have a high exposure to AI because a significant portion of their work involves data analysis, computer modeling, and report writing—tasks where AI is rapidly advancing. However, the physical requirement of field work, such as collecting water samples and measuring streamflow in remote locations, provides a substantial buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Hydrologists combine substantial computer-based work—data analysis, modeling, forecasting, and report writing—with field sampling and on-site observation that AI cannot directly replace. AI is likely to significantly boost productivity in the digital parts of the job, but physical fieldwork, scientific judgment, and collaboration with engineers and public officials keep exposure below the very highest levels.

title: Industrial designers
old_rationale: Industrial design is a predominantly digital occupation where core tasks like sketching, 3D modeling, and rendering are being rapidly transformed by generative AI and advanced CAD automation. While the role requires physical prototyping and cross-functional collaboration, the vast majority of the design process is computer-based, making it highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and workflow restructuring.
new_rationale: Industrial designers do a large share of their work digitally—researching users, generating concepts, sketching/rendering, and building CAD models—so AI can substantially accelerate ideation, visualization, and iteration. But the occupation is not fully digital: physical prototypes, manufacturing constraints, materials, product testing, and collaboration with clients and engineers still require human judgment and real-world validation.

title: Industrial engineering technologists and technicians
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of digital work, such as analyzing production costs, using CAD/CAM software, and conducting statistical studies, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation. However, the role also requires physical presence on the manufacturing floor to observe workers, assess machinery performance, and evaluate physical layouts, which provides a partial buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: This job mixes digital analysis—studying workflows, preparing charts, interpreting drawings, and analyzing costs—with substantial on-site observation of manufacturing processes, equipment, and workers. AI can significantly assist with data analysis, scheduling, simulation, reporting, and process optimization, but the occupation still depends on physical-world context, plant-specific judgment, and collaboration on the factory floor.

title: Industrial engineers
old_rationale: Industrial engineering is heavily focused on data analysis, systems design, and process optimization—tasks where AI and machine learning excel. While the role requires physical site visits and human collaboration to understand workflows, the core work product is digital analysis and modeling, making the occupation highly susceptible to significant productivity gains and restructuring through AI-driven automation.
new_rationale: Industrial engineers do substantial knowledge work on a computer—analyzing process data, modeling systems, designing improvements, and presenting recommendations—so AI can significantly boost productivity in core tasks. But the job also depends on observing real-world operations, understanding plant- or service-specific constraints, and coordinating implementation with people on site, which limits full automation.

title: Industrial machinery mechanics, machinery maintenance workers, and millwrights
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, manual dexterity, and real-time troubleshooting in unpredictable physical environments, which provides a strong barrier against AI automation. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic capabilities and predictive maintenance scheduling, the actual repair, installation, and physical manipulation of heavy machinery still require human presence and mechanical skill.
new_rationale: This work is fundamentally hands-on and site-specific: inspecting, disassembling, repairing, calibrating, welding, and moving industrial machinery in factories and construction settings. AI can assist with diagnostics, predictive maintenance, manuals, and troubleshooting support, but it does not replace the core physical repair and installation tasks, and increased automation may actually increase demand for these workers.

title: Industrial production managers
old_rationale: This role is a hybrid of digital knowledge work and physical oversight. AI will significantly enhance the analytical aspects of the job, such as production scheduling, budget optimization, and predictive maintenance data analysis, but the core requirements of physical plant presence, personnel management, and real-time problem-solving in a manual environment provide a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: This job has substantial exposure because AI can assist with production planning, data analysis, scheduling, budgeting, quality monitoring, and reporting. But exposure is limited by the need for on-site plant oversight, safety management, real-time problem solving in physical operations, and leadership of workers across a manufacturing environment.

title: Information clerks
old_rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because its core tasks—processing routine data, answering inquiries, and maintaining records—are fundamentally digital and information-based. While some roles like hotel desk clerks or airport agents require a physical presence, the majority of the work involves routine information processing that is increasingly being automated by AI-driven self-service systems, chatbots, and automated data entry tools.
new_rationale: This occupation includes a large amount of routine information processing—recordkeeping, data collection, standard responses, order handling, and basic customer inquiries—that AI and self-service software can increasingly automate or speed up. Exposure is tempered by the many roles that still require in-person service, physical presence, or handling sensitive government and customer interactions, but overall AI is likely to significantly reduce headcount and reshape workflows.

title: Information security analysts
old_rationale: This occupation is entirely digital, involving tasks like monitoring networks, analyzing vulnerabilities, and writing reports that are highly susceptible to AI automation. While AI increases the demand for security to counter AI-driven threats, it also significantly automates the core functions of threat detection, log analysis, and incident response, drastically increasing individual worker productivity.
new_rationale: This job is almost entirely digital: monitoring systems, analyzing threats, writing reports, researching vulnerabilities, and recommending controls are all computer-based tasks where AI can already provide substantial assistance. However, cybersecurity is adversarial and high-stakes, so human judgment, real-time investigation, and accountability remain important, meaning AI is more likely to heavily augment and reshape the role than fully replace it.

title: Instructional coordinators
old_rationale: Instructional coordinators perform high-level knowledge work including curriculum development, data analysis, and educational material review, all of which are digital-first tasks highly susceptible to AI augmentation. While the role requires significant interpersonal interaction for teacher coaching and stakeholder management, the core 'product'—curricula and instructional strategies—can be generated and optimized much faster using generative AI and advanced analytics.
new_rationale: This job contains a large share of digital knowledge work—developing curriculum materials, analyzing test data, reviewing educational content, and recommending instructional methods—which AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. But instructional coordinators also rely on in-person observation, teacher coaching, training, and relationship-based change management in schools, which limits full automation.

title: Insulation workers
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are highly physical and require manual dexterity in unpredictable, confined environments like attics and crawl spaces. AI has minimal impact on the physical installation of materials, though it may slightly assist with peripheral tasks like blueprint analysis or material estimation.
new_rationale: Insulation work is overwhelmingly physical and site-specific: measuring, cutting, fitting, spraying, climbing, and working in confined or hazardous spaces. AI may help a bit with estimating materials, reading plans, or paperwork, but it does not directly replace the core hands-on installation work.

title: Insurance sales agents
old_rationale: The core tasks of analyzing policies, customizing insurance programs, and maintaining records are digital and data-driven, making them highly susceptible to AI automation and augmentation. While the role requires interpersonal trust and persuasive sales skills, AI-powered chatbots and recommendation engines are increasingly capable of handling routine inquiries and policy comparisons, significantly increasing individual agent productivity and reducing the need for human intervention in simple transactions.
new_rationale: Insurance sales agents do substantial information-processing work that AI can increasingly assist or automate: lead generation, policy comparison, client analysis, quote preparation, renewals, CRM updates, and explaining standard products. But the occupation still depends heavily on trust, persuasion, relationship-building, and licensed human judgment in client interactions, which keeps exposure below the most fully digital occupations.

title: Insurance underwriters
old_rationale: Underwriting is a fundamentally digital occupation centered on routine information processing, risk modeling, and data analysis—all areas where AI excels. The BLS already projects a decline in employment due to automation, and the shift from traditional software to advanced AI will likely automate even complex, non-standard risk assessments that previously required human judgment.
new_rationale: Insurance underwriting is highly exposed because the core work is digital information processing: analyzing applications, assessing risk, and setting coverage and premiums using software. The BLS description already notes that automated underwriting tools handle many routine cases and are expected to reduce employment further, leaving mainly more complex judgment-heavy cases for humans.

title: Interior designers
old_rationale: Interior design is heavily reliant on digital tools like CAD and BIM, which are being rapidly transformed by generative AI capable of creating photorealistic renderings and floor plans from text prompts. While the job requires physical site visits, material inspections, and complex interpersonal coordination with contractors, the core creative and technical drafting tasks are highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and productivity gains.
new_rationale: Interior design has a large digital component—creating plans, renderings, specifications, and documentation in CAD/BIM—where AI can already help with concept generation, layouts, visualization, material selection, and drafting. But the job also depends on site visits, coordination with contractors, code compliance, and nuanced client-facing aesthetic judgment, so AI is more likely to augment and compress staffing needs than fully replace the role.

title: Interpreters and translators
old_rationale: The core work product is purely digital information (text and speech) in a domain where Large Language Models already demonstrate near-human proficiency. While high-stakes interpreting (legal, medical) and sign language still require human presence and nuanced judgment, the vast majority of translation and routine interpreting tasks are being rapidly automated or shifted to 'post-editing' roles, significantly reducing the total human labor required per unit of work.
new_rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because a large share of the work—especially written translation and localization—is fully digital, and AI translation tools already automate or heavily assist core tasks through machine translation and post-editing. Exposure is somewhat lower than a 9 or 10 because live interpreting, legal/medical nuance, sign language, and high-stakes cultural judgment still require substantial human accuracy, presence, and trust.

title: Ironworkers
old_rationale: Ironworking is a highly physical occupation performed in unpredictable, outdoor environments and at great heights, which provides a significant natural barrier to AI and robotics. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like blueprint analysis or site scheduling, the core duties of manual welding, bolting, and balancing on structural beams require real-time human dexterity and physical presence that cannot be automated with current or near-future technology.
new_rationale: Ironworkers do hands-on, physical construction work in unpredictable outdoor environments, often at heights, using tools, machinery, and real-time coordination on jobsites. AI may help with peripheral tasks like blueprint interpretation, scheduling, safety monitoring, or fabrication planning, but it does not substantially automate the core installation, alignment, cutting, and fastening work.

title: Janitors and building cleaners
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are almost entirely physical, involving manual labor in unpredictable and varied environments like schools, hospitals, and construction sites. While AI might assist with minor administrative tasks like supply ordering or scheduling, the primary work of cleaning, disinfecting, and making physical repairs remains a significant barrier to AI and robotic automation.
new_rationale: This job is overwhelmingly physical and location-specific: cleaning buildings, handling trash, restocking supplies, and making minor repairs all require in-person manual work. AI may marginally help with scheduling, supply ordering, or more advanced cleaning equipment, but it does not strongly automate the core tasks in most settings.

title: Jewelers and precious stone and metal workers
old_rationale: The core of the job involves physical dexterity, manual repair, and hands-on craftsmanship that AI cannot replicate. However, AI and automation are significantly impacting the design and appraisal phases through generative CAD tools and automated gemstone grading, contributing to a projected decline in total employment.
new_rationale: This occupation is centered on hands-on physical craftsmanship: setting stones, soldering, polishing, repairing, and grading valuable items with fine manual dexterity. AI can affect design, appraisal documentation, pricing research, and digital modeling, and automation like CAD/CAM, 3D printing, and robotic polishing can raise productivity, but much of the core work still requires skilled physical manipulation and in-person handling of jewelry.

title: Judges and hearing officers
