old_rationale: The core tasks of researching legal issues, evaluating documents, and writing opinions are highly digital and susceptible to AI automation, which can significantly increase productivity and reduce the need for human staff. However, the role requires high-stakes human judgment, presiding over physical courtrooms, and maintaining public trust, which creates a significant barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: Judges and hearing officers do substantial knowledge work that AI can increasingly assist with: researching legal issues, reviewing briefs, summarizing records, and drafting opinions. But the core role also depends on human authority, procedural oversight, credibility judgments, and in-person courtroom or hearing management, which creates a meaningful barrier to full automation.

title: Kindergarten and elementary school teachers
old_rationale: AI will significantly automate back-end knowledge work such as lesson planning, grading, and administrative communication, which currently occupy a large portion of a teacher's time. However, the core of the role involves physical supervision, emotional support, and real-time behavioral management of young children, which creates a substantial barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: AI can meaningfully help with lesson planning, worksheet creation, grading, parent communications, and adapting materials for different student needs. But the core job still depends heavily on in-person classroom management, supervising children, social-emotional development, and real-time interaction with young students, which limits full automation.

title: Labor relations specialists
old_rationale: This occupation is predominantly digital and knowledge-based, involving the drafting of contracts, policy development, and legal compliance—all areas where AI excels. While the high-stakes interpersonal negotiation and dispute resolution provide a buffer, AI will significantly automate the research, document generation, and investigative analysis components of the role.
new_rationale: This job combines substantial digital knowledge work—drafting policies, reviewing agreements, analyzing compliance, preparing materials, and documenting cases—with high-stakes interpersonal negotiation and dispute resolution. AI can meaningfully automate research, writing, summarization, and case preparation, but in-person relationship management, trust-building, and judgment in tense labor-management situations keep exposure below the very highest levels.

title: Landscape architects
old_rationale: Landscape architecture is a predominantly digital knowledge-based occupation where core tasks like CADD modeling, site analysis, and cost estimation are highly susceptible to AI automation and enhancement. While the role requires physical site visits and interpersonal client management, the generative design capabilities of AI can significantly accelerate the creative and technical drafting phases, potentially reducing the total human hours required per project.
new_rationale: Landscape architects do substantial computer-based design, drafting, visualization, estimating, and environmental analysis work, all of which AI can increasingly assist or partially automate. But the job also depends on site visits, client collaboration, regulatory and environmental judgment, and coordinating real-world construction outcomes, which limits full automation.

title: Lawyers
old_rationale: Lawyers perform high-level knowledge work that is almost entirely digital, involving document drafting, legal research, and complex data analysis—all areas where LLMs excel. While courtroom advocacy and high-stakes interpersonal negotiation provide a protective barrier, the vast majority of billable hours are spent on tasks that AI can now significantly accelerate or automate, leading to major structural changes in the profession.
new_rationale: Lawyers do a large share of their core work in fully digital domains—researching law, analyzing facts, drafting documents, and communicating advice—where AI is improving rapidly and can already boost productivity substantially. But important parts of the job still rely on human judgment, client trust, negotiation, strategy, and in-person advocacy in courts and proceedings, which keeps exposure below the maximum.

title: Librarians and library media specialists
old_rationale: Librarianship is fundamentally about information retrieval, organization, and research—domains where LLMs and AI search engines excel. While the physical presence in schools and public libraries provides a buffer through community programming and interpersonal support, the core technical tasks of cataloging, database management, and reference assistance are highly exposed to AI automation and displacement.
new_rationale: Librarians do substantial information-search, classification, research-support, and database work that AI can increasingly assist or partially automate, especially for reference questions, discovery, and administrative tasks. But the occupation also depends on in-person patron service, teaching, community programming, collection stewardship, and institutional judgment, which creates meaningful limits on full automation.

title: Library technicians and assistants
old_rationale: A significant portion of this role involves routine information processing, cataloging, and answering reference questions, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation. While physical tasks like reshelving books and interpersonal community programming provide some protection, the core digital and clerical functions are increasingly being handled by automated systems and AI-driven search tools.
new_rationale: This job mixes physical, in-person tasks like reshelving materials, staffing service desks, and running programs with routine information work such as cataloging, clerical tasks, and answering basic reference questions. AI can automate or reduce much of the digital and administrative portion, but the physical handling of materials and face-to-face patron support create a meaningful barrier to full automation.

title: Licensed practical and licensed vocational nurses
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical tasks such as bathing, dressing, and wound care, which are currently beyond the reach of AI and robotics. However, AI will significantly impact the information-processing aspects of the job, including documenting patient care, monitoring vitals, and assisting with diagnostic reporting, leading to moderate overall exposure.
new_rationale: LPNs/LVNs do substantial hands-on, in-person care: checking vitals, changing bandages, helping patients bathe or dress, collecting samples, and responding to patient needs in real time. AI can meaningfully assist with documentation, recordkeeping, monitoring, and care coordination, but the core work depends on physical presence, human judgment, and bedside interaction.

title: Loan officers
old_rationale: The core tasks of loan officers—analyzing financial data, verifying documentation, and assessing risk—are fundamentally digital and highly susceptible to AI automation. While complex commercial loans and relationship-based mortgage sales still require human judgment and interpersonal skills, the rapid advancement of automated underwriting and AI-driven customer service significantly reduces the need for human intervention in routine loan processing.
new_rationale: Loan officers do substantial digital information-processing work—collecting documents, analyzing financial data, checking compliance, and making recommendations—which AI and automated underwriting can increasingly handle or accelerate. But the role also includes sales, client guidance, relationship-building, and final judgment on higher-stakes or complex loans, which keeps exposure high rather than near-total.

title: Lodging managers
old_rationale: Lodging managers perform a hybrid of digital knowledge work and physical site management. While AI can automate back-office tasks like revenue management, dynamic pricing, and staff scheduling, the core role requires physical inspections of facilities and high-stakes interpersonal conflict resolution with guests and staff that cannot be fully digitized.
new_rationale: Lodging managers have meaningful exposure because AI can automate or augment reservations, pricing, budgeting, forecasting, reporting, scheduling, and parts of guest communication. But the job still depends heavily on physical presence, staff supervision, inspections, and in-person problem resolution with guests, which limits full automation.

title: Logging workers
old_rationale: The core work is highly physical, involving the operation of heavy machinery and manual labor in unpredictable outdoor environments. While AI may improve peripheral tasks like log grading through computer vision or optimize harvest planning, the physical requirements of felling and transporting timber provide a strong barrier to AI replacement.
new_rationale: Logging work is overwhelmingly physical, outdoor, and safety-critical, involving chain saws, heavy machinery, rough terrain, and real-time coordination in unpredictable environments. AI may assist at the margins through equipment automation, log grading, or planning, but it does not substantially replace the core hands-on work of felling, moving, and handling timber.

title: Logisticians
old_rationale: Logisticians perform high-level knowledge work that is primarily digital, involving data analysis, forecasting, and supply chain optimization—tasks where AI excels. While the role requires human-to-human negotiation and real-world problem-solving for physical disruptions, the core functions of inventory management and route optimization are increasingly being automated or enhanced by sophisticated AI algorithms.
new_rationale: Logisticians do predominantly digital planning, forecasting, analysis, scheduling, and coordination work, all of which are increasingly supportable or partially automatable with AI. But the occupation is tied to real-world supply chains, supplier relationships, and exception handling in fast-moving physical operations, which keeps human judgment and cross-organizational coordination important.

title: Machinists and tool and die makers
old_rationale: The role involves a significant physical component, including manual dexterity, machine setup, and material handling in a factory environment. However, AI and advanced automation are increasingly capable of optimizing CNC programming, interpreting CAD designs, and monitoring machine health, which increases worker productivity and reduces the demand for traditional tool and die making skills.
new_rationale: This occupation is centered on physical, hands-on manufacturing work: setting up machines, handling materials, measuring tolerances, and operating in a shop environment, which limits direct AI automation. AI and automation can meaningfully affect the digital portions—CAD/CAM, CNC programming, process optimization, and inspection—and the BLS already notes technology-driven efficiency gains reducing demand for some tasks, but the core job still requires in-person mechanical skill and precision.

title: Management analysts
old_rationale: Management analysts perform high-level knowledge work—including data analysis, report writing, and financial modeling—that is highly susceptible to AI augmentation and automation. While the role requires significant interpersonal skills for client interviews and stakeholder management, the core 'back-office' tasks of synthesizing information and developing efficiency recommendations are increasingly being streamlined by AI tools.
new_rationale: Management analysts do predominantly digital knowledge work: gathering information, analyzing data, developing recommendations, and producing reports and presentations are all tasks where AI can significantly assist or partially automate. However, client interviews, onsite observation, relationship management, and persuading leaders to adopt changes still require substantial human judgment and interpersonal skill, keeping exposure below the maximum.

title: Manicurists and pedicurists
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves high-precision physical labor, tactile sensitivity, and real-time human interaction in a physical environment. While AI can assist with peripheral business tasks like scheduling or inventory management, the primary service of cleaning, shaping, and decorating nails remains a manual task that is difficult to automate with current robotics.
new_rationale: This job’s core tasks are highly physical and in-person: cleaning, shaping, polishing, and decorating nails with manual dexterity and direct client contact. AI may help with peripheral tasks like scheduling, marketing, design ideas, or inventory, but it does little to automate the main service itself.

title: Marine engineers and naval architects
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves digital design, complex mathematical modeling, and technical report writing, all of which are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and automation. While physical site visits to shipyards and offshore platforms provide a buffer, the shift toward AI-integrated CAD, automated performance testing, and generative design for hulls and machinery significantly reshapes the workflow.
new_rationale: This is largely engineering knowledge work: designing vessels and systems, producing drawings and schematics, analyzing performance, estimating costs, and writing technical reports are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with. But the occupation also depends on physical-world constraints, safety-critical judgment, regulatory compliance, and on-site testing/oversight in shipyards, coastal sites, or at sea, which limit full automation.

title: Market research analysts
old_rationale: The core duties of this role—data collection, statistical analysis, trend forecasting, and report generation—are fundamentally digital and align perfectly with AI's strengths in processing large datasets and generating insights. While human judgment is still needed for high-level strategy and client relationships, AI can now automate the vast majority of the technical and administrative tasks that define the daily work of an analyst.
new_rationale: This job is predominantly digital knowledge work: collecting data, analyzing trends, designing surveys, interpreting results, and producing reports and presentations. AI can already automate or greatly accelerate much of the research, statistical analysis, synthesis, and writing, though human judgment is still important for framing questions, validating insights, and advising clients.

title: Marriage and family therapists
old_rationale: While the work is increasingly digital via telehealth and involves significant information processing, the core value of therapy lies in human-to-human empathy, trust, and the navigation of complex interpersonal dynamics. AI will likely automate administrative tasks and provide diagnostic support, but the high-stakes emotional nature of family and marriage counseling provides a significant barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: Marriage and family therapists do substantial information-processing work—assessment, treatment planning, documentation, and coordination—where AI can assist and improve productivity. But the core job depends on high-trust human interaction, empathy, nuanced judgment, and live therapeutic relationships, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Masonry workers
old_rationale: Masonry is a highly physical trade performed in unpredictable outdoor environments that require manual dexterity, strength, and real-time sensory feedback. While AI and robotics may assist with blueprint analysis or specialized brick-laying machines in controlled settings, the core tasks of navigating muddy sites, handling heavy materials, and finishing surfaces remain resistant to digital automation.
new_rationale: Masonry work is overwhelmingly physical, site-based, and hands-on: lifting materials, laying brick or stone, finishing concrete, and adjusting to real-world conditions on construction sites. AI may help with peripheral tasks like estimating materials, reading plans, or scheduling, but it does little to automate the core manual craft, so overall exposure is low.

title: Massage therapists
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is highly physical, requiring manual manipulation of soft tissues and real-time human empathy in a physical setting. While AI can assist with peripheral administrative tasks like scheduling, marketing, and documentation, it cannot replace the tactile skill and physical presence required for massage therapy.
new_rationale: Massage therapy’s core work is highly physical, hands-on, and requires in-person human presence, touch, and real-time assessment of clients’ bodies. AI may help with peripheral tasks like scheduling, marketing, documentation, or treatment planning, but it does not meaningfully automate the central service itself.

title: Material moving machine operators
old_rationale: The core of the job is physical operation of heavy machinery in dynamic environments like warehouses and construction sites, which provides a buffer against full automation. However, AI-driven computer vision and autonomous navigation are increasingly being integrated into forklifts and conveyor systems, leading to moderate exposure as these technologies augment or replace human operators in structured settings.
new_rationale: This job is centered on physically operating machinery in real-world environments like warehouses, ports, and construction sites, which limits direct AI substitution compared with digital desk work. However, AI-enabled automation, sensors, scanners, and autonomous material-handling systems can increasingly take over parts of the core task and reduce labor demand, especially in structured settings such as warehouses.

title: Material recording clerks
old_rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because its core functions—tracking data, scheduling, and inventory reporting—are digital information-processing tasks that AI and automated systems excel at. While some physical presence is required to inspect goods or handle packages, the rapid integration of AI-driven logistics software, RFID technology, and computer vision for quality control is already leading to a projected decline in employment.
new_rationale: This job combines routine digital recordkeeping, scheduling, inventory tracking, and report compilation—tasks that AI and adjacent automation can increasingly handle or streamline. But many clerks also need physical presence in warehouses or plants to inspect goods, scan items, handle packages, and coordinate real-world material flow, which keeps exposure below the highest levels.

title: Materials engineers
old_rationale: Materials engineering is heavily reliant on digital simulation, data analysis, and computational modeling, areas where AI and machine learning are already accelerating material discovery and property prediction. While the role requires physical lab work and manufacturing oversight, a significant portion of the core value—designing alloys, analyzing failures, and writing technical reports—is increasingly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and automation.
new_rationale: Materials engineers do substantial knowledge work on computers—simulation, analysis, coding, reporting, and material selection—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity and assist with design, prediction, and documentation. But the occupation also depends heavily on physical testing, lab and factory work, real-world validation, and engineering judgment tied to manufacturing constraints, which limits full automation.

title: Mathematicians and statisticians
old_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital, involving data analysis, mathematical modeling, and coding—all domains where AI and Large Language Models excel. While high-level theoretical research and complex interdisciplinary communication require human judgment, AI can now automate significant portions of data cleaning, statistical testing, and code generation, drastically increasing individual productivity and restructuring the workflow.
new_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: defining data needs, designing studies, building models, analyzing results, writing code, and communicating findings are all tasks AI is rapidly improving at. Human judgment, methodological rigor, and domain expertise still matter, but AI is likely to significantly reshape workflows and increase output per worker.

title: Mechanical engineering technologists and technicians
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of digital knowledge work and physical labor. While AI can significantly automate digital tasks like 3D modeling, data analysis, and cost estimation, the role still requires a substantial physical presence for assembling parts, setting up vehicle crash tests, and operating machinery like lathes and grinders.
new_rationale: This occupation has substantial exposure because important parts of the work are digital and information-based: reviewing drawings, using 3D design software, analyzing test data, making calculations, and documenting recommendations. But exposure is capped by the hands-on physical side of the job—assembling parts, operating machinery, setting up tests, troubleshooting equipment, and working in factories or labs—which still requires human presence and manual skill.

title: Mechanical engineers
old_rationale: Mechanical engineering is heavily reliant on digital tools like CAD, simulations, and data analysis, all of which are being rapidly enhanced by AI to automate complex design iterations and predictive modeling. While the role requires physical prototyping, site visits, and hardware testing, the core intellectual work of designing and analyzing mechanical systems is increasingly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and automation.
new_rationale: Mechanical engineers do substantial digital knowledge work—CAD design, simulation, analysis, troubleshooting, and documentation—where AI can already boost productivity and may automate meaningful portions over time. But the occupation also depends on physical-world testing, prototyping, manufacturing oversight, and engineering judgment about real systems, which limits full automation.

title: Medical and health services managers
old_rationale: This role involves a high volume of digital knowledge work, such as budgeting, scheduling, and regulatory compliance, which are highly susceptible to AI optimization. However, the core responsibilities of recruiting, supervising staff, and navigating complex interpersonal relationships within a physical healthcare facility provide a significant buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: This job includes substantial digital knowledge work—budgeting, reporting, scheduling, compliance monitoring, record management, and analyzing healthcare metrics—where AI can significantly boost productivity. But the role also depends heavily on human leadership, staff supervision, cross-functional coordination, and on-site operational judgment in real healthcare settings, which limits full automation.

title: Medical assistants
old_rationale: Medical assistants perform a hybrid of physical and digital tasks; while AI can significantly automate administrative duties like scheduling, coding, and medical record entry, it cannot perform physical clinical tasks such as drawing blood, administering injections, or taking manual vital signs. The role requires a high degree of real-time human interaction and empathy in a physical clinical setting, which serves as a natural barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: Medical assistants have meaningful exposure because a substantial share of their administrative work—scheduling, documentation, insurance forms, coding, and record entry—is digital and can be assisted or partly automated by AI. But the occupation also includes many core in-person clinical tasks like taking vital signs, drawing blood, giving injections, preparing specimens, and assisting with exams, which require physical presence and keep overall exposure moderate.

title: Medical dosimetrists
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves complex mathematical calculations, image analysis (CT/MRI), and digital treatment planning, all of which are domains where AI and machine learning excel. While there is a minor physical component involving patient positioning and equipment calibration, the vast majority of the work is digital information processing that is highly susceptible to automation and AI-driven optimization.
new_rationale: Medical dosimetrists do highly specialized computer-based work: reviewing scans, calculating radiation doses, and designing treatment plans, all of which are strong targets for AI assistance and partial automation. But the job remains constrained by clinical oversight, safety-critical judgment, regulatory requirements, equipment QA, and coordination with the oncology team, so AI is more likely to augment and raise productivity than fully replace the role soon.

title: Medical equipment repairers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is physical and manual, requiring dexterity to handle tools, disassemble machinery, and work in tight physical spaces. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic software, troubleshooting, and predictive maintenance scheduling, the physical act of repairing, installing, and calibrating hardware remains a human-centric task that cannot be automated by digital AI alone.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is hands-on: installing, diagnosing, calibrating, and physically repairing complex medical equipment on site in hospitals and clinics. AI can help with manuals, troubleshooting support, documentation, software updates, and diagnostics, but it does not replace the need for physical presence, tool use, regulatory checks, and real-world repair work.

title: Medical records specialists
old_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital, involving the processing, classification, and entry of data into electronic systems. AI is already highly capable of natural language processing and automated medical coding, which directly automates the core tasks of reviewing records and assigning clinical codes, significantly reducing the need for human intervention.
new_rationale: This job is almost entirely digital, centered on reviewing documentation, entering data, assigning standardized codes, and managing records in EHR systems—all tasks in domains where AI is advancing rapidly. Human oversight, compliance, and occasional clarification with clinicians still matter, but the core work is highly susceptible to automation and major productivity gains from AI.

title: Medical scientists
old_rationale: Medical scientists perform high-level knowledge work including data analysis, grant writing, and literature reviews, all of which are highly susceptible to AI augmentation and automation. While physical laboratory work and clinical trials provide a buffer, the core of the profession is shifting toward computational biology and AI-driven drug discovery, significantly increasing productivity and reshaping research methodologies.
new_rationale: Medical scientists do substantial computer-based work—analyzing data, reviewing literature, designing studies, writing grants, and publishing findings—so AI can significantly boost productivity in important parts of the job. But core research still depends heavily on physical lab work, clinical trials, handling biological samples, and high-stakes scientific judgment, which limits full automation.

title: Medical transcriptionists
old_rationale: Medical transcription is a purely digital, routine information-processing task that has already been heavily impacted by speech recognition. Modern AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) can now handle complex medical terminology, formatting, and summarization with high accuracy, transforming the human role into a diminishing oversight function or eliminating it entirely.
new_rationale: This job is almost entirely digital, consisting of converting, editing, and entering dictated medical information into standardized records on a computer. AI speech recognition and natural language processing already perform much of the core work today, with humans increasingly limited to review and error correction, which is why the occupation is already declining.

title: Meeting, convention, and event planners
old_rationale: A significant portion of the role involves digital tasks such as logistics planning, vendor research, contract review, and communications, which are highly susceptible to AI automation and productivity gains. However, the core of the job requires physical site inspections, real-time on-site problem solving during events, and high-stakes interpersonal negotiation and relationship management, which provide a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: AI can assist significantly with the digital parts of this job—drafting communications, comparing venues and vendors, building schedules and budgets, and analyzing attendee feedback. But the occupation still depends heavily on in-person site visits, live event coordination, vendor/client relationship management, and real-time problem solving in physical settings, which limits full automation.

title: Metal and plastic machine workers
old_rationale: The core of the work is physical and requires presence on a factory floor to handle materials and maintain machinery. However, AI and advanced automation are increasingly capable of handling the digital aspects of the job, such as CNC programming, machine monitoring, and quality inspection, which is already contributing to a projected decline in employment.
new_rationale: This work is centered on physically setting up, loading, monitoring, and maintaining industrial machines on a factory floor, which limits direct substitution by AI alone. However, the occupation is meaningfully exposed through automation, CNC programming, robotics, and AI-enabled process control that let fewer workers oversee more machines and reduce demand for many routine operator roles.

title: Microbiologists
old_rationale: Microbiologists have a high exposure to AI because a significant portion of their work involves data analysis, pattern recognition in genetic sequences, and technical reporting—all areas where AI is rapidly advancing. However, the physical requirement to conduct laboratory experiments, maintain biological cultures, and collect field samples provides a substantial buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Microbiologists do meaningful computer-based work—analyzing data, writing reports, reviewing literature, and preparing grant applications—so AI can significantly assist with the information-processing parts of the job. But the core occupation still depends heavily on physical laboratory experiments, specimen handling, specialized equipment, and scientific judgment in real-world research settings, which limits full automation.

title: Middle school teachers
old_rationale: Middle school teachers have high exposure because a significant portion of their workload—lesson planning, grading, and content creation—is digital and highly susceptible to AI automation. However, the core of the job requires physical presence for classroom management, emotional support for adolescents, and real-time interpersonal interaction, which provides a substantial buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: AI can meaningfully assist with lesson planning, creating materials, grading some assignments, test preparation, and parent communications, which raises productivity in the knowledge-work parts of teaching. But the core of the job still depends on in-person classroom management, motivating adolescents, adapting in real time to student behavior and needs, and providing human supervision, so AI is more likely to augment than replace most middle school teachers.

title: Military careers
old_rationale: Military careers are a vast composite of physical combat, manual labor, and high-level digital strategy. While AI will heavily automate back-office administration, logistics, and intelligence analysis (high exposure), the core requirements of physical presence, tactical human leadership, and operation in unpredictable physical environments provide a significant buffer.
new_rationale: Military careers span many specialties, but the occupation overall is grounded in physical presence, operational readiness, leadership, and work in dangerous real-world environments that AI cannot replace directly. AI will increasingly affect planning, intelligence, logistics, administration, and some technical roles, but much of the core work still requires human judgment, command responsibility, teamwork, and on-site action.

title: Mining and geological engineers
old_rationale: Mining and geological engineering involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, including mine design, technical reporting, and data analysis, which are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains. However, the role also requires substantial physical presence for site evaluations, safety inspections, and supervising construction in remote, unpredictable environments, which provides a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Mining and geological engineers do substantial analytical and design work on computers—modeling mines, evaluating sites, monitoring production, and writing technical reports—so AI can assist meaningfully with planning, analysis, and documentation. But the occupation also depends heavily on site-specific physical conditions, field inspections, safety oversight, and coordination with operations in real mining environments, which limits full automation.

title: Models
old_rationale: While runway and live event modeling require physical presence, the majority of modeling work results in digital or print imagery which is highly vulnerable to AI-generated synthetic media. AI can now create hyper-realistic virtual models or 'digital twins' of existing models, allowing brands to showcase clothing and products without physical photo shoots, significantly reducing the demand for human labor in commercial and catalog sectors.
new_rationale: Modeling depends heavily on physical appearance, in-person photo shoots, runway shows, and live brand representation, which limits full automation. But AI-generated imagery and image reuse can directly reduce demand for human models in digital advertising and other visual media, creating meaningful indirect exposure.

title: Music directors and composers
old_rationale: The occupation is a hybrid of high-exposure digital work (composing) and lower-exposure physical work (conducting). Generative AI is rapidly automating music composition, arrangement, and scoring for commercial media, while the music director role remains more resilient due to the need for live leadership, physical rehearsals, and human-to-human artistic interpretation.
new_rationale: AI is increasingly capable of generating, arranging, and editing music, so the composing side of this occupation faces meaningful exposure, especially for routine commercial or background music work done digitally. But a large share of the job—conducting ensembles, directing rehearsals, giving artistic feedback, auditioning performers, and leading live performances—depends on in-person leadership, musical interpretation, and real-time human coordination.

title: Musicians and singers
old_rationale: While AI can generate high-quality digital music and vocals, a significant portion of this occupation involves live performance, physical presence at venues, and human-centric events like religious services and concerts. The 'human element' and the physical act of playing an instrument or singing in real-time provide a natural barrier, though AI will increasingly compete for studio/session work and background music production.
new_rationale: This job’s core value is embodied live performance, rehearsing, touring, and audience connection, which limits direct automation by AI. But AI is increasingly relevant for recorded music production, background/session work, promotion, and even generating music itself, so parts of the occupation—especially its digital and commercial side—face meaningful disruption.

title: Natural sciences managers
old_rationale: This role is predominantly knowledge-based, involving data analysis, budgeting, and technical reporting, all of which are highly susceptible to AI enhancement and automation. While the job requires significant human-centric leadership and physical oversight of laboratories, AI will drastically increase productivity in reviewing research, drafting operational reports, and optimizing resource allocation.
new_rationale: Natural sciences managers do substantial digital knowledge work—planning, budgeting, reviewing research, reporting, and communicating—so AI can meaningfully boost productivity in many core tasks. But the occupation also depends on leadership, scientific judgment, cross-functional coordination, hiring, and oversight of labs or fieldwork, which keeps exposure below the very highest levels.

title: Network and computer systems administrators
old_rationale: This occupation is primarily digital and involves tasks like configuration, monitoring, and troubleshooting that are highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and 'infrastructure as code' practices. While some physical hardware maintenance remains, the shift toward cloud computing and AI-powered network management tools is already leading to a projected decline in employment as fewer workers can manage larger, more complex systems.
new_rationale: This is predominantly digital work—configuring systems, monitoring performance, managing permissions, troubleshooting, and maintaining security—so AI can increasingly automate or accelerate a large share of the core tasks. Exposure is not maximal because the job still involves responsibility for real-world infrastructure, outage response, environment-specific judgment, and occasional hands-on hardware/network work, but AI and automation are likely to significantly reduce labor needed per system administered.

title: News analysts, reporters, and journalists
old_rationale: The core work product is digital (text, scripts, and multimedia), and AI can already automate significant portions of research, drafting, and editing. While physical presence for field reporting and the cultivation of human sources provide a protective barrier, the overall occupation faces high exposure as AI tools drastically increase individual productivity, potentially leading to further industry consolidation and fewer required roles.
new_rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because much of its core work is digital information processing: researching, analyzing, writing, editing, summarizing, and producing content for multiple platforms. AI can already assist substantially with drafting, background research, transcription, headline generation, and story adaptation, though in-person reporting, source development, interviews, and on-the-ground judgment keep it from being near the maximum.

title: Nuclear engineers
old_rationale: Nuclear engineering is a high-level knowledge profession where core tasks—such as reactor design, dose calculations, and safety simulations—are performed using digital tools and complex mathematical modeling. While the high-stakes regulatory environment and the need for physical inspections at power plants provide a buffer, AI is rapidly advancing in its ability to optimize designs, analyze sensor data for predictive maintenance, and automate technical documentation.
new_rationale: Nuclear engineering is heavily knowledge-based and computer-centered: modeling, analysis, documentation, compliance review, and technical design are all areas where AI can substantially assist and boost productivity. But the occupation also involves high-stakes safety judgment, regulatory accountability, experiments, and oversight of physical facilities, which creates an important barrier to full automation.

title: Nuclear medicine technologists
old_rationale: The role requires significant physical presence to prepare and administer radiopharmaceuticals, operate heavy machinery, and physically assist or position patients. While AI will likely automate the digital aspects of the job—such as optimizing image quality, calculating dosages, and streamlining record-keeping—the core clinical and manual tasks remain insulated from full automation.
new_rationale: This job has some digital and technical components—operating imaging equipment, keeping records, and assessing procedures—where AI can assist with workflow, documentation, and decision support. But the core work requires in-person patient care, administering radiopharmaceuticals, operating specialized equipment, and following strict physical safety protocols, which creates a substantial barrier to automation.

title: Nuclear technicians
old_rationale: Nuclear technicians perform a mix of digital monitoring and physical labor, such as collecting environmental samples and maintaining hardware. While AI can significantly automate the data analysis, radiation calculation, and system monitoring aspects of the role, the requirement for physical presence in hazardous environments and the manual calibration of equipment provide a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Nuclear technicians do some digital monitoring, calculations, and documentation that AI can assist with, especially in anomaly detection, analysis, and reporting. But the core job also requires on-site equipment operation, physical inspections, radiation measurement, safety compliance, and incident response in tightly regulated environments, which limits full automation.

title: Nurse anesthetists, nurse midwives, and nurse practitioners
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of high-level knowledge work, such as diagnosing conditions and analyzing test results, which is highly susceptible to AI assistance. However, the core of the job requires physical presence for exams, procedures (like delivering babies or administering anesthesia), and complex interpersonal communication that provides a strong buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: AI can meaningfully assist APRNs with documentation, test interpretation, decision support, and care planning, so the information-processing parts of the job are moderately exposed. But the core work still depends heavily on physical exams, procedures, bedside monitoring, patient counseling, and real-time clinical judgment in high-stakes settings, which limits full automation.

title: Nursing assistants and orderlies
old_rationale: The core duties of nursing assistants and orderlies—such as bathing, dressing, and physically transferring patients—are highly manual and require real-time human presence in unpredictable physical environments. While AI may assist with peripheral tasks like recording vital signs or scheduling, the primary work product is physical care and interpersonal support, which remains largely insulated from AI automation.
new_rationale: This work is centered on hands-on physical care: bathing, feeding, lifting, repositioning, transporting patients, cleaning, and responding to people in person. AI may help a bit with documentation, monitoring, or scheduling, but it does not meaningfully automate the core bedside and facility-based tasks, so overall exposure is low.

title: Occupational health and safety specialists and technicians
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical fieldwork and digital knowledge work. While AI can significantly automate data analysis, report writing, and regulatory cross-referencing, the core duties require physical inspections of worksites, manual testing with specialized equipment, and real-time human interaction to conduct safety trainings and emergency investigations.
new_rationale: AI can help meaningfully with the desk-based parts of this job—analyzing incident data, interpreting regulations, generating reports, and creating training materials. But the core role still depends heavily on in-person inspections, fieldwork in hazardous environments, observing worksites directly, and enforcing safety practices on site, which limits full automation.

title: Occupational therapists
old_rationale: Occupational therapy is a highly physical and interpersonal profession that requires real-time observation, manual manipulation of patients, and the assessment of physical environments like homes and workplaces. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks such as treatment planning, progress documentation, and medical history analysis, the core of the job involves hands-on rehabilitation and emotional support that cannot be replicated digitally.
new_rationale: Occupational therapists’ core work is hands-on, in-person care: evaluating patients, demonstrating exercises, adapting environments, and building rapport with clients and families. AI can assist with documentation, treatment planning, and administrative tasks, but it is unlikely to replace the physical presence, clinical observation, and interpersonal coaching central to the job.

title: Occupational therapy assistants and aides
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical assistance, manual therapy, and real-time human interaction, such as lifting patients and guiding motor skill exercises, which AI cannot perform. While AI can streamline administrative tasks like scheduling, documentation, and billing, the primary value of the role is rooted in physical presence and empathetic interpersonal support.
new_rationale: Most core duties are hands-on, in-person patient care: guiding therapeutic activities, moving clients, setting up equipment, and observing progress in real time. AI may help with documentation, scheduling, billing forms, and some treatment-planning support, but it does not replace the physical assistance, interpersonal coaching, and clinical presence central to the job.

title: Oil and gas workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves heavy physical labor, equipment maintenance, and manual operation in unpredictable outdoor environments, which provides a strong buffer against AI. However, the industry is increasingly adopting automated drilling technologies and remote monitoring systems that improve productivity, meaning fewer human workers may be required to achieve the same output.
new_rationale: This work is overwhelmingly physical, site-based, and equipment-focused, often in hazardous outdoor or offshore environments where human presence, manual skill, and crew coordination are essential. AI may reduce some labor indirectly through automated drilling, robotics, and remote monitoring, but it does not strongly automate the core hands-on tasks of operating, moving, repairing, and maintaining oilfield equipment.

title: Operations research analysts
old_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital, involving data collection, mathematical modeling, and report writing—all areas where AI and Large Language Models excel. While analysts still provide high-level human judgment and stakeholder persuasion, AI can now automate the core technical tasks of coding analytical tools, mining data, and generating optimization simulations, significantly increasing individual productivity and restructuring the role.
new_rationale: This job is almost entirely digital: collecting and analyzing data, building quantitative models, using analytical software, and writing reports and recommendations. AI is rapidly improving in exactly these domains, so it can automate substantial portions of the work and greatly increase each analyst’s productivity, though human judgment and stakeholder persuasion still matter.

title: Opticians
old_rationale: The role involves a significant amount of physical, hands-on work such as measuring faces, adjusting frames with tools, and teaching patients how to insert contact lenses. While AI can automate administrative tasks and assist in frame selection via virtual try-on technology, the core requirements of physical fitting, manual repairs, and real-time interpersonal healthcare guidance provide a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Opticians’ core work is hands-on and customer-facing: measuring faces, fitting and adjusting eyewear, teaching contact lens use, and helping people choose products in person. AI may help with peripheral tasks like inventory, records, sales support, and online frame recommendations, but it does not strongly automate the physical fitting and service components.

title: Optometrists
old_rationale: Optometry is a hybrid of physical clinical procedures and high-level data analysis. AI is highly effective at analyzing retinal scans and diagnostic data to identify diseases, which will significantly augment the diagnostic process; however, the core role requires physical presence for eye exams, minor surgical procedures, and the fitting of corrective lenses, which provides a strong buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Optometrists’ core work depends on in-person eye exams, use of specialized equipment, clinical judgment, and direct patient care, which limits full automation. AI can meaningfully assist with image analysis, screening, documentation, and prescription support, but it is more likely to augment productivity than replace the occupation’s hands-on clinical role.

title: Orthotists and prosthetists
old_rationale: This occupation involves a significant amount of physical, hands-on work including patient examinations, manual measurements, and the physical fabrication or adjustment of medical devices. While AI and computer-aided design (CAD) can greatly enhance the diagnostic and design phases, the core requirements of real-time human interaction, physical dexterity, and clinical judgment in a healthcare setting provide a strong barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: This job has some AI exposure in design, measurement analysis, documentation, and possibly computer-aided customization of devices. But the core work remains heavily physical and patient-facing: evaluating bodies in person, fitting and adjusting devices, using hands-on fabrication tools, and working directly with patients and care teams.

title: Painters, construction and maintenance
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are entirely physical, requiring manual dexterity, mobility at heights, and real-time adaptation to unpredictable physical environments. While AI might assist with peripheral business tasks like estimating or scheduling, it cannot perform the labor-intensive tasks of surface preparation and paint application.
new_rationale: This job’s core tasks are physical and on-site: preparing surfaces, climbing ladders, handling tools, and applying coatings in real-world environments. AI may help with peripheral work like estimating materials, scheduling, or color selection, but it does little to automate the hands-on painting itself.

title: Painting and coating workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, manual dexterity, and presence in industrial or repair environments, which provides a natural barrier to AI. While AI and computer vision can enhance automated spray systems and quality control sensors, the need for human workers to set up equipment, prepare surfaces, and perform manual touch-ups in unpredictable repair settings remains significant.
new_rationale: This job is centered on physical, in-person work: preparing surfaces, operating spray/coating equipment, monitoring finishes, and doing manual touch-ups in factory or repair settings. AI may modestly increase automation through smarter machine control, inspection, and process optimization, but the core work still depends heavily on hands-on operation in the physical world.

title: Paralegals and legal assistants
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation—legal research, document drafting, and information organization—are fundamentally digital and align perfectly with the strengths of Large Language Models. While some interpersonal and courtroom logistics remain, AI can already automate or significantly accelerate the vast majority of a paralegal's workload, leading to the BLS's projection of 0% job growth despite high demand for legal services.
new_rationale: This job is overwhelmingly digital and document-based: legal research, drafting, organizing files, reviewing transcripts, and coordinating case materials are all core tasks where AI is rapidly improving. Some in-person trial support and client interaction remain, but AI can substantially automate or accelerate much of the occupation’s main work, likely reducing labor demand per case.

title: Personal financial advisors
old_rationale: The core technical tasks of this role—analyzing market data, optimizing portfolios, and tax planning—are digital and highly susceptible to AI automation, as evidenced by the rise of robo-advisors. However, the occupation is protected by a significant interpersonal component, as clients often require human trust, emotional reassurance during market volatility, and complex ethical judgment that AI cannot yet fully replicate.
new_rationale: Personal financial advisors do substantial digital knowledge work—analyzing portfolios, researching investments, modeling scenarios, and communicating recommendations—so AI can automate or greatly accelerate much of the core information-processing work. But the occupation also depends heavily on trust, relationship-building, sales, and nuanced client judgment around life goals and risk tolerance, which should keep humans important even as AI raises productivity and reshapes the role.

title: Pest control workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is highly physical, requiring workers to crawl into tight spaces, set manual traps, and apply chemicals in unpredictable real-world environments. While AI may assist with peripheral tasks like route optimization, pest identification from photos, or automated billing, the primary labor cannot be performed digitally or remotely.
new_rationale: This job is dominated by in-person physical work: inspecting buildings, crawling into tight spaces, applying treatments, setting traps, driving to sites, and interacting with customers on location. AI may help with scheduling, recordkeeping, pricing, or pest identification, but it is unlikely to automate the core hands-on fieldwork.

title: Petroleum engineers
old_rationale: Petroleum engineering involves significant digital knowledge work, such as reservoir modeling, data analysis, and drilling simulation, which are highly susceptible to AI-driven optimization and automation. However, the role remains anchored by the necessity of physical site visits to remote or offshore locations to oversee equipment installation and troubleshoot real-world mechanical issues that AI cannot physically address.
new_rationale: Petroleum engineering includes substantial computer-based analysis, modeling, design, and technical planning, so AI can meaningfully assist with reservoir analysis, drilling plans, optimization, and documentation. But the job is still tightly tied to physical operations, field conditions, safety, equipment, and site coordination, which limits full automation and keeps human engineering judgment and on-site oversight central.

title: Pharmacists
old_rationale: Pharmacists face moderate exposure because their core knowledge-based tasks—checking drug interactions, verifying dosages, and analyzing patient records—are highly susceptible to AI automation and digital optimization. However, the role remains anchored by physical requirements such as administering immunizations, performing clinical rounds, and providing real-time, empathetic patient counseling that requires human trust and physical presence.
new_rationale: Pharmacists have meaningful exposure because medication review, drug interaction checks, documentation, insurance coordination, and patient information tasks are information-heavy and increasingly AI-assisted. But the occupation still depends heavily on licensed human judgment, legal accountability, patient counseling, supervision, and in-person clinical/retail work such as dispensing and vaccinations, which limits full automation.

title: Pharmacy technicians
old_rationale: The role involves a significant amount of physical labor, such as measuring medications, packaging prescriptions, and interacting with patients in person, which provides a buffer against full automation. However, AI and automated dispensing systems are highly capable of handling the information-processing tasks like insurance claims, inventory management, and prescription data entry, which constitute a large portion of the daily workload.
new_rationale: Pharmacy technicians have meaningful exposure because parts of the job involve routine information processing: entering patient data, processing insurance claims, managing inventory, and handling basic customer communications. But core work still requires physical presence, medication handling, packaging, hospital/retail workflow, and close supervision in a regulated safety-critical environment, which limits full AI replacement.

title: Phlebotomists
old_rationale: The core of the job is a highly tactile, physical procedure (venipuncture) performed on human patients in unpredictable clinical settings, which is resistant to AI automation. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like data entry, sample tracking, and scheduling, the primary value of the role lies in manual dexterity and providing interpersonal emotional support to anxious patients.
new_rationale: Phlebotomy’s core tasks—drawing blood, handling specimens, maintaining sterile equipment, and calming patients in person—require physical dexterity and real-world human interaction, which limits direct AI automation. AI may help with peripheral work like data entry, labeling checks, scheduling, and documentation, but it does not substantially replace the hands-on clinical role.

title: Photographers
old_rationale: Photography is a hybrid role where the physical act of capturing real-world events (weddings, news, portraits) provides a buffer, but the digital output is highly vulnerable to AI. Generative AI can now create high-quality commercial and stock imagery from scratch and automate complex post-production tasks, significantly reducing the billable hours and demand for professional photographers in commercial and creative sectors.
new_rationale: Photography has meaningful AI exposure because image editing, enhancement, archiving, marketing, and some commercial image creation are digital tasks that AI can increasingly automate or compress. But the occupation still relies heavily on physical presence, equipment handling, real-world image capture, and client interaction—especially for weddings, portraits, events, and on-location shoots—so AI is more likely to reshape parts of the job than fully replace it.

title: Physical therapist assistants and aides
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical, hands-on work such as massage, stretching, and assisting patients with mobility, which AI cannot perform. While AI can streamline peripheral tasks like scheduling, documentation, and exercise plan generation, the requirement for real-time human presence and physical dexterity creates a strong barrier to automation.
new_rationale: Most core tasks are hands-on and in-person: guiding exercises, moving patients, using equipment, cleaning treatment areas, and observing physical progress. AI may help with documentation, scheduling, insurance forms, and treatment planning support, but it is unlikely to replace the physical, interpersonal, and safety-critical parts of the job.

title: Physical therapists
old_rationale: The core of physical therapy involves manual manipulation, physical assistance, and real-time observation of human movement in a physical environment, which AI cannot replicate. While AI can assist with administrative tasks, medical history review, and generating personalized exercise plans, the high level of physical dexterity and interpersonal empathy required limits its overall exposure.
new_rationale: Physical therapists’ core work is hands-on, in-person, and highly physical: observing movement, manipulating patients, guiding exercises, and building trust during treatment. AI can assist with documentation, treatment planning, and reviewing records, but it is unlikely to replace the real-time physical care and patient interaction at the center of the job.

title: Physician assistants
old_rationale: Physician assistants perform a mix of high-level knowledge work and physical clinical tasks. While AI will significantly augment diagnostic accuracy, treatment planning, and administrative documentation, the role requires physical presence for examinations, surgical procedures, and hands-on treatments like stitching wounds, as well as complex interpersonal counseling that is difficult to automate.
new_rationale: Physician assistants do significant in-person clinical work—examining patients, performing procedures, prescribing, and coordinating care—which creates a strong barrier to full AI replacement. But AI can meaningfully reshape the job by assisting with documentation, history-taking, diagnostic support, test interpretation, and treatment planning, increasing productivity in the information-processing parts of care.

title: Physicians and surgeons
old_rationale: Physicians and surgeons have moderate exposure because while AI can significantly automate digital tasks like diagnostic image interpretation (radiology), medical history analysis, and treatment planning, the core of the job requires physical presence, manual dexterity for procedures, and complex interpersonal communication. The high-stakes nature of medical liability and the need for human empathy in patient care provide a natural barrier to full automation, though AI will drastically increase productivity in information-heavy specialties.
new_rationale: AI will significantly assist physicians with documentation, image and test interpretation, decision support, and treatment planning, especially in more information-heavy specialties like radiology and pathology. But the occupation overall still depends heavily on in-person exams, procedures and surgery, real-time judgment under uncertainty, patient trust, and legal accountability, which limit full automation.

title: Physicists and astronomers
old_rationale: Physicists and astronomers perform high-level knowledge work that is increasingly digital, including data analysis, mathematical modeling, and software development. While AI significantly accelerates data processing and literature review, the core requirements of original theoretical innovation, experimental design, and complex physical-world problem solving provide a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Physicists and astronomers do a large share of their work in highly digital domains—mathematical modeling, coding, data analysis, paper writing, and grant writing—where AI can already provide substantial assistance and boost productivity. But the occupation also depends on original scientific judgment, experimental design, collaboration, and in many cases specialized physical equipment or observations, which limits full automation of the core role.

title: Plumbers, pipefitters, and steamfitters
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are highly physical, requiring manual dexterity, strength, and real-time problem-solving in unpredictable physical environments like crawl spaces and construction sites. While AI may assist with peripheral tasks like cost estimation, blueprint analysis via BIM, and scheduling, it cannot perform the essential hands-on installation and repair work.
new_rationale: This job’s core tasks are physical, on-site, and manual: installing, repairing, testing, and troubleshooting piping systems in homes, buildings, and industrial settings. AI may help with peripheral work like estimates, blueprint interpretation, scheduling, or BIM-assisted planning, but it does not substantially automate the hands-on trade work that defines the occupation.

title: Podiatrists
old_rationale: Podiatry is a medical specialty that requires significant physical presence for exams, manual procedures, and complex surgeries that AI cannot perform. However, AI will heavily impact the knowledge-based aspects of the role, such as analyzing X-rays, diagnosing conditions from medical history, and automating clinical documentation and administrative tasks.
new_rationale: Podiatrists do substantial knowledge work in diagnosis, imaging review, documentation, and treatment planning, all of which AI can increasingly assist. But the core of the occupation still depends heavily on in-person physical exams, procedures, surgery, and direct patient care, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Police and detectives
old_rationale: The core of the job involves physical presence, emergency response, and manual tasks like making arrests and patrolling, which are resistant to AI. However, a significant portion of the work involves digital tasks such as writing detailed reports, searching databases, and analyzing evidence, all of which are highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and efficiency gains.
new_rationale: Police and detective work is centered on physical presence, field response, patrol, arrests, evidence collection, and real-time judgment in unpredictable situations, which creates a strong barrier to direct AI automation. AI can still meaningfully affect paperwork, report writing, record searches, case preparation, and some investigative analysis, but it is unlikely to replace the core in-person enforcement role.

title: Political scientists
old_rationale: Political science is a purely digital knowledge-work occupation centered on data analysis, research, and writing, all of which are core strengths of generative AI and LLMs. While high-level strategic judgment and interpersonal networking remain human-centric, AI can now automate the collection of public opinion data, the drafting of policy briefs, and the forecasting of political trends, leading to significant productivity gains and a projected decline in total employment.
new_rationale: This is predominantly digital knowledge work: researching, analyzing survey and policy data, forecasting trends, and writing/presenting findings are all tasks AI can increasingly assist with or partially automate. Human judgment, domain expertise, and credibility still matter for interpreting complex political contexts and shaping research agendas, but the occupation is highly exposed because much of the core workflow happens on a computer.

title: Postal service workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, such as delivering parcels and navigating outdoor environments, which provides a natural barrier to AI. However, AI and advanced automation are significantly impacting the sorting and processing components of the job, while the shift toward digital communication reduces overall mail volume and labor demand.
new_rationale: Postal service work is centered on physical mail handling, delivery routes, customer service, and operating equipment, which limits direct AI substitution. However, AI and automation can significantly reduce labor in mail sorting, address recognition, route planning, and some counter-service tasks, so the occupation faces moderate indirect exposure even though the core work is not fully digital.

title: Postsecondary education administrators
old_rationale: This occupation is heavily centered on digital information processing, including reviewing applications, analyzing student data, managing budgets, and maintaining academic records—all tasks where AI can significantly increase productivity or automate routine workflows. While roles like deans and student affairs officers require high-level human judgment and interpersonal relationship management, the underlying administrative and analytical work is highly susceptible to AI integration and restructuring.
new_rationale: This is largely knowledge and administrative work—reviewing applications, analyzing student data, managing records, preparing communications, scheduling, and handling routine advising—all areas where AI can significantly assist or partially automate tasks. But the role also depends on human judgment, institutional accountability, sensitive student and faculty interactions, and on-campus coordination, which limits full replacement.

title: Postsecondary teachers
old_rationale: Postsecondary teaching is primarily knowledge work involving research, writing, and curriculum design—all areas where AI is highly capable. While the role requires significant human interaction and mentorship, AI can automate grading, lesson planning, and literature reviews, potentially increasing individual productivity to the point of restructuring faculty needs.
new_rationale: Postsecondary teaching includes a large share of digital knowledge work—creating course materials, grading, writing, research, and communication—where AI can already boost productivity substantially. But the occupation still depends heavily on live instruction, mentoring, classroom presence, and subject-matter judgment, so AI is more likely to augment and restructure faculty work than fully replace it.

title: Power plant operators, distributors, and dispatchers
old_rationale: The core tasks of monitoring data, adjusting power flows, and responding to system abnormalities are increasingly digital and data-driven, making them highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and smart-grid optimization. While the high-stakes nature of power infrastructure and the need for physical equipment inspections provide a buffer, the BLS already projects a 10% decline in employment due to technological advances and automated control rooms.
new_rationale: AI and automation can meaningfully reduce headcount in this occupation by improving monitoring, control-room decision support, and automating some dispatch and rerouting tasks; the BLS already notes smart-grid technology and modernized control rooms as reducing employment. But the work remains tied to safety-critical physical infrastructure, on-site operations, emergency response, and licensed human oversight, which limits full automation of the core job.

title: Preschool and childcare center directors
old_rationale: This role is a hybrid of administrative knowledge work and high-stakes physical presence. While AI can significantly automate digital tasks like budgeting, scheduling, and curriculum planning, the core of the job involves physical facility oversight, staff supervision, and building trust-based relationships with parents and children that cannot be replicated by AI.
new_rationale: AI can assist with substantial administrative parts of the job—budgeting, scheduling, drafting communications, recordkeeping, and some program planning—making directors more efficient. But the role’s core still depends heavily on onsite leadership, supervising staff, interacting with parents and children, and ensuring facility and regulatory compliance in a physical setting.

title: Preschool teachers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical care, emotional support, and real-time social interaction with children under age five, which AI cannot replicate. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like lesson planning, curriculum design, and progress reporting, the primary job requires physical presence and human empathy to manage safety and developmental needs.
new_rationale: AI can help with peripheral knowledge-work tasks like lesson planning, activity ideas, documentation, and parent communications. But the core of the job is in-person care, supervision, play-based teaching, and emotional interaction with very young children, which strongly limits automation and large labor reduction.

title: Private detectives and investigators
old_rationale: The occupation is a hybrid of digital research and physical field work. AI significantly enhances the digital aspects—such as background checks, social media analysis, and report writing—making investigators much more productive, but the core requirements of physical surveillance, in-person interviews, and undercover store work provide a substantial barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: This job combines digital information work—searching records, analyzing evidence, and writing reports—with physical surveillance, fieldwork, and in-person interviewing. AI can significantly boost the research, pattern-finding, and documentation parts of investigations, but the core work still often depends on lawful real-world observation, human judgment, and physical presence.

title: Probation officers and correctional treatment specialists
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of high-exposure digital tasks (writing case reports, risk assessment, and data-driven monitoring) and low-exposure physical/interpersonal tasks (home visits, drug testing, and building trust with hostile clients). While AI can significantly automate documentation and predictive risk modeling, the legal requirement for human testimony and the physical necessity of community supervision provide a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: This job combines significant paperwork, reporting, and risk assessment—areas where AI can help with documentation, case summaries, and decision support—with core duties that require in-person supervision, interviews, home visits, court participation, and relationship management. AI will likely raise productivity on the administrative side, but the occupation’s legal accountability, fieldwork, and human judgment needs limit full automation.

title: Producers and directors
old_rationale: Producers and directors are highly exposed because their core work products—scripts, visual aesthetics, and edited video—are increasingly being generated or enhanced by AI tools. While the role requires significant human leadership and physical presence on set, the rapid advancement of generative AI in pre-production (scripting/storyboarding) and post-production (editing/VFX) allows a single director to do the work of a much larger team, potentially consolidating the industry.
new_rationale: Producers and directors are significantly exposed because many core tasks—developing concepts, evaluating scripts, planning productions, overseeing editing/postproduction, and promotion—are information-rich and increasingly supported by AI tools. But the job also depends heavily on in-person leadership, creative taste, fundraising, and directing actors and crews in physical production environments, which limits full automation.

title: Project management specialists
old_rationale: Project management is a digital-heavy knowledge occupation involving data analysis, scheduling, and documentation, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation and optimization. While AI can significantly increase productivity in planning and monitoring, the role's heavy reliance on human-centric skills like stakeholder negotiation, conflict resolution, and team leadership provides a protective barrier against full automation.
new_rationale: Project management specialists do predominantly digital knowledge work—planning, scheduling, budgeting, tracking milestones, producing documents, and coordinating information—all areas where AI can substantially assist or automate parts of the workflow. But the role also depends heavily on human judgment, stakeholder management, negotiation, and accountability across teams, which makes full replacement harder even as AI boosts each worker’s productivity.

title: Property appraisers and assessors
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves analyzing data, comparing market trends, and generating reports, all of which are highly susceptible to Automated Valuation Models (AVMs) and AI-driven data analysis. While physical site inspections and the need to defend assessments in public hearings provide a buffer, the shift toward 'desktop appraisals' and AI-assisted mass assessments significantly reduces the human labor required per valuation.
new_rationale: This job combines significant computer-based analysis—reviewing records, running comparables, maintaining databases, and writing valuation reports—with physical site visits and local judgment about property condition and context. AI and automated valuation models can substantially speed the analytical and documentation side, especially for assessors using mass appraisal systems, but in-person inspection, defending assessments, and nuanced market judgment keep exposure below fully digital occupations.

title: Property, real estate, and community association managers
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of digital knowledge work and physical site management. While AI can automate significant portions of the job—such as drafting leases, financial reporting, budgeting, and coordinating maintenance requests—the role still requires physical inspections of facilities and high-stakes interpersonal conflict resolution. The exposure is high because the administrative and analytical 'office' tasks that comprise a large part of the day are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains.
new_rationale: This job mixes sizable administrative and digital work—leasing communications, recordkeeping, budgeting, reporting, marketing vacancies, and maintenance coordination—that AI can increasingly automate or streamline. But much of the core role still depends on physical presence, property inspections, showing units, contractor oversight, and handling tenant or homeowner disputes, which limits full automation.

title: Psychiatric technicians and aides
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical tasks such as bathing, feeding, and physically restraining patients, which AI cannot perform. While AI can assist with monitoring patient behavior and automating documentation or reporting, the high requirement for real-time human presence, physical stamina, and interpersonal compassion creates a significant barrier to automation.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is in-person patient care: monitoring behavior, helping with daily activities, escorting patients, administering treatments, and sometimes physically restraining people. AI may assist with documentation, intake, risk flagging, or care planning, but it does not replace the hands-on, real-time human presence and judgment central to the role.

title: Psychologists
old_rationale: Psychology is a knowledge-based profession where core tasks like analyzing data, writing reports, and researching are highly susceptible to AI enhancement. However, the critical requirements for human empathy, ethical judgment, and real-time interpersonal observation in clinical and school settings create a significant barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: Psychologists have meaningful exposure because AI can assist with testing, documentation, report writing, research, and some aspects of assessment or treatment planning. But much of the occupation depends on trust, nuanced observation, ethical judgment, licensure, and direct human interaction with clients, students, families, or organizations, which limits full automation of the core role.
