title: Public relations and fundraising managers
old_rationale: The core digital tasks of this role—writing press releases, drafting speeches, analyzing social media trends, and creating fundraising strategies—are highly susceptible to AI automation and enhancement. However, the role's heavy reliance on high-stakes interpersonal relationships, such as face-to-face donor meetings and managing media crises, provides a significant human-centric buffer that prevents a higher score.
new_rationale: This job has high AI exposure because much of the work is digital information and communication work: drafting press releases, speeches, campaigns, grant materials, audience analysis, and monitoring media trends. However, it is not near-maximum exposure because core value still depends on human judgment, crisis management, leadership, and relationship-building with executives, media, and especially donors in face-to-face settings.

title: Public relations specialists
old_rationale: The core tasks of this occupation—writing press releases, drafting speeches, creating social media content, and monitoring public sentiment—are digital-first and align perfectly with the capabilities of Large Language Models. While high-level strategy and real-time interpersonal crisis management provide some protection, the vast majority of the daily work product can be generated or significantly optimized by AI, leading to high exposure and potential workforce consolidation.
new_rationale: This job is heavily centered on digital communication tasks—writing press releases, drafting speeches, monitoring social media, shaping messaging, and responding to media inquiries—all areas where AI can already generate, summarize, analyze, and optimize output. Human judgment, relationship-building with clients and media, and crisis communication still matter, but AI is likely to significantly increase productivity and reduce the amount of routine content and monitoring work needing human labor.

title: Public safety telecommunicators
old_rationale: The core tasks of this role—receiving digital inputs (voice, text, video), processing information according to protocols, and dispatching resources—are highly susceptible to AI automation and augmentation. While the high-stakes nature and need for human empathy provide a temporary buffer, AI's ability to transcribe, translate, and prioritize emergency calls in real-time will significantly restructure the occupation and increase individual worker productivity.
new_rationale: This job is heavily information-processing and computer-mediated, so AI can assist with call intake, transcription, protocol guidance, translation, routing, recordkeeping, and some triage. But the core work is real-time, high-stakes emergency judgment and emotionally sensitive communication with callers and responders, where reliability, accountability, and human oversight remain critical.

title: Purchasing managers, buyers, and purchasing agents
old_rationale: The core tasks of analyzing price proposals, monitoring inventory, and reviewing financial reports are highly susceptible to AI automation and optimization. While high-stakes negotiations and physical site visits provide a buffer, the majority of the work is digital information processing that can be significantly streamlined by AI, leading to higher productivity per worker and a restructuring of the procurement process.
new_rationale: This is largely office-based knowledge work involving analyzing proposals, comparing suppliers, reviewing contracts, maintaining records, and communicating with vendors—all areas where AI can substantially boost productivity. But the job also depends on negotiation, supplier relationships, strategic judgment, and sometimes site visits or product evaluation, which keep it from the very highest exposure tier.

title: Quality control inspectors
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical inspection and data analysis. While AI-powered computer vision systems are rapidly automating the visual detection of defects, the role still requires significant physical presence to handle materials, operate manual gauges, and perform mechanical tests that robots cannot yet fully replicate in diverse manufacturing environments.
new_rationale: This job’s core work happens in the physical world: inspecting materials, measuring parts, monitoring production lines, and handling products on-site. AI and machine vision can automate parts of defect detection, reporting, and process monitoring, but human inspectors still need physical presence, judgment, and hands-on verification in manufacturing environments.

title: Radiation therapists
old_rationale: The role requires a significant physical presence to position patients, operate heavy machinery, and provide empathetic interpersonal care during serious illness. While AI will heavily automate the digital aspects of the job—such as tumor localization, treatment planning, and dosage calculations—the physical and clinical safety requirements create a substantial barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: Radiation therapists’ core work requires physical presence: positioning patients, operating radiation equipment, monitoring reactions, and maintaining strict safety procedures in clinical settings. AI may increasingly assist with treatment planning, imaging, documentation, and workflow efficiency, but it is unlikely to replace the hands-on patient care and in-person machine operation that define the job.

title: Radiologic and MRI technologists
old_rationale: While AI is revolutionizing image analysis and quality control, the core of this role requires physical presence to position patients, administer contrast agents, and operate heavy machinery in a clinical setting. AI will significantly enhance productivity and image evaluation, but the physical and interpersonal requirements of patient care provide a substantial barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: AI will significantly affect parts of this job such as image quality optimization, scan protocol guidance, workflow, documentation, and preliminary image assessment. But the core work still requires in-person patient positioning, equipment operation, safety procedures, contrast administration, and hands-on interaction in clinical settings, which creates a substantial barrier to full automation.

title: Railroad workers
old_rationale: The occupation involves a significant amount of physical labor, mechanical adjustment, and real-world safety monitoring that AI cannot perform. However, core functions like train operation, scheduling, and signal management are increasingly susceptible to automation and AI-driven efficiency systems, which the BLS notes is already limiting employment growth.
new_rationale: Railroad work is rooted in physical operations, on-site safety monitoring, equipment handling, and real-time coordination in trains and rail yards, which creates a strong barrier to full AI replacement. However, dispatching, scheduling, diagnostics, signaling, and some train operation tasks are already being automated, so AI will likely reduce staffing needs and reshape parts of the occupation over time.

title: Real estate brokers and sales agents
old_rationale: Real estate agents perform a mix of digital knowledge work and physical/interpersonal tasks. AI can automate significant portions of their digital workload, such as market analysis, property descriptions, lead generation, and contract preparation, which increases individual productivity and may reduce the total number of agents needed. However, the core of the job still requires physical property showings, high-stakes human negotiation, and local networking, which provide a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: AI can significantly assist with the occupation’s information-heavy tasks: generating listings and ads, analyzing comparable properties, drafting documents, answering client questions, and automating lead generation and follow-up. But the core of the job still depends heavily on in-person property showings, local relationship-building, trust, and live negotiation, which limits full automation even as AI may reduce the number of agents needed per transaction.

title: Receptionists
old_rationale: Many core tasks such as scheduling, answering phones, and data entry are highly susceptible to automation via AI voice agents and chatbots. While the role retains a physical component for greeting and security, the BLS already projects zero growth due to automation, and the digital nature of the administrative workload makes it highly exposed to further restructuring.
new_rationale: Receptionist work includes many routine information-processing tasks that AI and software can increasingly handle: answering common questions, routing calls, scheduling appointments, data entry, and managing correspondence. However, the job still often requires physical presence at a front desk, greeting and checking in visitors, and handling in-person interactions and security-related tasks, which keeps exposure below the highest levels.

title: Recreation workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical presence, manual instruction, and real-time interpersonal interaction in unpredictable environments like camps and community centers. While AI can assist with peripheral administrative tasks like scheduling, program planning, and creating newsletters, it cannot replace the physical supervision, safety monitoring, and emotional leadership required to manage group activities.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is in-person: leading activities, supervising groups, adapting to participants’ needs, ensuring safety, and providing basic first aid. AI may help with peripheral tasks like scheduling, paperwork, newsletters, and activity planning, but it does not replace the physical presence, real-time interaction, and hands-on supervision central to the role.

title: Recreational therapists
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical presence, manual assistance, and real-time interpersonal interaction through activities like adaptive sports, dance, and community outings. While AI can significantly assist with digital tasks like patient assessment, treatment planning, and progress documentation, it cannot replace the physical and emotional labor required to lead therapeutic interventions in person.
new_rationale: Recreational therapists do meaningful documentation, assessment, and treatment-planning work that AI can assist with, especially around records review, progress notes, and program ideas. But the core of the job is highly in-person and physical: observing patients directly, building trust, leading therapeutic activities, and adapting in real time to patients’ emotional and physical needs.

title: Registered nurses
old_rationale: Registered nursing is a hybrid role that combines significant physical labor, such as administering treatments and moving patients, with complex interpersonal care and emotional support. While AI will heavily impact the knowledge-work components—such as patient monitoring, diagnostic analysis, and care planning—the core requirement for physical presence and human empathy creates a strong barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: Registered nurses have meaningful exposure because AI can assist with documentation, patient monitoring, care planning, diagnostic support, and analyzing test results, making the information-heavy parts of the job more efficient. But the core occupation still depends heavily on bedside physical care, real-time judgment in clinical settings, and human interaction with patients and families, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Rehabilitation counselors
old_rationale: Rehabilitation counseling involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, such as developing rehabilitation plans, maintaining records, and researching resources, which are highly susceptible to AI assistance. However, the core of the profession relies on high-stakes interpersonal interaction, empathy, and physical advocacy for clients with disabilities, creating a natural barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: Rehabilitation counselors combine significant documentation, assessment, care planning, and resource-matching work that AI can increasingly assist with. But the core of the occupation depends on trust, empathy, counseling, advocacy, and coordination with clients and families in sensitive real-world situations, which limits full automation.

title: Respiratory therapists
old_rationale: Respiratory therapy involves a significant amount of physical, hands-on patient care, such as performing chest physiotherapy, intubating patients, and maintaining equipment in real-time clinical environments. While AI will heavily assist in diagnostic analysis, ventilator setting optimization, and patient monitoring, the core of the job requires physical presence and interpersonal interaction that cannot be digitized.
new_rationale: Respiratory therapists do hands-on clinical work: examining patients, administering treatments, operating ventilators, collecting samples, and responding in person in hospitals or homes. AI may help with monitoring, documentation, and treatment decision support, but the core job depends on physical presence, patient interaction, and real-time clinical judgment.

title: Retail sales workers
old_rationale: Retail sales involve a mix of physical tasks (stocking shelves, demonstrating products) and interpersonal interaction that provides a barrier to full automation. However, AI is increasingly handling the information-heavy aspects of the job, such as personalized product recommendations, answering technical questions via kiosks/mobile apps, and automating inventory management and checkout processes, which reduces the total human labor required per store.
new_rationale: Retail sales work depends heavily on physical presence in stores, face-to-face customer service, stocking, and handling merchandise, which limits direct automation by AI. However, AI and related technologies can reduce labor needs indirectly through better online shopping, automated checkout, inventory systems, and product recommendation tools, so the occupation faces meaningful but not extreme exposure.

title: Roofers
old_rationale: Roofing is a highly physical occupation performed in unpredictable outdoor environments that require manual dexterity, balance, and heavy lifting. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like drone-based inspections or material estimation, the core work of installing and repairing shingles and membranes remains entirely dependent on human labor.
new_rationale: Roofing is overwhelmingly physical, hands-on outdoor work that requires climbing, lifting, measuring, cutting, and installing materials on real buildings. AI may help a little with estimates, scheduling, or paperwork, but it does not meaningfully automate the core task of repairing and installing roofs.

title: Sales engineers
old_rationale: Sales engineers perform high-level knowledge work that is increasingly digital, including technical presentations, product customization, and troubleshooting. While AI can automate technical documentation, demo generation, and data-driven customer insights, the role's heavy reliance on complex interpersonal relationships, negotiation, and trust-building provides a significant buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Sales engineers do substantial knowledge work on a computer—preparing presentations, analyzing customer requirements, tailoring solutions, and providing technical support—so AI can meaningfully automate or accelerate large parts of the job. But core value still depends heavily on relationship-building, persuasion, negotiation, and customer-facing interactions, often with travel and live technical discussions, which keeps exposure below the highest levels.

title: Sales managers
old_rationale: Sales managers perform high-level knowledge work including data analysis, sales forecasting, and strategic planning, all of which are highly susceptible to AI augmentation. While the role requires significant interpersonal leadership and relationship management, much of the core digital workflow—such as monitoring CRM data, generating reports, and developing training materials—is being rapidly transformed by generative and analytical AI.
new_rationale: Sales managers do substantial digital knowledge work—analyzing sales data, forecasting, budgeting, setting goals, and coordinating strategy—where AI can significantly boost productivity and automate parts of planning and reporting. But the job also depends heavily on leadership, hiring, coaching, negotiation, relationship management, and handling escalated customer issues, which keep human judgment and interpersonal presence central.

title: School and career counselors and advisors
old_rationale: This occupation is a mix of high-touch interpersonal work and digital information processing. AI can significantly automate the 'hard' data tasks like analyzing student performance trends, matching aptitudes to career paths, and drafting résumés, but the core functions of emotional support, crisis intervention (reporting abuse), and navigating complex human relationships in a school setting provide a strong buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: AI can assist with assessments, recordkeeping, résumé and application guidance, labor-market information, and identifying at-risk students from data, so the information-processing parts of the job are meaningfully exposed. But the core work still depends heavily on trust, empathy, sensitive judgment, and live interaction with students, families, and staff in school or counseling settings, which limits full automation.

title: Secretaries and administrative assistants
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation—scheduling, drafting correspondence, managing databases, and transcribing—are fundamentally digital and align perfectly with the capabilities of LLMs and AI agents. While some roles require physical presence for visitor greeting or office management, the vast majority of tasks are routine information processing that can be automated or significantly streamlined, leading to major restructuring and reduced headcount per organization.
new_rationale: This occupation is heavily exposed because much of the core work is routine digital information processing: scheduling, document preparation, email and phone handling, database maintenance, transcription, and basic bookkeeping. AI can automate or greatly accelerate many of these tasks, and even where full replacement is limited by coordination or interpersonal needs, each worker can support more people, reducing headcount demand.

title: Securities, commodities, and financial services sales agents
old_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital and information-based, involving data analysis, market monitoring, and financial modeling—all areas where AI is rapidly achieving parity with human experts. While high-level relationship management and complex negotiation (like M&A) provide some insulation, the core tasks of identifying investment opportunities, executing trades, and providing routine financial advice are highly susceptible to automation and significant productivity gains that could reduce the need for human agents.
new_rationale: This job is heavily exposed because much of its core work is digital information processing: analyzing markets, evaluating deals, monitoring securities, and executing trades, all areas where AI and automation are advancing quickly. However, the occupation still depends substantially on client relationships, persuasion, trust, regulatory accountability, and high-stakes judgment, which limits full automation even as AI reduces demand for some roles like routine traders and brokers.

title: Security guards and gambling surveillance officers
old_rationale: The occupation is a hybrid of physical presence and digital monitoring. While AI-powered computer vision is rapidly automating the 'surveillance' and 'monitoring' aspects of the job, the core requirements of physical patrolling, detaining suspects, and responding to real-world emergencies provide a significant barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: This work is centered on physical presence, patrolling property, controlling access, and responding to incidents in real time, which creates a meaningful barrier to full AI replacement. However, AI-enabled cameras, remote monitoring, and automated alerting can reduce demand for some monitoring and surveillance tasks, especially for gambling surveillance officers and stationary guard roles.

title: Semiconductor processing technicians
old_rationale: The role involves a significant physical component, requiring presence in cleanrooms to handle wafers, maintain equipment, and perform manual inspections. However, AI and advanced automation are increasingly capable of monitoring process data, optimizing machine parameters, and performing high-precision quality control, which automates the information-processing and monitoring aspects of the job.
new_rationale: This job is centered on physical, on-site manufacturing work in cleanrooms: operating equipment, handling wafers, adjusting machinery, and inspecting output. AI can meaningfully improve process monitoring, defect detection, documentation, and optimization, but the core role still depends on human presence around specialized equipment and real-world production systems.

title: Set and exhibit designers
old_rationale: The core creative process—including script analysis, conceptual sketching, and CAD modeling—is increasingly susceptible to generative AI and automated design tools that can rapidly produce 3D environments and visual concepts. While the role requires physical-world considerations like material budgets and on-site installation oversight, the shift toward digital production (such as virtual sets and LED volumes) and AI-assisted drafting significantly increases productivity and reduces the total human labor required per project.
new_rationale: Set and exhibit designers do substantial digital knowledge work—concept development, visual design, CAD drafting, and budget/planning—where AI can already assist and may significantly boost productivity. But the job also depends heavily on site-specific physical constraints, collaboration with directors/curators, and overseeing real-world installation, which limits full automation.

title: Sheet metal workers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical labor, manual dexterity, and on-site installation in unpredictable construction environments, which are highly resistant to AI. While AI and computer-controlled machinery (CADD, lasers, and automated presses) are increasingly used in the fabrication phase, the majority of the work—including installation, maintenance, and complex physical maneuvering—cannot be performed by digital tools.
new_rationale: Sheet metal work is dominated by physical fabrication, installation, and repair in shops and construction sites, requiring manual skill, strength, and on-site presence. AI may modestly affect planning, CADD use, and automated fabrication equipment, but it does not directly replace the core hands-on tasks for most workers.

title: Skincare specialists
old_rationale: The core of the job involves physical, hands-on treatments like facials, massages, and hair removal that require real-time human presence and manual dexterity. While AI can assist with peripheral tasks like skin analysis, product recommendations, and business management, it cannot replace the tactile and interpersonal nature of the primary service.
new_rationale: Skincare specialists do hands-on, in-person physical work: evaluating skin, performing treatments, hair removal, massages, and maintaining sanitary equipment and spaces. AI may help with peripheral tasks like scheduling, marketing, product recommendations, or basic skin-analysis tools, but it does not replace the core client-facing physical services.

title: Small engine mechanics
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical manipulation of mechanical components, manual dexterity, and real-world troubleshooting in unpredictable environments. While AI can assist with diagnostic software and technical documentation, it cannot perform the physical labor of disassembling engines, replacing parts, or conducting hands-on repairs.
new_rationale: Small engine mechanics do predominantly hands-on physical repair work that requires in-person inspection, disassembly, tool use, and testing of real equipment in shops, marinas, or onsite. AI can help with diagnostics, service documentation, and accessing repair information, but it is unlikely to automate the core manual troubleshooting and repair tasks, so overall exposure is low.

title: Social and community service managers
old_rationale: This role involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, such as grant writing, data analysis, and budget management, which are highly susceptible to AI augmentation. However, the core responsibilities of community stakeholder engagement, staff supervision, and navigating complex human crises provide a buffer that requires physical presence and high-level emotional intelligence.
new_rationale: This job includes substantial knowledge-work that AI can assist with—data analysis, reporting, proposal writing, budgeting, planning, and administrative oversight. But core effectiveness still depends heavily on human judgment, staff supervision, community relationships, fundraising, and in-person coordination, so AI is more likely to augment than fully replace the role.

title: Social and human service assistants
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of digital administrative work and high-touch interpersonal service. AI can significantly automate the 'knowledge work' components, such as researching community resources, assisting with complex benefit applications, and coordinating service schedules. However, the core of the job requires physical presence in homes or clinics and deep emotional intelligence to support clients in crisis, which provides a substantial buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: This job mixes paperwork, benefits research, service coordination, and follow-up—tasks AI can increasingly assist with—with substantial in-person, relationship-based work with vulnerable clients. Because the core role depends on trust, empathy, judgment, and navigating local real-world services, AI is more likely to augment productivity than fully replace the occupation.

title: Social workers
old_rationale: Social work involves a high degree of interpersonal interaction, empathy, and crisis management in physical environments, which provides a strong barrier against full automation. However, AI will significantly impact the profession by automating administrative tasks like case documentation, assisting in resource research, and providing diagnostic support tools for clinical practitioners.
new_rationale: Social workers have meaningful exposure because AI can help with documentation, case management, resource referral, treatment planning support, and other administrative information-processing tasks. But the core of the job relies on in-person trust, empathy, crisis response, advocacy, and licensed clinical judgment in complex human situations, which creates a substantial barrier to full automation.

title: Sociologists
old_rationale: Sociology is a knowledge-intensive field where core tasks like data analysis, literature reviews, and report writing are highly susceptible to AI enhancement. While qualitative field research and interpersonal interviews provide a buffer, the shift toward digital data collection and AI-driven statistical modeling significantly increases worker productivity and reshapes the research process.
new_rationale: Sociologists do predominantly digital knowledge work: designing studies, analyzing data, synthesizing findings, and writing reports and presentations, all of which are areas where AI is advancing quickly. Human judgment remains important for research design, interpreting social context, interviewing people, and policy relevance, but AI can substantially increase productivity and reshape much of the occupation.

title: Software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers
old_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally digital, with core tasks like coding, debugging, and test automation being primary use cases for Large Language Models. While high-level system architecture and complex stakeholder communication provide some insulation, AI is drastically increasing individual productivity, which will likely lead to significant restructuring of entry-level roles and QA functions.
new_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: designing software, writing or specifying code, testing, documenting, and communicating about requirements are all tasks AI can increasingly perform or accelerate. Human judgment, system design, stakeholder coordination, and accountability still matter, but AI is likely to substantially boost productivity and reshape team structure, workflows, and staffing needs.

title: Solar photovoltaic installers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is highly physical, involving manual labor, climbing roofs, and using hand tools in unpredictable outdoor environments. While AI might assist with peripheral tasks like system design optimization or site measurements via computer vision, the actual installation and maintenance require human dexterity and physical presence that current and near-future AI/robotics cannot replicate.
new_rationale: This job is overwhelmingly physical and site-specific: measuring roofs, mounting panels, using tools, climbing ladders, wiring systems, and performing outdoor maintenance. AI may help with peripheral tasks like system design, planning, diagnostics, or paperwork, but the core work requires hands-on installation and real-world safety in unpredictable environments.

title: Special education teachers
old_rationale: AI will significantly streamline the digital and administrative aspects of the role, such as drafting Individualized Education Programs (IEPs), adapting lesson plans, and tracking student data. However, the core of the job requires intense physical presence, emotional intelligence, and real-time behavioral management for students with complex disabilities, which AI cannot replicate.
new_rationale: Special education teaching includes substantial knowledge work that AI can assist with, such as drafting IEPs, adapting lessons, preparing materials, tracking progress, and handling documentation. But the core job still depends heavily on in-person instruction, behavioral support, individualized judgment, and close human relationships with students, parents, and staff, which limits full automation.

title: Special effects artists and animators
old_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital, with core tasks like 3D modeling, rendering, and animation being directly disrupted by generative AI and automated rigging tools. While high-level creative direction and complex team collaboration remain human-centric, AI is rapidly automating the 'routine' aspects of CGI and visual effects, significantly increasing individual productivity and reducing the total number of workers needed for large-scale productions.
new_rationale: This job is overwhelmingly digital: creating graphics, animation, visual effects, and sometimes code on a computer, often even from home. AI is rapidly improving at image generation, style transfer, rotoscoping, motion, and other core production tasks, so it is likely to significantly reshape workflows and reduce labor needed for many routine or iterative parts, even though human creativity, taste, and collaboration still matter.

title: Speech-language pathologists
old_rationale: Speech-language pathology involves a significant amount of digital-adjacent work, such as analyzing speech patterns, documenting progress, and developing treatment plans, all of which are highly susceptible to AI enhancement. However, the core of the job requires physical presence for swallowing therapy, real-time interpersonal coaching, and high-stakes clinical judgment that limits full automation.
new_rationale: Speech-language pathologists do substantial assessment, documentation, and treatment planning that AI can assist with, and AI tools may increasingly support screening, progress notes, and therapy materials. But the core of the job depends on in-person clinical observation, hands-on swallowing treatment, and sustained human interaction with patients and families, which limits full automation.

title: Stationary engineers and boiler operators
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical maintenance, manual repairs, and hands-on troubleshooting of heavy mechanical equipment in unpredictable physical environments. While AI and automated control systems can enhance monitoring and data logging, they cannot perform the physical tasks of overhauling valves, lubricating parts, or crawling inside boilers for inspections.
new_rationale: This job is centered on operating, inspecting, repairing, and maintaining physical boiler and building systems in person, often in hazardous industrial environments. AI may help with monitoring, diagnostics, predictive maintenance, and paperwork, but the core work still depends on hands-on mechanical skill, on-site judgment, and continuous physical presence.

title: Substance abuse, behavioral disorder, and mental health counselors
old_rationale: This occupation is a mix of high-level knowledge work and intense interpersonal interaction. While AI can significantly assist with digital tasks like documentation, treatment planning, and monitoring progress, the core of the job requires human empathy, crisis intervention, and the navigation of complex emotional and physical environments that AI cannot yet replicate.
new_rationale: This job combines significant knowledge work with deeply human, trust-based interaction: counseling, crisis intervention, assessment, and relationship-building are central and hard to automate fully. AI can meaningfully assist with documentation, intake, treatment-plan drafting, psychoeducation, and some lower-acuity support, but the core therapeutic role still depends on licensed human judgment and interpersonal presence.

title: Surgical assistants and technologists
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is highly physical, requiring manual dexterity, sterile technique, and real-time presence in an operating room. While AI and robotics can assist with surgical precision or inventory management, the role remains centered on hands-on patient care and physical equipment handling that cannot be digitized.
new_rationale: This work is centered on physical, in-person assistance in operating rooms: preparing sterile fields, handling instruments, positioning patients, and directly assisting surgeons during live procedures. AI may help with peripheral tasks such as documentation, supply tracking, or robotic support, but it does not meaningfully replace the core hands-on, real-time surgical role.

title: Survey researchers
old_rationale: Survey research is a fundamentally digital knowledge occupation involving background research, survey design, statistical analysis, and report generation—all areas where LLMs and specialized AI tools excel. The BLS already projects a decline in employment due to data mining and big data efficiencies, and the rapid advancement of AI in synthesizing qualitative data and automating complex statistical workflows makes this role highly susceptible to significant restructuring and labor reduction.
new_rationale: Survey researchers do predominantly digital knowledge work: designing questionnaires, analyzing data with statistical software, troubleshooting sampling issues, and presenting findings. AI can already assist substantially with survey drafting, data analysis, visualization, and reporting, and newer data-mining tools may reduce demand for traditional survey work, though human judgment still matters for methodology, validity, and interpretation.

title: Surveying and mapping technicians
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical fieldwork and digital data processing. While AI and automation (via drones and GIS software) significantly enhance data analysis, map creation, and calculation tasks, the role still requires a physical presence to navigate rugged terrain, set markers, and operate equipment in the real world.
new_rationale: This occupation has a meaningful digital component—processing survey data, updating GIS databases, using CAD, and working with aerial imagery—where AI can assist with image analysis, map updates, calculations, and documentation. But a large share of the job still requires physical fieldwork, operating instruments, traveling to sites, and collecting measurements in real-world conditions, which limits full automation.

title: Surveyors
old_rationale: Surveying is a hybrid occupation that combines significant physical fieldwork with digital data processing. While AI and automation (via drones and GIS) are rapidly increasing productivity in the mapping and analysis phases, the core requirements of physical site visits, navigating rugged terrain, and providing legally certified human testimony in court create a substantial barrier to full automation.
new_rationale: Surveyors have meaningful exposure because parts of the job are digital and information-heavy: researching records, processing measurement data, creating maps with GIS/CAD, and preparing reports can all be increasingly assisted by AI. But the occupation still depends heavily on fieldwork, physical measurement on site, travel, and licensed legal judgment about boundaries, which limits full automation.

title: Tax examiners and collectors, and revenue agents
old_rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because its core tasks—reviewing financial documents, identifying discrepancies, and interpreting tax law—are digital information-processing tasks where AI excels. While revenue agents handling complex corporate audits and collectors performing field investigations require human judgment and interpersonal skills, a significant portion of the routine examination and data verification work is already being automated or enhanced by sophisticated algorithms.
new_rationale: This job is heavily centered on digital information processing: reviewing tax returns, analyzing financial records, applying tax rules, documenting cases, and communicating findings. AI can automate or greatly accelerate much of this analytical and paperwork-heavy work, though some exposure is capped by the need for legal judgment, taxpayer interaction, investigations, and occasional field audits.

title: Taxi drivers, shuttle drivers, and chauffeurs
old_rationale: While the core task of driving is highly susceptible to automation through autonomous vehicle technology (AI), the job currently requires significant physical presence for tasks like loading luggage, assisting passengers with disabilities, and performing vehicle maintenance. The transition to fully driverless fleets faces regulatory and technical hurdles in complex environments, but the long-term trajectory for this digital-adjacent physical task is one of high potential displacement.
new_rationale: This job requires continuous physical presence in a vehicle, real-world navigation, passenger assistance, and in-person customer service, which keeps exposure below the highest levels. But the core task of driving is a major target for AI and autonomy, so over time self-driving systems and AI dispatching could substantially reshape demand for human drivers.

title: Teacher assistants
old_rationale: Teacher assistants have moderate exposure because AI can significantly automate their administrative and instructional support tasks, such as grading, lesson preparation, and providing personalized tutoring through AI agents. However, a large portion of the role involves physical supervision, behavioral management, and assisting students with special needs or personal hygiene, which require a physical human presence that AI cannot replace.
new_rationale: Teacher assistants’ core work is in-person supervision, behavior support, individualized help, and in many cases physical assistance with children or students with disabilities, which limits direct automation. AI can still meaningfully affect lesson reinforcement, grading support, recordkeeping, and creation of educational materials, but it is more likely to augment than replace most day-to-day duties.

title: Technical writers
old_rationale: Technical writing is a fully digital occupation centered on synthesizing complex information into clear documentation, a core strength of Large Language Models. AI can already draft manuals, summarize technical specs, and generate code documentation, significantly increasing individual productivity and reducing the total number of human writers needed for routine documentation tasks.
new_rationale: Technical writing is almost entirely digital work: drafting, revising, summarizing, standardizing, and formatting documentation are all tasks where AI is improving rapidly and can already provide major assistance. Human input still matters for understanding complex products, coordinating with engineers, and ensuring accuracy and usability, but AI is likely to substantially increase productivity and reduce labor demand per unit of output.

title: Telecommunications technicians
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is physical labor, including climbing towers, installing hardware, and manual troubleshooting in unpredictable environments, which provides a strong barrier against AI automation. While AI can assist with remote diagnostics, network optimization, and predictive maintenance, the physical necessity of splicing cables and mounting equipment ensures the role remains primarily human-driven.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical: installing, testing, repairing, climbing towers, running cable, and working in homes, businesses, or central offices. AI may help with diagnostics, remote monitoring alerts, documentation, and troubleshooting guidance, but it does not replace the hands-on fieldwork, safety demands, and on-site customer interaction that define the occupation.

title: Tellers
old_rationale: Tellers face high exposure because their core tasks—processing routine financial transactions and verifying information—are digital in nature and highly susceptible to automation through AI and advanced self-service technology. While the physical handling of cash provides a slight buffer, the rapid shift toward mobile banking, AI-driven fraud detection, and interactive video kiosks is significantly reducing the need for human intervention in these roles.
new_rationale: Tellers’ work includes highly routine, rules-based transaction processing and customer service tasks that are increasingly being automated through ATMs, kiosks, online banking, fraud detection, and remote service tools. The job still requires physical presence to handle cash and interact with customers, but AI and related automation can significantly reduce headcount by shifting routine transactions away from human tellers and making each remaining teller more productive.

title: Top executives
old_rationale: Top executives perform high-level knowledge work including financial analysis, strategic planning, and contract review, all of which are highly susceptible to AI augmentation. However, the core of the role involves human-centric responsibilities like leadership, high-stakes negotiation, and accountability to boards or the public, which provide a significant buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: Top executives do substantial digital knowledge work—analyzing reports, reviewing budgets, setting strategy, communicating, and coordinating decisions—so AI can meaningfully augment much of their information processing and planning. But the core of the job also depends on leadership, accountability, negotiation, trust, and high-stakes judgment across real organizations, which limits full automation even as AI may reduce the need for some managerial layers.

title: Tour and travel guides
old_rationale: While AI can automate the digital aspects of the job—such as researching history, planning itineraries, and providing multilingual translations—the core of the role requires physical presence and real-time human interaction. Guides must physically escort groups through unpredictable environments, manage group dynamics, and provide a personal, empathetic experience that digital tools cannot fully replicate.
new_rationale: The core of this job is physical, place-based, and highly interpersonal: escorting groups, managing logistics in real time, and engaging visitors on-site. AI can help with peripheral tasks like research, itinerary planning, translation, marketing, and recordkeeping, but it is unlikely to replace the in-person presence, adaptability, and customer interaction at the center of the occupation.

title: Training and development managers
old_rationale: This role is predominantly digital and knowledge-based, involving the creation of instructional content, data-driven needs assessments, and budget management—all areas where AI is highly capable. While the role requires significant human-centric leadership and stakeholder collaboration, AI can automate the generation of training materials, personalize learning paths, and analyze program effectiveness, substantially increasing the productivity of each manager.
new_rationale: This is predominantly knowledge work done through planning, evaluating materials, analyzing training needs, budgeting, and coordinating staff—areas where AI can significantly assist or boost productivity. But the role also depends heavily on managerial judgment, cross-department collaboration, leadership, and organizational relationship-building, which makes it less exposed than fully digital execution-focused jobs.

title: Training and development specialists
old_rationale: Much of the core work, such as designing training manuals, creating online learning modules, and assessing needs through data, is digital and highly susceptible to AI automation and augmentation. While the role requires interpersonal skills for live delivery and collaboration, AI can now generate curriculum, simulate training scenarios, and personalize learning paths, significantly increasing individual productivity and potentially reducing the number of specialists needed for content creation.
new_rationale: This job has high AI exposure because much of the core work is digital knowledge work: assessing needs, creating training materials, building online modules, reviewing content, and evaluating programs. However, exposure is not at the very top because specialists also deliver training, facilitate learning, coordinate with stakeholders, and adapt to organizational culture and live audience dynamics, which still benefit from human presence and judgment.

title: Transportation, storage, and distribution managers
old_rationale: This role involves a significant amount of digital knowledge work, such as logistics planning, budgeting, and supply chain optimization, which are highly susceptible to AI-driven efficiency gains. However, the core job also requires physical presence for facility inspections, safety compliance monitoring, and high-stakes interpersonal leadership in unpredictable environments like warehouses and railyards, providing a buffer against full automation.
new_rationale: This job includes substantial knowledge work—planning logistics, analyzing operations, budgeting, compliance review, and coordinating across departments—where AI can significantly boost productivity and automate parts of scheduling, reporting, forecasting, and optimization. But the role also depends heavily on overseeing physical operations, managing staff, handling disruptions in real time, and maintaining safety and service in warehouses and transportation networks, which keeps exposure below the highest levels.

title: Travel agents
old_rationale: The core functions of a travel agent—researching destinations, comparing prices, building itineraries, and processing bookings—are entirely digital and involve information synthesis that AI can now perform instantly. While high-end human agents provide personalized judgment and crisis management, AI's ability to handle complex natural language queries and real-time logistics makes the vast majority of the occupation's routine tasks highly susceptible to automation.
new_rationale: Travel agents’ core work is largely digital information processing: researching options, comparing fares, building itineraries, communicating with clients, and making bookings on computer systems. AI can already assist heavily with trip planning, recommendations, customer communication, and routine reservation tasks, though human judgment, sales ability, and handling complex disruptions still keep the role from being a 10.

title: Tutors
old_rationale: Tutors primarily engage in knowledge transfer, explanation, and feedback—tasks where generative AI and personalized learning algorithms excel. While the interpersonal and motivational aspects of tutoring provide some protection, the core work product is digital/informational, and AI's ability to provide 24/7, low-cost, personalized instruction for standardized tests and core subjects creates significant displacement risk.
new_rationale: Tutoring combines knowledge work that AI is increasingly good at—explaining concepts, generating practice materials, giving feedback, and helping with test prep—with live human interaction and adaptation to individual students. AI will likely automate or commoditize many routine tutoring tasks and reduce demand in some segments, but human presence, motivation, rapport, and specialized support for diverse learners keep exposure below the highest levels.

title: Umpires, referees, and other sports officials
old_rationale: The occupation is a hybrid of physical presence and objective data processing. While computer vision and AI can already automate 'calls' (like strike zones or out-of-bounds) with higher accuracy than humans, the role still requires physical presence to manage player conduct, ensure safety, and handle real-time interpersonal disputes on the field.
new_rationale: This job’s core work happens in person, in real time, on the field or court, requiring physical presence, positioning, visual observation, and split-second judgment under pressure. AI may assist at the margins through video replay, training, and administrative tasks, but it is unlikely to replace the core officiating role broadly in the near term.

title: Urban and regional planners
old_rationale: Urban planners perform heavy knowledge work including data analysis, GIS mapping, and report writing, all of which are highly susceptible to AI enhancement and automation. However, the role requires significant physical site inspections and high-stakes interpersonal negotiation with government officials and the public, which provides a buffer against total automation.
new_rationale: Urban and regional planners do substantial digital knowledge work—analyzing data, evaluating site plans, using GIS, and writing reports—so AI can meaningfully accelerate core parts of the job. But the occupation also depends heavily on stakeholder meetings, public communication, political judgment, and occasional site visits, which limits full automation even as AI raises productivity.

title: Veterinarians
old_rationale: Veterinary work is a hybrid of physical procedures and complex diagnostic knowledge work. While AI will significantly enhance diagnostic accuracy through image analysis (X-rays/ultrasounds) and treatment planning, the core of the job requires physical surgery, manual examinations, and high-stakes interpersonal communication with pet owners that cannot be automated.
new_rationale: Veterinarians have meaningful AI exposure in diagnostic support, imaging interpretation, recordkeeping, treatment planning, and client communication, all of which can improve productivity. But the core of the job still requires physical examination, surgery, procedures, emergency response, and in-person judgment with animals and owners, which creates a strong barrier to full automation.

title: Veterinary assistants and laboratory animal caretakers
old_rationale: The core duties of this occupation are almost entirely physical and manual, involving the handling, feeding, and restraining of live animals in unpredictable environments. While AI might assist with peripheral administrative tasks or diagnostic data entry, it cannot replace the physical presence and manual dexterity required for animal care, sterilization, and surgical assistance.
new_rationale: This job’s core tasks are highly physical and in-person: feeding, bathing, restraining animals, cleaning cages, sterilizing equipment, and assisting with procedures. AI may help a bit with recordkeeping, scheduling, or monitoring data, but it does not meaningfully automate the hands-on animal care and real-time physical assistance that define the occupation.

title: Veterinary technologists and technicians
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves physical tasks that AI cannot perform, such as restraining animals, administering anesthesia, performing surgery prep, and providing emergency first aid. However, AI has significant exposure in the diagnostic and administrative aspects of the job, including analyzing lab results, interpreting X-rays, and managing patient records, which may increase individual productivity.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical and in-person: restraining animals, administering treatments and anesthesia, collecting samples, assisting in surgery, and providing hands-on care. AI can help with documentation, case histories, lab interpretation, imaging support, and owner communication, but it is unlikely to replace the manual, clinical, and animal-handling parts that dominate the occupation.

title: Waiters and waitresses
old_rationale: While AI and automation are increasingly handling digital tasks like order-taking and payments via kiosks or mobile apps, the core of the job remains physical and interpersonal. The physical requirements of carrying trays, clearing tables, and providing real-time human hospitality in a dynamic environment create a significant barrier to full AI replacement.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is physical and in-person: greeting diners, taking orders, carrying food, checking on tables, and handling the live restaurant experience. AI can affect peripheral and adjacent tasks through kiosks, digital ordering, and payment automation, but it does not replace most of the hands-on service and real-time customer interaction.

title: Water and wastewater treatment plant and system operators
old_rationale: The occupation is a hybrid of physical labor and digital monitoring, with AI and advanced automation already contributing to a projected 7% decline in employment. While AI can optimize chemical dosing, analyze sensor data, and manage complex system flows, the role still requires physical presence for equipment maintenance, manual sampling, and emergency response to mechanical failures.
new_rationale: This job has meaningful exposure because operators already use computerized control systems, and AI can further automate monitoring, anomaly detection, process optimization, and regulatory reporting. But the core occupation still requires on-site physical presence for inspections, sampling, maintenance, safety response, and manual operation during malfunctions or emergencies, which limits overall exposure.

title: Water transportation workers
old_rationale: The core of the work is physical and requires real-time presence in unpredictable maritime environments, involving manual tasks like equipment maintenance, cargo handling, and emergency response. While AI can assist with navigation, fuel optimization, and predictive maintenance for engineers, the high-stakes physical safety requirements and the need for human intervention in complex docking or mechanical failures limit overall exposure.
new_rationale: This work is centered on physically operating, maintaining, and supervising vessels in real-world marine environments, often requiring onboard presence, manual skill, and safety-critical judgment. AI may assist with navigation, monitoring, maintenance diagnostics, and paperwork, but it is unlikely to replace the core hands-on and in-person duties for most water transportation workers in the near term.

title: Web developers and digital designers
old_rationale: This occupation is entirely digital, with core tasks like writing code (HTML, JavaScript), designing layouts, and creating prototypes being areas where AI has demonstrated high proficiency. AI tools can now generate functional code from natural language and create sophisticated visual mockups, significantly increasing individual productivity and potentially reducing the number of developers needed for standard projects.
new_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: writing code, designing interfaces, creating mockups, testing usability, and monitoring sites are all tasks AI can increasingly perform or accelerate. Human judgment, client communication, and product taste still matter, but AI is likely to substantially reshape workflows and raise productivity enough to reduce labor needed per project.

title: Welders, cutters, solderers, and brazers
old_rationale: The core of this occupation is highly physical, requiring manual dexterity, physical stamina, and real-time adaptation to unpredictable environments like construction sites or repair shops. While AI and robotics can automate routine assembly-line welding, the specialized, non-routine tasks and the need for physical presence in varied locations provide a strong barrier against AI displacement.
new_rationale: This occupation is dominated by physical, hands-on work in factories, construction sites, and repair settings, requiring manual dexterity, spatial judgment, and real-time control of equipment around hazardous materials. AI may help with blueprint interpretation, quality inspection, or increase automation in some manufacturing settings, but it does not strongly affect the core in-person welding and cutting tasks for most workers.

title: Wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives
old_rationale: This occupation is heavily focused on information processing, communication, and data analysis, all of which are highly susceptible to AI enhancement and automation. While high-stakes B2B negotiations and physical trade shows provide a buffer, AI can now handle lead generation, personalized outreach, price optimization, and technical product explanations, significantly increasing individual productivity and reducing the total number of representatives needed.
new_rationale: AI can automate and augment meaningful parts of this job—lead generation, customer research, drafting outreach, answering routine product questions, CRM updates, reporting, and some inside-sales interactions. But much of the core value still depends on human relationship-building, persuasion, negotiation, travel, and in-person customer contact, especially for complex or technical products, so exposure is high but not near-maximum.

title: Wind turbine technicians
old_rationale: The core of this occupation involves high-stakes physical labor, including climbing 200-foot towers, rappelling, and performing manual repairs in confined spaces. While AI can assist with remote monitoring and predictive maintenance diagnostics, the physical requirement to replace components and perform manual inspections in unpredictable outdoor environments provides a strong barrier to automation.
new_rationale: This job’s core work is highly physical and site-specific: climbing turbines, inspecting equipment, troubleshooting mechanical/electrical systems, and making repairs in dangerous outdoor environments. AI may help with remote monitoring, diagnostics, scheduling, and documentation, but it does not replace the hands-on maintenance, physical dexterity, and on-site safety work that define the occupation.

title: Woodworkers
old_rationale: The core of the job involves physical manipulation of materials, manual dexterity, and real-time sensory monitoring in a workshop environment, which are resistant to AI. While AI can optimize digital design (CAD) and CNC programming, the physical assembly, finishing, and machine maintenance require a human presence that limits overall exposure.
new_rationale: Woodworking is centered on physical machine operation, material handling, assembly, finishing, and in-person safety awareness, which limits direct AI substitution. AI may help with design interpretation, CNC programming, quality checks, and productivity in automated shops, but the core work remains hands-on and tied to physical equipment and materials.

title: Writers and authors
old_rationale: The core work product of writers and authors is entirely digital and text-based, which aligns perfectly with the primary capabilities of Large Language Models. AI can now generate drafts, conduct research, and perform editing tasks across various formats like blogs, copy, and scripts, significantly increasing productivity while simultaneously threatening to automate many entry-level and routine writing roles.
new_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital: researching, drafting, revising, and producing text can largely be done from a computer anywhere. AI is rapidly improving at core writing tasks such as generating copy, summarizing research, adapting tone, and iterating drafts, so it is likely to both automate some work directly and sharply increase output per writer, even though top-tier creativity, original reporting, and client/audience judgment still preserve some human role.

title: Zoologists and wildlife biologists
old_rationale: This occupation is a hybrid of physical fieldwork and digital knowledge work. AI significantly impacts the data-heavy aspects of the job, such as analyzing GIS data, processing camera trap imagery, and modeling population dynamics, but it cannot replace the physical requirements of collecting biological specimens, handling wild animals, or navigating remote outdoor environments.
new_rationale: AI can meaningfully assist with the occupation’s digital tasks—data analysis, modeling, GIS, literature review, and drafting reports or research papers. But a large share of the job still depends on fieldwork, specimen collection, observing animals in natural habitats, and context-specific scientific judgment in the physical world, which limits full automation.
